interviewee_bio: Dan launched Maveron , in 1998, with Howard Schultz, former CEO and Executive Chairman of Starbucks Coffee Company.
video_title: Dan Levitan (Maveron) at Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: Welcome to the Star of Grime. Before we get going, can I just have a Sofia Vergara story? I don't have one. I have one. Okay, yeah. Because you just mentioned her. So, I was at a friend's 60th birthday party in Greece in July. And we're out, has anyone been to Mykonos? Okay, so you know how crazy Mykonos is. So, it's about one or two in the morning, and we're at this nightclub with a group of people. And my wife comes over to me and says, do you know who Sofia Vergara is? And of course you don't. I don't know who Sofia Vergara is. And she explains to me that there was a guy who was there at the nightclub who she was talking to. Because I saw her talking to this guy. And they were talking for about seven or eight minutes, and then she comes over to me. Well, as it turns out, it's a guy who has a company, and he had sent Mavron a business plan. And so, here we are. It's 2 a.m. in Mykonos, in the connected world we live in. And he says to her, I think that's Dan Levitan over there. And she's like, I've never had my husband be so popular. What's going on here? And she figures if she gets friendly with this guy, then she'll get the intro to Sofia Vergara. So, if you go to my wife's Facebook page, Stacy Levitan, you'll see a big picture of her with Sofia Vergara. And the quid pro quo was, she introduced me to this entrepreneur, who's a guy named Nick Loeb, who was actually highlighted in the New York Times over the weekend for a business called Onion Crunch. And anyhow, that's my Sofia Vergara story. It fell flat a little, but I thought it was a good idea. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Exactly. Well, we normally don't get to, you know, we say those things, but we don't actually have stories about the people. So, Dan, again, welcome. Thanks for coming. I know you've got a busy schedule. I know it's a busy week for you. So, that's great. Love it. Sounds like it's a busy week for the Seattle startup community. Yeah, it is. Very busy week. I'm hoping you guys leave here with some sense of, this was worth your time. So, go ahead. I'm sure they will. So, you grew up in New York. I did. Do you miss it? I miss my family and friends. And what's really happened in New York, which I think is interesting, is after Wall Street kind of imploded in 2007, you're starting to see some of those people do startups. So, do I miss it? I love being out here. But I think that seems to be, in America today, the fastest growing kind of consumer area. Yeah, New York. Yeah, yeah. I agree. And did you come to Seattle because of Maveron to start Maveron with Howard? I didn't. The line I like to use is, young men have always gone west to fulfill their dreams in America. And I did that, too. Yeah, yeah. And what would you, looking back on that decision, was there anything you would change about that decision? Would you have moved earlier or later? Would you have not moved? Well, because that was 15 years ago? Yeah. No, it was the best decision of my life. But I think the context of it was interesting. I remember seeing Howard in 1993, after Starbucks had gone public. He was so happy and he was so in synchronicity with what he wanted to do. And I was, I'm ashamed to say, an investment banker. And I was on planes all the time. And I kind of said to him one day, you know, basically, Howard, how come you have it right? And how come I don't? And he said something that I will never forget, which was, you haven't put yourself in a position to win. And I've thought about that for, you know, the last 20 years. First of all, I think implicit in that is, what is winning for you? And a lot of us, I think, go along life with a certain inertia in our lives. And I was going along with a certain inertia. And so the challenge of taking the time, getting over the peer pressure, and figuring out what winning is. And then figuring out, is the life you're in, particularly business in this case, is it, if you're lucky, and if you're fortunate, and if you work, can you really win? And I found myself kind of in a mismatch between my own personal business model and being an investment banker. And so he opened my mind up to the fact that I wasn't in a position to win. Yeah. So I quit as an investment banker at age 40 and took some time off, and we looked at a bunch of deals, Howard and I, over the summer of 97. And then the strangest thing happened to me, on August 31st, 1997, I lost an opportunity to buy a company. And the second I hung up the phone, I decided I was moving to Seattle. And I slept on it that night, because Howard and I had talked about it for a while. I slept on it that night, and I called him the next day and said, I'm moving. And so he said, fine. And no, but I'm going into business with you. And he said, great. And so that was September 1st, and I moved out in December. And he had, you know, he kind of put me up for a while. And, you know, I think so much of, and Maveron's a startup, right? I mean, that's the only startup I've ever done. But it was a startup, and I think Howard has a great line. If you're going to build a 100-story skyscraper, make sure the corners are perfect when you lay the foundation. And I think a lot of the foundation is the partnership you have with the people you work with. And I think it's easy for us to kind of ignore that this is all about the people. But every time I've ignored it's about the people, I've made mistakes. So it was the best decision I ever made. Yeah. And what did you want to do when you grow up, when you were a kid? What did you want to be? Baseball player? No, I definitely didn't want to be a baseball player. I was looking through some of my camp yearbooks, and in one of them it said that as a seven-year-old, I was an improved right fielder. I don't know what's lower than that. So you were a star. You were a star. I was always intrigued by investments. And, you know, like Xerox, I'm dating myself, but Xerox was a big company in those days, and it was kind of this huge growth company. And I remember kind of studying it and being totally intrigued. You know, kind of the path from obscurity to ubiquity is just an exciting thing. And I remember I was on Wall Street. My route was going to be Wall Street just because I was a New York guy. And there really was no venture capital business when I was growing up institutionally. Sure. And I remember I was at a company called L.F. Rothschild the day that Genentech went public, and it was just buzzing. And, you know, the stock tripled or quadrupled, and it was like people were head spinning, and that was just so exciting. And so I kind of saw the deal side of it, and I must say, as time's passed, I've gotten less interested in the deal side and more enamored by the strategy side, the people side. I think we all want to be part of something that's bigger than each of us, and to watch the creation of something from an idea is much more fun than the deal side. And what was the first job you had that you actually made a dollar that wasn't from a relative? First job I ever had that I actually made a dollar that wasn't from a relative, I sold clothes at Barney's during the summer of college. Very cool. And it was fantastic for two reasons. One, we worked really, really long hours, and the pay was good, kind of over the summer. And then second, I was the only straight guy in the department that I worked in. So you got a lot of dates? Is that what you're saying? You know, I wouldn't put it that way. I would just leave it the way I did. What books are currently on your nightstand, or book, or in your Kindle, or in your whatever? Lean in, but I haven't read it. I guess the last book that I read, I will say that, you know, one of the problems with our business is I don't feel like I have a lot of leisure reading. Free time. Yeah. But let me get back to you on that. I am spacing. Okay. That's all right. You're allowed to. Anyhow, go ahead. Yep. Why did you attend college? Because you wanted to, or because you had to? Inertia. Inertia. Just because that's what they were doing. You know, when you grow up in New York, and you go to a high school that defines success as Harvard, Yale, and Princeton, in that order. And I didn't get into Harvard, Yale, and Princeton, in that order. I went to college because I had to. And one of the many things I've learned in life is what you think is so clear isn't clear in the fullness of time. So I see Buzz in the front row. Buzz and I share a Duke together. I went to Duke because I didn't get into Harvard, Yale, and Princeton. And then when business school came around, I only applied to one business school. I got into Harvard, and I went there. Had you told me 30 years after I got out of those places that I would be far more fond of Duke than Harvard, I would have said, back then, there's no way. And that's the case. And so I think one of the things you learn from age is just humility and sometimes the fullness of time changes your thoughts. Yeah, yeah, yeah, drastically. What is the favorite part of your day in your work environment? What's the favorite part? What part of your day, or maybe it's not part of your day, what's your favorite part of your week that you enjoy, that you actually look forward to? You know, our days are so varied. We can spend a full day at a company, or we could spend talking LPs or helping look at new companies. I think it's either when you have discovered some person and company that you think is really exciting, and kind of digging into that, or just hanging out at something that you know is just humming. Yeah. I mean... A company that you already know is doing well. 2010, whenever I was cranky, my wife used to say, just go over to Zulu. And it was, you know, on a Friday afternoon, she'd say, before you come home, just go by Zulu. And it was funny, but it was true. And, you know, when you're with a group that is, they know it's their time, and you have the privilege to kind of see that. And it's just an exciting ride. Yeah. Yeah, yeah. And let's talk about some of those things. So, what was one of the companies that you've invested in that was a big surprise that they failed? You know, every time you invest, you have this... It's a chance, right? You always... Exuberance. I think the most interesting recent company, it didn't fail, but it didn't work as an investment, was Groupon. You know, I probably met 15 people who said to me, Oh, you've invested in a Groupon? And, you know, normally, when it's too good to be true, it probably is too good to be true. And so, we invested in late 2010, I think it was. And, you know, I mean, the company's actually come back quite strongly. And stock price has done very well. But that was probably the biggest swing between market exuberance and reality. Yeah, like truth. And, you know, I mean, the financial markets are always driven by some modicum of fear and greed. And you've got this very interesting kind of buoyancy right now where, you know, stocks are going up 10, 20, 50 points in a day. And, you know, IPOs are actually working. Our IPOs are working really well. And, you know, sometimes in our business, it's just better luck to be lucky than smart. We were fortunate enough. We had backed a really young guy who went to Duke, and he had a company called Seatme in the Bay Area, and he sold it to Yelp. And so, about two months ago, and we were forced to take Yelp paper. Well, now the stock's gone up three times, or two and a half times. And, you know, what seemed like a difficult thing then turns out to be really lucky. Yes, and like you say, lucky is not so bad. I'd rather be lucky than smart, yeah. So, you are, as you've, you know, we've talked before, you're a huge, huge fan of startup founders. Why is that? Why do you get so much excitement and, you know, exuberance talking to a startup founder? You know, I think the first thing is just, as I've gotten older, I just think it's cool when you can live a life that is truly your dreams. And I think startups provide that. On the other hand, one of the things that I would debunk is that, you know, how many of you in the room are CEOs? Okay. I would venture to say that a decent percentage of you, no disrespect, I learned this the hard way as a CEO of Mavron. You know, I'm not sure that we have such a societal pressure that people have to lead things. Yeah. And what I love about startups is you can create something far bigger than you, and you can go from being an idea to integrating it to so many people's lives, whether or not that's employees or customers, or ultimately shareholders. And the first ride I saw of that was Starbucks. And, you know, I spent an hour and a half with Howard the first time in August of 1991, and he couldn't stop talking, and meeting ended. And I thought it was a bad meeting because I didn't get a chance to talk. And as an investment banker, all you have to do is sell, right? And so I couldn't sell. So I was frustrated. And we walked out, and, you know, it was clear that the meeting was over. Howard was finished. And we were leaving. And there, in the old Starbucks headquarters, not this one, there was a really long corridor. And at the middle of the corridor, he stopped. And he turned to me, and he said, do you know what the problem with investment bankers are? Of course, you're, like, stuttering. I did not know what to say. And this was 1991, and I hadn't really decided that there were a lot of problems with investment bankers. And so he looked me in the eye, and he said, there are no mensches in investment banking. How many of you know what that word means? Okay, for those of you who don't, mensch is a Yiddish word that means just being a good guy. And it was really fascinating that he kind of, in my mind, as an investment banker, put a bullseye on his forehead by saying, be a mensch, and you'll get my business. And it was really, I was struck. I didn't know what to say. But I never forgot that. Sure. Because I had really never had a meeting where someone made the assumption I would understand what that word meant and was as direct with me. And so, but anyhow, I'm wandering aimlessly, sorry. That's right. Why do I, so I think part of it was, that was the first time that I was somewhat of a hanger-on, but watching something go from 50 stores to today, 20,000. And so, just watching services and products that are great integrate into the lives of their employees and customers, I just think is kind of really gratifying. And, you know, what I hope for the startup world, frankly, is I think it's gotten a little too popular lately. You know, and we're taking advantage of that. We have an investment in a company called General Assembly, which is in New York. I see a bunch of heads nodding. And they've moved to a bunch of places. And it's attempting to kind of help educate people about entrepreneurships and startup. But, you know, for a lot of people, it's great and it's a whole new venue. But for some people, I think there is a trendiness to it that is unfortunate. And, you know, I think get in touch with who you are and what you really want, what risks you want, and what rewards you want. Put yourself in a position to win. And if that fits in a startup context, fantastic. And accept the consequences that most startups don't work. Don't work. You know, and another thing that I love about it is I am really intrigued by the concept of what role does failure play in your success in life. And Buzz and I share a mentor, Coach K, and he has this great quote, get comfortable that failure is part of the road to success. Now, here's the guy who's the winningest coach in college basketball history, and he says that. And most people who aren't really students of the game don't remember the challenges that he had health-wise in 1996 and how everyone said Duke was done. And, you know, then he clawed his way back. He was out and had to leave, and he clawed his way back. And lo and behold, he won the championship in 2001. And he's done okay. He's done okay. But because he was comfortable with the fact that his failure ended up motivating him. But not everybody kind of works that way. Yeah, not all of us do. And the other thing he said is they asked him, why does he win so much? And he says, I hate losing more than I like winning. And so if I had to give you guys, for those of you who are in a startup, you know, we've created a list of, like, the 10 things of an entrepreneur. And one of the first is just this relentless tenacity. I mean, how many times are people going to tell you this can't be done? And a lot of times, frankly, they may be right. But if you're going to be a successful entrepreneur, I think you've got to kind of take feedback but ignore it in a certain respect. Yeah, at some level, you just can't believe that it's true. Or you have to kind of believe that you're going to pivot and you're going to succeed. Yeah, so you mentioned something there about mentors. Do you enjoy being the mentor or the mentee more? Because obviously you have mentors and you have mentees. You're in both sides of that. Which do you enjoy more? Which do you enjoy more? Well, I remember when I was in my 30s and for the first time, someone said, how come you don't have more mentors? And I said, what do you mean? I said, well, people want to be people's mentors. Have you really kind of come to grips with that? And frankly, I hadn't. I thought that was an imposition to people. And the guy who said this to me was kind of an incredible politician. And he was a congressman. And then I saw him in a business setting after he left Congress. And he said, make sure to create mentors. So I spent a lot of my 30s creating mentors and my 40s. And I would say as I've gotten older, it's become more fun to turn it around and just find someone that wants to be mentored but yet has a certain boldness and brashness, wants to be disruptive, but also wants to listen. And I think getting aligned with somebody to go after something, win, lose, or draw after you know you're aligned is really very rewarding. Yeah, there's, well, there's not many feelings like that, right? There's not many times in your life you get to feel that way. If you, oh, let's go in another direction. Give us three things not to do in a pitch to you. Not to do. Without any data, don't act like you know this is going to work. Talk to me just about brand and not about facts. Because I think most businesses, particularly these days, are an incredible balance between data and heart. And what else? Say, I did this, I did that, and prove to me that you can't build a team. That's good. Do you, at Mavron, do you guys decide by committee? Or do you have an internal, you know, one becomes like you're a champion of XYZ company? How does that, how does that decision work internally? Well, obviously you all have, you all have some say. Well, the, what I like to use these days is, we're a four and a half year old firm with 15 years of experience. So we started in 1998, and frankly in 1998, a monkey with a cup could have raised the fund. That's not the case anymore. A lot of monkeys did, and I might have been one of them. And like you say, you're lucky, you're lucky is good, right? Lucky is, lucky is good. Anyhow, so, in our current iteration, which is four and a half years old, we have a really good karma around the table on Mondays. And that is, kind of, we're looking for three things. I mean, more, but if you had to narrow it down. We're looking for really exceptional people. I mean, we're not looking for the 1%. We're looking for the one-tenth of 1% of the person and the team. Second, we're looking for an incredibly differentiated product orientation. We're not looking to make trades. We're looking to, kind of, be in the ground floor of enduring consumer businesses. And that's all we invest in, is consumer. And we're looking for people who have, kind of, a DNA that says, I want to create a different consumer product or service that's enduring. And then, lastly, we're looking for people that are attacking problems in very big markets. Because, unfortunately, one of the realities about the VC business that we should talk about is, you know, grand slam home runs are what defines us. And it's not a doubles and triples business, period. So, we're looking for those three things. And what we've learned is that the six or seven people around the table are different in their level of discernment of each of those three things. Because you've matured. Because we've matured. The team's matured. But, you know, take people judging for a second. VC, you have to both identify the space, identify the people, and then, kind of, pick the company. But the person that might have the insight and vision to say, this is a great space, might not be the best person to really drill down and find out how great an entrepreneur and an executor he or she is. So, getting back to your question, what we've done is, we know what we have to do. And we know each of our strengths and weaknesses, and we kind of weigh in around our strengths. So, regardless of who finds the company, there's one person on our team who's fantastic at interviewing. And we all respect his experience as being an operating executive and hiring and firing a lot of people. And so, he weighs in on, is this person what we call an A entrepreneur? And so, it's a collective set of decisions. Now, does it have to be uniform? No. Oh, yeah. I don't think. If it's so obvious, it's probably wrong. Wrong, exactly. If everybody bets, it's probably the wrong bet. But we've learned to trust each other's strengths and hopefully play to those strengths. Yeah. And, you know, you talked about books. There's a bunch of really interesting books about playing to your strengths and not trying to spend your whole life neutralizing your weaknesses. And that's where I get back to the whole alignment question. How do you have the courage to decide to live your life based on your strengths? I was at my 30th business school reunion three weeks ago. Never thought I'd be old enough to say that. And one of the presentations that was standing room only was, are you leading an extraordinary life? And there was this guy who basically kind of said, hard work isn't the determinant of success. He said, you know, his maid works really hard. No one harder in his life than her. And, you know, I mean, hasn't reaped the financial rewards. And he basically kind of talked about getting in touch with what you do. You know, the two things. Figure out what you like and figure out what you do best and go do it. A lot of it. Just keep doing it until someone tells you to stop. If you had to choose, can you tell us in a few short sentences how you manage a relationship with a founder? Can you narrow that down? Each relationship is different. I think I try, we try at Mavron to not have an individual style or a template. I mean, my relationship with the guys at Zulily or Potbelly is totally different than my relationship with a seed or whatever. So, you know, the first thing we do is try to understand each other before we make the investment. You hear so many terrible stories about VCs and entrepreneurs. And the reason is neither party is always committed to alignment at the start. You know, first of all, I'd say there are a lot of companies that probably shouldn't raise VC money. I mean, I look at a guy like Eric Levine here in town for Seller Tracker. I was talking about him earlier. Any of you guys know Seller Tracker? Whinies? Okay. So, you know, here's a guy who worked at Microsoft and he's been feigning. I don't want VC money. I don't need it. And he's growing all the time. So, I think the first question, he's in touch with what he wants. So, I think the first question in terms of how do you manage a relationship is really understand what motivates that person. What are their hot buttons? Why are they doing this? Why is it that they're going to succeed when others fail? Why is it that they're just going to refuse to lose? Are they aligned with us? And kind of work through a lot of that personal and business stuff up front. And so, I think that's the foundation and a cornerstone for a more active dialogue of this is what's on my mind. I mean, I had a chat today with a guy who's one of our CEOs. And I'm asking him, does he want, we're pivoting the company. And I said, you know, do you want to go? Do you want to leave? And he has a military background. So, he's so, I'm not leaving until I finish the mission. And I said, if you want to leave, you've earned the right to leave. You've also earned the right to stay. What was fascinating was he was pushing it back to me to help him think through it and getting back to how do you manage it. I feel like that kind of alignment, mutual respect, creates better outcomes. Yeah. And the key, I think one of the keys for Maveron is because you're doing that early on. You're doing it actually before you write the check, most of the, or at least. Hopefully. Hopefully. Maybe not every time. What's the most challenging time period in your career? Oh, 2008. Why? World's imploding. Were you guys raising a new fund then? You weren't raising a new fund. We raised a fund in late 07 and early 08. But we raised it from foundations and endowments who found their balance sheets in a state that they didn't expect. In early 09. A couple of our partners left. And we're in consumer businesses that were challenged. So it was kind of a triple storm. And, you know, you just got to, you know, I had the Noah's Ark kind of analogy. You just got to get through the storm. You know it's going to pass. Yeah, you know. And, you know, our most recent IPO is a company called Potbelly. And, you know, had you told me that that stock was going to trade at, you know, I think it closed today at 26 or 27 in the dog days of, boom, do we put more money in? What do we do? No. So, you know, I would never have imagined that. So I think it's incredibly rewarding to watch yourself and a team go through these moments of resilience and come out stronger for it. And I think it makes it even better kind of when you get through as a group. But that was definitely our most challenging time. Yeah. And what has been your most fun investment? Not the most profitable. What's been the most fun? So many different ways to define fun. How do you define fun? How does Dan define fun? Is it the CEO's fun? Is it the company's vision? Is that the exciting part? What one sticks in your mind like, you know what, I had a lot of fun with that company. Well, I would say Trupanion has been as rewarding as any in terms of fun for me. So I get a call in 2006 from a guy who at the time was an EIR at Benchmark. And he says, Benchmark looked at this, really think it's an interesting business, but it's not going to grow really fast. But I think it's got a lot of potential. You should meet this guy. So I meet this guy. He comes by. I'm in New York. We meet in New York. And a lot of times it doesn't click. But in this particular time, I just kind of felt that this guy, something said to me, he's quiet, but something special here. So I got to know his business and he had a 4,000 pet health insurance company in a place called Bowen Island. Has anyone been to Bowen Island? Okay. Two people. Outside of Vancouver, B.C., right? So I got to go. So I'm intrigued by this. So I fly up to Bowen Island. And Kenmore Air takes me up there. And I land in a seaplane in this outer island outside of Vancouver. And walk up the hill. And he's got seven people in his company. And he has arranged for me to meet all seven people. Is he Canadian? He's Canadian. If your name is Daryl and you're Canadian, we'll fund you. If there's anyone in the audience whose name is Daryl and you're Canadian, we'll fund you. That's a different joke. Anyhow, so I'm going through these meetings. And it strikes me how early stage this company is. And then I meet a guy named David. And so I shake his hand. He's kind of a middle-aged guy. And I say, why are you here, David? And he says, well, you know, I'm a territory partner for this company. It wasn't called True Panion at the time. And I also am the father of the CEO. And so I said, okay. I hadn't seen this situation before where a guy, as one of seven employees, hires his father. And so the father tells me this story that, lo and behold, from the age of 16, his son has wanted to create the world's leading pet health insurance company. And I was like, really? And he said, yeah, loves animals, this and that. And that's what he wanted to do. And, you know, here he is six years into that odyssey. And it was really interesting because getting back to one of our key things for entrepreneurs is, why is this person advantaged? Why will this person continue to just persevere in the face of challenges? And so that was a really interesting data point. Anyhow, so I came home and my partner said, you don't know anything about insurance, forget about this deal. And I couldn't forget about the deal. And I didn't know anything about insurance. Sure. So they said. But you know somebody that does. Well, so they said, go out and find somebody who knew insurance. And total happenstance, I met a guy from my fraternity who hooked me up with another guy from my college fraternity who I hadn't spoken to either one of them in 15 years. And so we found an insurance company. And then I said, you know, then they said, well, you don't know anything about, or, you know, we need to raise the rest of the money and we're only putting up X. Anyhow, there was a bunch of milestones. We got through it all. We did the funding. And first thing we said is, okay, we need to augment this guy's passion and vision with an operating guy. And the first operating guy we found didn't work out. And so the board gets into this kind of rope-a-dope routine and feels like they're paralyzed because, you know, we were all for one in terms of the fit. It was your guy, right? Well, it was close to my guy. Yeah, but no, I'm just saying it was the board's guy. Yeah. It was their guy. They're putting him in. We were convinced it was going to work and it did. Anyhow, so going from that to a company that's really incredibly fully staffed. But what I didn't say is, so during the courting process, this guy, Daryl Rawlings, says to me, we really want Maveron. We think you're going to add a lot of value. He said, okay. Why don't you move from Bowen Island to Seattle if you think we can add a lot of value? Next day, he calls me up and he says, I spoke to my wife. We'll move if you guys do the deal. You know, again, really interesting data point that says to me, this guy is serious. And it's been exciting to kind of watch that and fun. And we've gotten to know each other really as people. And it's exciting for me to think what comes next for him. And, you know, he's made it very clear to me that he wants to do this for 15 years. Sure. That's his passion. Yeah. Yeah. Exactly. Pet insurance. Pet health insurance. Who to none. Well, how much do you think Americans spend on pets every year? 51 billion. Yeah. Lots and lots. And in the U.K., what percentage of the pets do you think are insured? Zero. No. 25%. In the U.S., what percentage? Two. Two. So there's your market. Hopefully. We're growing pretty good, so there's a market out there. Yeah, yeah. You are growing. Which company has done the largest pivot and made it? I think Motley Fool for us. Wow. You guys invested in Motley Fool? Yeah. Holy cow. Really? Which idea was that? I think that was my idea. September 28, 1999. We used to have this thing where right before our annual meetings, we would announce investments. Just total happenstance. And so we got foolish and invested in the Motley Fool. And, you know, did the thing. That was a big round, too. Wasn't that a big round? Or, I mean, didn't they? It was a very big round. Us, Mayfield, and a few others. Yep. And, anyhow, brought in the big company guy. That didn't work out. And the advertising eyeball orientation in 2000 and 2001, 2002, you know, was completely changed. And so that was the early days of the internet. They were kind of one of the first preeminent financial advice sites. And 100% advertising, but 100%, you know, in those days, it was just kind of eyeballs. And so we had to create, they had to create a real business and business model. And they went about it starting in 2002 and, you know, five, six times a year, seven times a year, I would fly back to Washington, D.C. Helped them recruit a bunch of board members. We ended up pivoting to a subscription orientation. And today, very successful, cash flow positive, and, you know, kind of terrific company. Is there going to be an exit there? We exited. Oh, you exited. Okay. It was clear that the founders had a different exit orientation than we did. Yeah. And so the company bought us out. Yeah. If you could pick one activity that you talk about more than anything else to an entrepreneur, what would that be? Getting the team right. I just, you know, like real estate, people talk about location, location, location. I would say startups, to me, it's about people, people, people. And there's a funny story, getting back to Coach K. In 2000, after the world blew up in October of 2000, I had this incredible privilege to be with him for an hour and a half in his office at Cameron Indoor Stadium. And at one point, he looks over to me and he says, Dan, do you love your team? I said, wow, I hadn't really thought about that. And I think for certain people like him, loving your team is kind of a conditioned precedent requirement for being successful. And I hadn't really examined that for me. But 13 years later, it's pretty clear that in a bizarre way, he understood business far better than I did. And so I think what I try to talk to, if I, I mean, there's a lot of things, right? Because it's not, but if you have the right people that you rely on and trust and feel have a complimentary skill, then I think you have an advantage. And one of the things we ask ourselves, if we did a seed a year or two in, how is the person recruited? And if the, if we're two years in and we think this is a big idea and there's been no impact players hired, that kind of tells you something about either the space or the CEO. And I think there, the pattern that I've observed is the best, biggest companies always seem to be able to hire people they shouldn't hire because the CEO, the environment creates something that attracts people who've kind of been there, done that to balance out this incredible disruptive attitude. And it's that alchemy of, of experience and new that I think every company has to go through and transition. But if I had to pick one thing, it would be people. Yeah. People first, product second. But, you know, one of my other mentors is Bill Campbell and he's product, product, product, product. And what's, we've had a number of sessions where Howard spoke and Bill Campbell spoke. And Howard says, first thing you should hire is a, is a HR person. And Bill says, first thing you should hire is a product person. And so I think part of that is the, is a different orientation between classic consumer businesses and technology. But I think we're in this incredible time right now that we're all so privileged to live in where the intersection of consumer and technology is like it's never been before. And, you know, you see so many of these companies that three, four years ago they had a zero mobile penetration. And today it's 40%. So, I mean, we're living in a transformative thing. And my belief is that great consumer tech companies start from the inside out and you get the people right, then it flows to the customers. Yeah. And you figure it out, like you say, because you'll end up pivoting or something's going to change. If, if, what, what type of company would you like to meet? One that's creating some revenue and is growing or a company that with no revenue but with a billion dollar idea? No revenue led by a killer guy with a billion dollar idea. Because you're swinging for the fences. Because swinging for the fences doesn't, we're looking for. You can't hit a double. If you, if you, if you, yeah, yes. Okay, I'll stop sugarcoating. We're swinging for the fences. That's fair. That's fair. What makes Maveron different from other VC firms in the area? Or at Maxnot area, let's just say, what makes Maveron different than other VC firms in your opinion? Probably three or four things. First, the consumer only focus is just different. Yeah. It's a little unique. And frankly, I think we can easily partner with other VCs that have a different orientation. The world doesn't have to be a zero sum game. So first, consumer only. Second, our entrepreneurs tell us that they meet a broader spectrum of people from Maveron and our network than typically they meet from other firms. So we have a kind of team sport attitude that when we are at events, you know, it's not Dan's company. It's our company. And we try to figure out how we can each help entrepreneurs. And then lastly, kind of sounds corny. But it was told to me by an entrepreneur two weeks ago, they feel more family-like with us. We try to foster a no-assholes family orientation and collaborate. And we have a CEO forum for three days, two days every year. And I'm just humbled by the action or the attitude that people come out of it, that it's a different conference. Why? Because people let their guards down and talk about real problems as opposed to they tell me a lot of these conferences they go to. And there's a need to put up a facade. And we try to foster that by just being hungry but humble ourselves and realize that really it's the entrepreneur that is creating all the value in our business. And it's taken us a while to get total clarity on that, but that's true. Every great win we've had, it's a function of the entrepreneur and the team have created something because of their hard work and focus. Do I want to believe that at times we've added value? Absolutely. Are there situations where we've, you know, helped materially? I think so. Sure. But pushing all the credit to the people that create value I think is a good thing in life. And so we try to make it a little more family-esque. The world doesn't have to be a zero-sum game. Sure. I think in a certain sense there are so few great entrepreneurs with so few great ideas that it is somewhat of a zero-sum game amongst VCs. But once you're in the game and in the company, I think it doesn't have to be a zero-sum game. Yeah, you can still win. You guys are investing, you guys have been investing earlier and earlier. Like you used to not do any seed. Correct. And now you're like pretty early. Is that because of the trend that everyone reads about or why is that? Why are you investing earlier and earlier? Well, getting back to the challenges of 2009, we looked around at the VC landscape and we asked ourselves, where can we excel? And we had added a lot of people with operating experience and we saw that successful companies in B and C rounds just got very pricey. And we said it takes far less capital ever to document consumer acceptance and traction. So we could be putting less capital to work yet getting meaningful stakes. So getting back to trying to find our place in the ecosystem, we decided we couldn't compete with these later stage guys or, you know, certain firms that could raise a gazillion dollars and could put 50 million or 20 million to work in a series B. So we decided to move earlier and frankly create a pipeline of entrepreneurs, not just a pipeline of deals. Yeah. So again, going back to the people, investing in that entrepreneur. Yeah. At Mavron, everyone has, like you've said, everyone kind of has a specialty. You know, they have some special pieces to what they're really knowledgeable about. What's yours? What do you bring to the table? So, well, I think in any company that you've found, you have a certain role as... As founder. As founder and some responsibility in terms of leadership, unless it's clear that someone else is doing that. But I tend... There's a lot of different ways to be successful in VC. I think... And there's more ways to be mediocre than there are to be successful. I tend to be focused on the people side and that could be building a network of folks that source and help our companies. I tend to be kind of oriented toward trying to think through the psychological contract that we have with CEOs and their people issues. I'm not as strong a product orientation guy. Yeah. And, you know, I think the next chapter for me in my career, frankly, is helping some of the people in my firm, Rebecca, Jason, David, kind of watch them come up and be successful. And kind of transcend Maveron from something that Dan and Howard founded to an enduring institution that's recognized and respected as one of the leading funders of consumer businesses. Yeah. That's a good goal. What is one of your favorite stories about your first meeting of a founder? Is it the guy that met with Viggo Figuero? No. One guy cold called and said, he sent me an hourglass that was about this big. And he said. So he sent it and then he called. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. And he said, here's an hourglass. All I need is 18 minutes of your time or whatever. And it was kind of cool. So I took it. We flipped it over. And by the end of the 18 minutes, that was that. But it was kind of an interesting way to go. I think, you know, like it or not, if you look at our funnel, we get over a thousand plans a year. And we're going to make four to six core investments and, say, 15 to 20 seed investments. So if things aren't referred to us in some way or another, it's hard for us to really focus on it. Just because of the ratio of. Yeah. Volume. Yeah. Yeah. What has the. What company had the easiest exit? And why do you think that was? Are any exits easy? Well, not really. But you know what I mean. I mean, like, what's been the. What's been something that's like, wow, that was. That was a lot easier than I expected, maybe. Maybe that's a better way to phrase that. Easier than expected. I think our first investment was the easiest exit. eBay. You know, I mean. Because how long did you hold it? We invested on June 11th, 1998, and it went public at the end of September. So that was pretty tough. Well, they wanted Howard on the board, and so we were fortunate enough to participate. The company went public, then it went up, then the stock went down. Sure. And then over the holidays in 1998, I had gotten married in November. And over the holidays, my wife and I went to Asia for our honeymoon. And for whatever reason, stock kind of kept going up five to ten points every day. And it was the best honeymoon anyone had ever had. It was like, it was almost a fantasy. And. Making money every day. It was like, oh, this is. Are you kidding me? But, you know, eBay was both a blessing and a curse. Right? You start a business. First investment. Your first investment ends up being so successful. This is so easy. Exactly. Rose-colored glasses. You can say it's better to be lucky than smart, but unless you get really kicked in the you-know-whats, I don't think I'm capable of learning the lesson. So it was a mixed blessing. Yeah. But that was the easiest. Yeah. Yeah, you get, you actually, and in your career, you're thinking, wow, I'm pretty good at this. Right? Just starting out, I had a first investment. Well, our first annual meeting was in September of 99, and we had made four or five investments. One was eBay, and at the time it was like a 40 or 50X, and another was drugstore.com, and at the time it was a 10X. Wow. And people thought we were drinking something special. Audience, questions, feel free to go here, Chris, right in the middle. How do you work with angel groups, and give us a sense of your vision for the future VCOs companies? So one of our partners in San Francisco runs the, or ran the Internet Investment Committee at the Band of Angels. So he was an angel for three or four years. You know, we try to have angel groups understand where we're different, which is consumer only, and we look at three verticals, web-enabled consumer services, education, and wellness. And so we're kind of looking for outstanding people in those three areas, and try to at least let those groups know what we're doing. And hopefully find enough of a screen that they don't send us, you know, kind of everything that, so I think in any deal referral situation, I think it's the trust and confidence to know, okay, I've taken the time to understand what you want, and I'm not bringing you things that you don't want. In terms of the future of VC, we invested in a crowdfunding company called Circle Up down in San Francisco, and it's crowdfunding for early-stage consumer businesses. And it's fascinating because we don't invest in, you know, kind of food or beauty companies, but they have used the web to create this highly targeted, like AngelList, but more targeted group around consumer companies. And I think a lot of the consumer companies love it because they're attracting, or they're getting, in a very efficient way, exposure to a lot of people who can really add value as angels to them, who have pattern recognition about this stuff. So, I think, you know, you're going to see more and more of that. To me, there's been a lot of debate lately about how radical is the model going to change. My intuition is that the model for late-stage funding may change, in that once the platforms of influence and the relationships are created in the seed A and perhaps B round, then what does a company really need? In my mind, a lot of times it just needs more fuel to grow. But it doesn't need another person around the boardroom table. Sometimes it does, in which case it should seek that out. But in many times, particularly for successful companies, I think they're just looking for capital. And it feels to me like technology and the web can be a crossing function as, you know, these secondary markets and all these things have kind of come earlier on. But, you know, I mean, to me, in terms of early-stage stuff, I get back to it's all about the people. And you can have really good track records but not click with an entrepreneur. And, you know, we sold a business a few months ago. And we're at the closing dinner. And business was sold to a strategic. And, you know, the entrepreneur basically says to me, this was an okay outcome, but here's to our next one. And, you know, I mean, to me, that's what I live for is the people you want to back again being in a position to have them know you and you know them. So I think there will be some disruption and I think AngelList and certain things like that are going to work. But I caution people against looking at early-stage money as fungible. It's not. And early-stage money comes with an attitude. And make sure that the people on your bus are people you want on the bus. And, you know, when I've seen, you know, we were in a company here in town, Decide.com. Sure. Sold to eBay. Sure. Mike Fridgeton did a really nice job. And, you know, it was an incredible -- Mike interviewed the investors as much as we interviewed him. Yeah. And, you know, we had Madrona in the A. We did the B. Vulcan did the C. And it was a wonderful experience because we were respectful of the entrepreneur and of each other. And it was kind of a complete collaboration. And I think Mike fostered that. You know, I remember the day we're signing the term sheet, he says, "Can I come over to your office? I'd like to sign the term sheet with you in person." That's interesting. So he comes over. And, you know, when he signs the term sheet, he says, "What would this be to really be one of your best investments? Because that's where I'd like to start." Right. And it was just a great psychological grounding of how much he cared. And that followed through to the boardroom dynamics. And it was just a much more pleasant experience. Yeah. Yeah. And I think more productive. For everybody. Go ahead. One of the things that you mentioned was the coach at Duke talking about getting comfortable and failing as part of success. What keeps you going, Dan, and what motivates you, you know, when you fail to get back up and keep doing what you need? You know, I think the biggest thing that motivates me now is transcending from a good fund to a great firm. And we're fortunate for a variety of reasons that we have a good fund going right now. And how do you create -- how do you help Maveron become an enduring business? And how do you lift up the people who have trusted you and wanted to be part of our team and have them inherit that mantle? So I get back to what I love about Duke basketball is it's bigger than any individual. And I'm trying to help Maveron become bigger than Howard or I. And, you know, give back to the people who trusted us and watch them grow. And I couldn't have said that, frankly, in my 30s or 40s because I hadn't achieved a level of grounding and satisfaction amongst myself. And so I feel fortunate that I'm able to try and do that. All the way in the back, very back row. Sorry for such serious topics. People say I'm serious. You know, let me -- you know, in our business, if your takeaway is that we're batting 600 or 700, then I've created the wrong takeaway. You know, this is hard. But the internal workings are you pick a strategy and you ignore the data. And you're really slow to pivot. And, you know, everyone's kind of still living on the investment thesis that they invested behind. And it's a year or two in. And, you know, we're going to make this work. We're going to work harder. We're going to, you know, broaden our product. And I think, you know, most of the time when it's a challenge, it's hard to pivot. And it's emotionally hard. And both the people around the table and the CEO sometimes can do it and sometimes they can't. But I think the biggest dynamic is how do you, as a group, help look at data and ask what's too impulsive and therefore you shouldn't move that fast or, you know, how you should pivot. And I would say ignoring the data in certain circumstances is the worst sin. And I think it happens because people are too committed to the initial hypothesis. And I would also say in a lot of boardrooms, there's the 800-pound gorilla that's under the table that no one has the guts to talk about. And I get back to it's the CEO's job in my mind to orchestrate a group of people who are transparent and can work together so that there is no 800-pound gorilla. You can talk about these things. And I found that I have some CEOs who are -- I can make the decision myself. And here's what I think we should do. And then there's others that need to iterate and create a discussion and get input. You know, some of us are kind of not burdened by self-doubt and are intuitive. And others of us like to analyze. And so I think knowing who you are and creating that environment around the table that makes you be successful as the CEO in your company is really important. Right here in the middle. So the question is, for early stage investments, young entrepreneurs, how do you handle those guys that are early on in their careers, maybe the first time? Yeah. I think we do handle them slightly different in the sense that, you know, I mean, the reality of our business is if we put $100,000 into a seed, we probably need to treat it differently than if we put $5 million into an A. That said, what we say to the entrepreneur is, in doing the seed, we want to earn the right to do your A. And so that means that we need enough connectivity and enough help that they feel we've earned the right to do the A. And in a number of circumstances here in town and other places, we've succeeded in that. And occasionally we haven't looked at the situation as objectively as a new investor. And that's painful. But, you know, I think ultimately creating a cadence of reporting and understanding enough to be helpful and then creating the right opening with the CEO when he or she needs high impact decision making that we can help them think through it is what we try to do. No, I think it's easier in certain ways to start a startup in Seattle. This Valley Seattle thing, I think, is nonsense. I think a lot of Rich Barton's thoughts on this make a lot of sense to me, which is this is a wonderful, wonderful place to start a company. I mean, I look at Zulily as an example, plenty of technology talent, plenty of consumer talent. And these guys flew below the radar screen for a long period of time, hired an awesome team. That same company in the Valley would have been scrutinized a lot more. And, you know, I've lived this, you know, they started in our office in November of 2009. And really until Andreessen Horowitz invested in them in 2012. No one knew about them. Very few people knew about them. And so they picked up ahead of steam. And so I love Seattle as a place to build a company. I think Seattle would benefit from greater, substantive, bold thinking. And when Barton talks about, you know, Seattle entrepreneurs need to be more audacious, I think it's a hard thing to talk about. But if you're a VC and you're looking for these kind of multi-hundred-million-dollar, billion-dollar companies, you need people who believe they can create those. And who are capable of finding ideas in those spaces that they can create them. But I think in terms of the environment of hiring people, clearly it's easier here. The loyalty of employees, I think clearly it's easier here. And I think the lack of scrutiny, Pando Daily and Crunchbase talking about the five startups that are doing the same thing as you increases your likelihood of success. So I think this is a fantastic place to start a company. And this nonsense about there's no money, BS. The world doesn't need N+1 VCs. The shortage is great entrepreneurs. There's too much, even in today's reduced environment, there's still too much capital chasing too few great entrepreneurs attacking big ideas. So when we started in 1998, we asked ourselves why should we exist? And Howard and I decided that we had a different view that technology was integrating into consumers' lives. And we wanted to create an expertise that focused on that. So that's the marketing pitch on why we do that. The other marketing pitch is if you create a lot of B2C companies, they can help each other. But the reality is we dabbled in enterprise and we sucked at it. And getting back to humility, this is a humbling business. It's really hard to be good. On average, people don't make money as VCs other than fees. And that's not how we want to make money. So we asked ourselves, and I think every startup should ask themselves this, what do you do better than other people? And how does that concentration work in your favor? So basically a pragmatic reality of trying to say, what do we do well? And I will say in the enterprise-oriented deals that I've done, I haven't been able to help the entrepreneur as much as I'd like to. And my team, same way. The transition from investment banker to successful venture capitalist is very, very hard to do. Because you're kind of not prepared for the reality. So the best thing that happened to me in investment banking, I met a guy named Howard Schultz. And he enabled me to free myself from being an investment banker. You know, better lucky than smart. I think there are elements of what you do in investment banking that can be relevant. But they're on the fringe. I mean, the first thing about great investments are picking great people. As an investment banker, you really don't get that training. In fact, you know, if the deal is going to happen, you want to be, you want to create optionality and be in it. The second thing is, how do you help people build real companies? And most investment bankers have no operating experience. I'm not going to be able to do that. I'm not going to be able to do that. interviewee_bio: Dan Lewis runs Convoy , an on-demand commercial trucking company that offers the industry’s top service levels.
video_title: Dan Lewis (CEO Convoy) at Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Grime. So those of you who don't know, Convoy has done some amazing things over the last 14 months. Very young company, but they've raised a few million dollars and actually started to make some headway. We have the CEO tonight of Convoy. So without any further time and ado, I'm going to welcome to the stage Dan Lewis from Convoy. Thank you. It's always great. Thank you. All right, is this on or should I use this? No, it's actually using both. All right. We'll do one for make sure that we don't miss anything. That sounds good. Hi. So again, for those of you who have not been to a Startup Grime event, which many of you have not, this is really just a fireside chat. It really is a conversation just between Dan and I. So like I said, if you need to get up to go to the restroom or get a glass of wine, feel free to do that. You're not going to interrupt us whatsoever. We'll take questions, you know, sometime in the next 40 minutes or so. So feel free to ask questions. We'll have time for that. But typically, early versions of products like that on a business-to-business perspective, there's no way to de-feature them and have them be worth anything. So, you're like, I have three features. I've taken two of the features out so you can have the free version. It's like, okay, well, you kind of gutted the product, right? So, from a B2B perspective, that's a bummer. As an investor, I would never use it. So, and I would look at AngelList as a comp and decide whether it's really worthwhile in pursuing. And the reason is because I don't invest in deals outside of my territory. I want to be able to look at you and go, how's it going? How's your team doing? Which is true for most agents. And most VCs. Most VCs don't want to get on a plane. So, to invest in something that there's no due diligence in. So, I've worked on corporate VC, regular VC, and Angel investors. The syndication that AngelList put together was really great in the valley. But if you look at the density of it outside of the valley, not so much. So, I think from a business model perspective, you have to do, that's a marketplace as a business model. I've written a lot about. So, there's nine business models. My blog is DKParker.com. I get paid so much when you read my blog. I'd really like to sell my product to you. So, marketplaces are one of the nine business models. The tough things about marketplaces is you have to get product market fit right on both sides. It's really hard. If you get it, you can create a bigger product, a bigger market than existed before. Uber is an example. Right? Because that market didn't exist that way before they created a bigger market than actually existed. So, if you do marketplaces right, you can do that. So, marketplaces are hard because you have to have a full product suite and you have to have product market fit on both sides. So, they're hard. So, that's one of the nine. Right? I tend to lean towards subscription model early versus service models. I run a services business now, which I always kind of scratch my head and go again. So, by the way, if people are thinking about doing a startup, you haven't decided yet on a startup, you're working on a startup. Some of you? Okay. Right. So, here's a pop quiz for you and then we'll be done. If you sell a services business, what multiple of revenue will it sell for on average? One point nine. So, one to three? What did you say? Point nine? Point nine? Point nine? Six. Up to six? From the accounting firm? Depends a lot on the business. But on services perspective, if you have a services business where people are required to deliver the product that you're selling, point nine to six is pretty generally the case. So, if you're doing a million dollars in revenue, the business will sell for $900,000 to $6 million, depending on what type of business it is, how good your customer base is. What's the multiple on a recurring revenue SaaS software business? Five to ten. Five to ten. Any other guesses? Up to twelve or fourteen. So, if you have a million dollar business on a recurring software as a services business that is going to sell, it could sell for up to twelve million dollars. Now, how much work is it to build a million dollar business? A lot. Is it a lot more different for a product than a services business? Not a hell of a lot. So, if you haven't decided to do a services business yet, I'm just saying, you may want to consider doing a product business. It's not that services businesses are bad, they're just harder to do. Right? Because you need people and every time you get a new client, you have to add more people. And you may have good methodology. Good methodology helps you to get to the multiple of six. Bad methodology throws people at stuff, which helps you get a multiple of 0.9. But product with recurring revenue and predictability. So, the more recurring revenue you can do. So, anybody read Tomas Tongas? Anybody know Tomas Tongas? Red Point Ventures? Best, if you have a SaaS company and you're doing recurring revenue SaaS business, read Tomas Tongas' blog. He's brilliant. He's the next Google guy and his metrics are amazing. Giving you all the clues. All the clues to watch. So, there's a little bit of advice for you. If you haven't decided on your startup yet and there's still time to pick, go with product. But you choose. What is Uber? Uber at launch and at scale, Uber is different business models. And this is a great point as well. Thanks for asking it. Because, again, as a founder, we tend to think of our business up here at scale. And we're like, we have six business models. Not at launch you don't. At launch you get one. And you better get it right. Because if you're wrong, you don't have enough runway to get to the second one. So, Uber at scale is a marketplace that also manages transaction fees. Right? So, but from a marketplace perspective, they own both sides of that marketplace. Very much like Airbnb. And at Airbnb at scale they get transaction fees and subscription fees. But at launch it was only a marketplace. So, I have buyers. I have sellers. I'm doing the matching process. And that's the definition of that. But at scale, you have choices. You have a bunch of different business models you can do at scale. But not at launch. At launch you got to sign one. At launch you got to pick. And pick wisely. Yes. Because you don't have much time to fail at that one. And choose the next one. Well, thank you guys for being here.

interviewee_bio: Enrique Godreau III co-founded 9Mile Labs , which provides capital, stage-appropriate guidance and resource
video_title: Enrique Godreau III (Partner, 9Mile Labs) at Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Grind. What gets you going in the mornings? You know, watching snow reports in the winter and watching kayak and tide charts in the summer. Big kayaker, big skier, and being in the northwest, you really want to be taking advantage of both of those opportunities. And so, definitely a big outdoors person, and that makes a big difference. While waking up to, you know, 6.30. Snow on the ground. Snow on the ground and get up early and watch out what's happening on the East Coast. It's nice being kind of three hours earlier, you know, than the rest of the East Coast. So, definitely pay attention to what's happened in New York and understand what's happened there. And they provide a little summary of what happened in Europe and what happened in Asia the day before. And so, all the things matter. Let's talk about that. So, you're from New York. I lived there. I was actually, I'm from Puerto Rico, but raised in New York City. Exactly right. So, tell us a fun story about growing up in New York. And how long did you live there? When did you leave New York? I left New York to go to college in upstate New York. Sure. So, went to, yeah. So, RPI in upstate New York and lived in Rochester, New York as well with Xerox Corporation and moved out of New York in 1985 to move to Palo Alto, California. Oh, there you go. Yeah, so it was in New York. And New York, you know, great place to be. I think, you know, the experiences you build there are just extraordinary. I think there's this Kurt Vonnegut rumored talk that he gave, you know, to the graduating class at MIT that talked about everyone living in New York but leaving after seven years before you get too hard and everybody needing to live in San Francisco but leaving before you get too soft. I happen to have lived seven years in both and feel fairly well-rounded and very happy to now be in Seattle. There you go. That's well put. So, seven years in seven years. So, you've experienced both goes? Yes, absolutely. And did you, why did you pick Palo Alto? Did it pick you or did you pick it? Yeah, I was, you know, fortunate. I started working with the Xerox Corporation out of college in Rochester, New York. I was in the team that led the introduction of CAD CAM systems, computer design systems into Xerox, in the early 80s, helping designers that had designed with vellum and pencil how to use this thing called interactive graphics and intergraph CAD systems for how to design using computers. And that work, we spent about a half a billion dollars in the 80s, which is a significant amount of capital to essentially retool the entire corporation globally, California, Europe, Fuji Xerox. And at the end of the day, we found that it still took five to seven years to design a printer or a copier. And so, the interventions and the value we were looking to get out of our systems deployment hadn't actually been realized. And so, an opportunity opened up at the Palo Alto Research Center to understand the business and the process of design from a different perspective. If we were using CAD CAM as a way of capturing the artifacts, the printers and copiers we were designing, perhaps there was an opportunity to look at design as a different process, as a different activity. And so, in 1985, we were looking at design as a social activity. And so, if you're going to document social activities, what tools do you use? And we were busy using local area video networks, desktop video conferencing systems, really capturing the conversations that take place prior to design activity, prior to actually marking things on paper and tying those things together. So, when you have a question about why a photoreceptor is where it is or why a paper handling system is where it is, we were using audio and video records in 1985 on 12-inch laser displayers over 56 kilobit networks to link designers together that were physically separated from each other. And so, I was in service of understanding this CAD CAM question that I moved from product land to seven years of research at PARC. So, being at PARC is no small thing. What were one of the takeaways that you got out of that experience? I mean, because that's a great experience. It was. I mean, it was really a formative and life-changing experience for me in a couple of ways. One was, you know, I walked into an office in a lab. It's called a small talk lab. I joined the lab that was run by Adele Goldberg. Adele is famous for having given Steve Jobs the demo that gave him the idea for the Lisa, which turned into the Macintosh. And there was a gentleman that took my, that, whose office I took who left in a hurry. He left paper everywhere. He went off to design something called a silicon engine. Some guy named Jim Clark who ultimately, you know, created. He didn't do much. Yeah, exactly. Didn't do an awful lot of things. But what was interesting to me is that amidst all of this technology and 200 researchers, there was also a small group of seven anthropologists. And the idea of anthropologists in a research center really threw me for a loop and really trying to understand why they were there. And the purpose of why they were there was really intended to understand the user interface of photocopiers in the early 80s. And the issue is as copiers got more sophisticated and more capable, you could print on both sides of the page. You could put a staple on the page. You could collate them in different ways. You could actually think about processing documents in different ways. The user interface didn't adapt to that. And it actually stayed pretty complicated. And so the idea of the anthropologists was to actually videotape workers using copiers and trying to understand the gap that existed between what they were trying to accomplish and how they could do that with the user interface and the copiers. And through that process, they began to understand intent. They began to understand the notion of user-centered modeling. And the interface for the copier began to change. And then they became usable and available to others. Well, we didn't really stop there. And so we began to rely on the anthropologists to help us understand the role of technology and its use in a broader context. And even though I was a computer scientist, I really began to understand that we build technology. There are people that use technology, but the thing that matters the most is the boundary between technology and its use. And so since then, I've been very focused, still interested in technology and new capabilities, but most interested in understanding the impact implications of technology. And so that notion, going back to anthropologists, was really key to build that appreciation in a techno-macho world, as John Seeley Brown is so fond of saying. So you're a product guy at heart. I am a technology guy at heart. Technology and use. You want to see how it's used. You want to make sure it's actually useful. Well, I want to understand how it's used. Exactly. Because technology, for technology's sake, is not interesting. It's about building things that actually make a difference and make impact. And I think for a long time, I felt that the Bronze Age, the Industrial Age, all of those ages are characterized by people putting in a little bit of input and getting a lot of value on the other end of the equation. But if you talk to anyone that needs to check their cell phone and their email and their car and their smartphone and voicemail and recharge devices, and we think about the amount of input we're putting into our technology as a function of the amount of value that we're getting out, it doesn't seem that we've quite yet hit that equation where the machines and technology is doing more for us than we're actually putting in. And as long as that's the case, there's lots of opportunity for many of us in the technology industry to continue to be innovating. Yeah, well put. Yeah, well put. So tell me a little bit about growing up. What did your parents do? What did they do when you were five, eight years, ten years old? Yeah, my father was actually in the military, Korean War. And when he came back from the Korean War, he started doing data processing in Puerto Rico. So it was a line printers, data processing. When we moved to New York in 1969, he joined IBM. And he joined IBM on 42nd Street and 3rd Avenue as a part of the World Trade Corporation. So I remember going to visit him at IBM on 42nd Street and had a chance to visit the Watson Research Center in Yorktown Heights, New York. And I am fairly certain that I'm the only person to ever bounce to basketball at Yorktown Heights Research Center coming out of middle school. It's a huge lead time. But we had just enough revenue to allow us to stay in the telecom space that we were successful in penetrating the defense space. And so, today, 100% of U.S. fighters fly with our software. Really? And so, it was this notion of beginning to think about, all right, we have to make a pivot. How do you do it? It took us forever. But it's one of the long, you know, our partner is Lockheed in that effort. And it's one of the longest standing military contracts. And little, tiny company that had fewer than 50 people won software company of the year by Lockheed three years in a row. You know, so it spoke to the quality of the software in the system. But we needed to pivot really quickly. And once we had, then telecom came back as a result of feature phones to smartphones. So, we did well on, you know, coming, we held our breath underwater long enough, got back to telecom, earned the defense market, and that allowed us to then go into the media market. And the media market was now video streaming over high-performance networks. And once again, you just don't want your, you know, you want it continuous and smooth, and our software actually can affect that. And so, we were able to go into a third market. And ultimately, when we put those pieces together, all on the same technical foundation, we were able to prove to the markets, like, look, we've really got the best high availability software out there. And once we did that, we changed the business model to provide an open-source version of it. And it wasn't 30 days later that the company was acquired. Yeah, it was over. Yeah. Yeah. Any questions? If I ask another question, when I'm soliciting the accredited investor, this is an individual who's got a net worth of, let's say, $50 million or $100 million. And I'm asking that individual to invest $50,000 in my small startup. And my question to you is, I understand one of their motivations is the gamble. They want the 10 times return. What are the other factors that would motivate an investor like that to spend $50,000 on me? Because, frankly, that's just crumbs off their table. Why would they get involved in my small business when they can spend all their time investing in IBM, et cetera, et cetera? I think this is where we go back to kind of the fact that we're people. And I think that this is where you really kind of tap into a human dimension as a real driver. Because to your point, it's not about financial return at that point. It's about a different dimension. It's about a couple of things. One is you reach a chord with something that they care deeply about. And so they're committed to seeing a change happen in whatever industry sector. And whether it's $50,000 or $5 million, they would do it anyway because they're just completely focused on making an impact on that sector. And so when you can find that level of strategic alignment, that's a wonderful thing because that's really fairly powerful. A second one is tied to reputation value for that individual and their opportunity to be able to celebrate and laud their investment activity, their investment portfolio. And really, you know, you become one of their investee children that they can talk fondly of and broadly about amongst their peers. And it's an incredible value to them that comes from being able to do that. The financial piece, that pales in comparison at the level of wealth that you were describing. Most of the appeals that we make at that level are not about the financials. They speak to aspirational, strategic, or other catalytic investment purposes, but they speak less to financial return. Having said that, they still have to be sensible financially, but they need to be actually subscribing and appealing to a different dimension for each of the individuals. Well, I want to say this is a very, a great, great chat. I absolutely loved it up here. I hope you guys enjoyed it as well. Thank you very much for being here. My pleasure. My pleasure. Thank you. I failed one, I forgot to thank one person who's very important. Founders Raw, who's shooting a video. Josh here. Thank you for being here and doing this. Founders Raw is a great, Nick Hughes, I don't know if some of you may know him, but he's doing a great job with Nick Founders Raw, and they're doing some really interesting stuff with the videos, so thanks again.

interviewee_bio: Joe Heitzeberg is an entrepreneur, computer scientist, MBA and co-founder and CEO of Crowd Cow . 2019 recipient of the Allen School Alumni Impact Award (UW Department of Computer Science).

interviewee_bio: Joe Wallin is a Partner of Davis Wright Tremaine LLP , which is composed of Startups, Angel & Venture Financings, and M&A.
video_title: Joe Wallin (Davis Wright Tremaine LLP) at Startup Grind Seattle

How do you say no? You're not going to do something. And frequently, you can't necessarily get what you want until you're willing to say no. Yeah. We're not going to do it that way. But how do you say that in a way that's accepted? Yeah. It's not something that's, I don't think, widely taught in our society. And you see all sorts of different styles and a lot of them, you know, are not the product of a study. They're just sort of how people react when they're, you know, under stress or whatnot. So, tell us a story. We love stories. So, tell us a story of a negotiation that went south. That went south? Yeah. Again, you don't have to use names. But tell us, like, a story where, you know, XYZ happened. He called her bad name and whatever. Yeah. Gosh. Yeah. You know, there have been lots of transactions I've been a part of where people have sort of lost it. And, you know, frankly, in all honesty, you know, it takes a little while as a lawyer to sort of lose it, like, well, no, actually, it doesn't. But I mean, I think it takes a while to sort of seasoning before you sort of know sort of what's sort of just sort of the noises people make when they're unhappy or uncomfortable. Sure. And they're not necessarily a reflection of the person. I think a good story, it's not really the deal I've done, but when Gary Kasparov used to play chess against other human beings, he would talk to himself, he would gesticulate, he would mumble, he would do all these things, and people thought he was just trying to intimidate the other player. And they thought he was, you know, kind of a jerk, maybe, because he had this style of play that was, you know, like this. Sure. But then when he sat in front of Big Blue, the computer, he did the same exact thing. And people thought, well, gee, that's just the way he is as a human. It doesn't really have anything to do with who's on the opposite side of the table or necessarily anyone else, right? It's just them. It's just who they are. How they respond. It's how it happens. There's nothing wrong with that. Have you ever been close to working for a startup? Oh, geez. I suppose so. Yeah. Yeah.
