interviewee_bio: John Cook is the co-founder of GeekWire , a technology news site based in Seattle. He covers startups, entrepreneurs and venture capital.
video_title: John Cook (Co-founder Geekwire) at Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Growing. Give us a hint just like some things early on. What was it like growing up in, is it Worcester? Worcester. Worcester. Yeah, Worcester. Not Worcester, Mass, which we got confused quite a bit. Oh boy, yeah. I mean, actually, that is a good place to start because I do trace a lot of kind of my entrepreneurial roots back to my home life, and it does kind of define who I am, certainly. I grew up in a very strong family. My mom was a newspaper reporter for the Akron Beacon Journal, working out of the home, and so, and she was covering courts and cops and elections, and so the discussions around the dinner table often were, you know, who was getting in trouble in the community, you know, who was, you know, I mean, murders, rapes, crime. I mean, Worcester is a town of about 25,000. It has a branch of Ohio State, their Agricultural Research Institute. It used to be the hometown of Rubbermaid, which is no longer there. So, my mom's influence certainly had a big role in who I am today, obviously, the journalism side, and then my dad was an entrepreneur in a family, ran our family business, which was a car and truck dealership, specializing more on the truck side of the business, which had been in the family of 45, 50 years, he ran that, but, you know, what was really interesting with my father was when I was in middle school, just going into high school, he pivoted and sold off the family business and decided he was going to become a financial planner and started a new business, which, you know, that really had a big impact on me. You know, some guy in his 50s that, like, just decides to change it up and move in a new direction, which obviously you have to do as an entrepreneur. So, I grew up with two older brothers, so a pretty male-dominated, male-dominant competitive environment. As you mentioned, I was a soccer player, played through high school and college, and it was a great place to grow up. I love being from Ohio, I wouldn't want to live there in my 30s, 20s, 30s, or 40s, but certainly it was a great place to grow up and a really strong-knit kind of Midwestern roots, and I think that's a big part of who I am today. Do you still play soccer? I do. I do. I've got kind of a hamstring injury right now, but I get out there and I play. I play on a game, a pick-up game on Sunday, and I play on really this cool game that takes place Monday, Wednesday, Fridays in the afternoon, and it's one of the benefits of running your own business. You actually can cut out occasionally to go and get some physical exercise, but it's a cool game because it is the most diverse spot on any given moment, on any given day in Seattle. I guarantee it. It's on the Queen Anne Bowl Field, just midway up from Seattle Pacific there, and I love it because there are Africans and South Americans, Brazilians, Mexicans, and it's just this wild clash of cultures out there, and it's one of the things I love about soccer is that you can take your love of that sport anywhere in the world, and people will embrace you, and you can get along with anybody through the language of soccer. So it's one of the great things about that game, among other things. So what did you want to do when you were growing up? Oh gosh. My earliest memory, I wanted to be a professional color. A color? Yeah. Like, no. What's a professional? With crayons. I wanted to be a professional color. Yeah. Color. That was my first memory. You know, getting into the high school years when you really start to think about that, the college, I didn't really have a real set plan on what I wanted to do. I was a history major in college. I was super into history growing up. I was really into the Civil War. I went to Gettysburg College, obviously, the site of one of the famous battles in Pennsylvania, and just had a real love of history, and I didn't know if I could actually turn that into a job or career or anything, but one thing that my parents did instill in me was to just always follow your passion and just, if you love something, you can make something out of that. And so they were never real strict on any of us, any of the three boys they had, and like, you've got to be a lawyer or a doctor or follow this path. It was more about do what you love and follow your path and you'll be successful. And I mean, I look at my other two brothers, one to the Naval Academy, he's on a pilot with UPS. My other brother's a geologist and is a principal at an environmental engineering company here in town. So, I think it paid off. Yeah. It worked and we all took very different paths. video_title: Julie Sandler (Principal Madrona Venture) At Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Grind. So first of all, what I want to know is how or why or where the psychology, your master's in psychology, not just a B.A., but master's in psychology, what were you going to do with that? I was going to become a psychologist. I had a very strong interest in how the human mind worked, and I think it was my freshman year in college that I took kind of a basic standard psych 101 class, and I got really interested in the psychology of personality, the psychology of culture, the psychology of emotion, and then spent a significant portion of the following years in the lab running experiments with human subjects and learning a ton about cognitive psychology and the psychology of perception. I think one of my favorite experiences was training in a system that they call Facial Action Coding System. Someone's heard of it, yes. There, a fan of Paul Ekman, a couple of Paul Ekman fans here. Effectively, it's training that involves a deep study in the minute contraction and relaxation of small muscles in the face and how that may or may not correspond with the experience of a particular emotion or cognitive state. There's actually a great television show called Lie to Me. Yeah, I see some nodding. It's actually a really fun way to get exposure to the applied use of facts, and it was just a really neat, really neat way to learn how people communicate. And useful, interesting enough. And when did the change from wanting to be a psychiatrist or psychologist of some kind to be, you know, you dabbled in that, you know, the end of the century or the T-street, what kind of turned the corner to be in the business world? Yeah, I wish I could say that there was some thread, some deliberate progression. For me, I think what it boiled down to was my senior year. I was in the midst of, you know, senior year activities and studying and working, you know, full-time and then I had, or sorry, not full-time. It felt like full-time on top of classes plus time in the lab and I got really burnt out with the research. And I was doing a master's degree my senior year too, and it was just too much. And so the last company that was recruiting on campus was a company called Accenture. And they said, well, we don't really care what you learned. Well, we'll bring you into the fold and teach you what you need to know to be what ended up being a product manager for hire. So I went in and out of all these different companies and these sort of classic staff augmentation style goals. You know, someone goes on the journey leave and you jump in for three months. Or, oh, the company doesn't want to invest in an FTE for a particular project, so you jump in. And I would go in and out of companies and launch products. And my clients would be my, you know, peers and bosses and reports, which was sort of a unique experience at that age. And certainly learned a lot, but more than anything got really excited about tech. That was your thing. I was like... Yeah, a few years. Yeah. And then, so T Street got you into the startup world working with, that was Ant, no that was... That was Dave Chappelle, the second most famous Dave Chappelle. Yes. Yes. And so then, then sooner or later, after a couple of states, Amazon... Amazon on the Kindle team, yeah. So tell us just like, how you got to the drama and did the Japanese and stuff. Yeah. Yeah. So, in Kindle, I gave something on the order of, I think it was over two months' notice. And I didn't know what I was going to do, but I gave them a ton of notice so that I could transition the projects and the hats that I was wearing over to the right people. It took time. It took time. Kindle was growing so fast, they couldn't hire enough or hire quickly enough. And I think that's still the case, actually. And so during that two-month period, I just interviewed at startups all over the country. I was looking predominantly at, you know, director of marketing, director of product roles and kind of wanted to delve more deeply into those areas in a sort of a consumer-focused startup. And I went all over and kind of collected a set of offers that I was excited about but was debating between. And I knew the guys at Madrona because not only of my experience at Cheap Street, but because I had reached out to them cold in business school, I think, to serve on some panel that I was organizing. So I figured, you know, if anyone has their ear to the ground and begin to scoop on these startups, it would be these guys. And the conversation around actually joining Madrona and staying in my hometown of Seattle actually spun out from there. Very cool. Very cool. And so you've been there three years. Three years, yeah. And I'm not going to say what you've learned three years because I've learned a lot. But what was the thing you learned that was the most surprising that you were like, wow, I didn't think I didn't even know this thing. What is there something that's -- it wasn't like just something you would know, oh, yeah, you have to be trying to fix things or whatever. But what was the thing that surprised you? Well, I think what surprised me the most is that I -- to a large extent, I still feel like a student of psychology. You know, with these early -- Not in that sense, not clinical psychology. But, you know, to a large extent, you know, in these very early stages, the success of a company comes down, one, to the people that you have at the table. And then, two, how they interact, how they work together, how they complement each other, how they don't complement each other. And, you know, there's a great -- a great book that was written by a former professor of mine in business school. The book's called Founder's Dilemmas. Some nodding heads again. Written by Noah Monserman. And he's -- for nine years, he spent countless hours with hundreds of founders, founding teams, successful teams, not as successful teams. Yeah, so we've assessed, you know, creating a science type of evaluation for the company, that's not really how it works. You have to be much more driven by, especially in the early stages, by what you learn from the people you talk to, who can not only give you background, but can also be critical of the opportunity as presented to them. Let's go back to that question. If you were fired tomorrow, what would you want to do? Well, when I was a kid, and I got asked that question, no one asked me, like, yeah. But they'd say, what do you want to do when you grow up? Sure, yeah. I'd say, well, I want to teach. I love to teach. And, yeah, well, one of the great things about this job is, you know, we are so tied to the University of Washington. I'd say over a dozen or so of the companies that we've invested in. So. Yeah. Are, I think, largely IT spun out of the University of Washington and then turned to a commercial endeavor. Yeah. And, right now, I, along with a couple of colleagues of mine, I'm on adjunct faculty at the UW and get to teach. And, you know, I think if I weren't doing this, that would be something I want to continue doing. But, you know, to tell you the truth, I think when I first joined Adrona, one of the things that was most exciting to me was the opportunity to get inspired by entrepreneurs and potentially join an early stage startup or even start one. I'm so happy right now and so just loving this right now that it's not top of mind at all. But, if I had to take a stab at answering that question, that's probably more of you. Good. Good. What do you think the difference between raising money here and raising money in the valley is? Um. In preface with both ANGEL and VC, what do you think the difference is between the two cities, two areas? Yeah. There's two questions. So, I think the answer for ANGEL and VC might be slightly different. So, in Seattle, I'll start with ANGEL. There is not a robust network of ANGEL investors in Seattle. There are some amazing groups. I think those of you that are looking to raise ANGEL funding. You know, Alliance of ANGELs. Um. Uh. Uh. Puget Sound. Uh. Venture. Venture Club. Serif. Um. Koretsu Forum. Um. Xeno Society. A lot of really great ANGEL funds. Um. Like, Founders Co-op. I think, uh, Seattle Angel Conference, by the way, also has one as well. Um. Founders Co-op is an amazing ANGEL group. There's a lot of very smart, very talented, um, very hands-on, great partnership or real-time. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Uh, questions. So, feel free to, go ahead. So, uh, what are the analytical metrics and ratios that you really look at when you analyze companies? Hold on. The question is... What are the analytical... Oh. What are the analytical questions, or what are the analytical metrics that you watch when you're investing, looking at when you're investing? Um. Well, I'll give you the... Go first. The answer is, it depends on the business. Um. But, but there's certain things that are obvious, and maybe less obvious, if you haven't been, um, thinking about this on that on a daily basis. So, one, I think, customer reaction and any type of quantifiable reaction from your customer, that this is something that is wanted, that they are clamoring for, they are coming back back for, again and again. You know, I think, key metrics that speak to that are, they should be pretty intuitive. Uh, what are the kinds of metrics that you're early saying on and the kinds of customers you're actually bringing? Uh, if you're a consumer company, are you collecting an email address? Do they get some kind of free version of a product? Do they get a paid version of the product? Understanding that funnel and what you're paying for each customer along each rung of that funnel? So imperative. Build for that from the get-go. And with that, the other question that VCs are trying to answer is, okay, well if you're paying X dollars to acquire one customer, how much value? How much value? In that customer. How much value you're extracting from that customer? Whether it's value in the first 12 months, the first two years, whether it's value in a lifetime. That metric is classically called, you know, LTV, lifetime value, or some people use lifetime revenue, but I think lifetime value is preferred because it considers the gross origins that are going to be driving your business both in the short and mid-term. So understanding how quickly you achieve payback on that acquisition, whether it's a few months, whether it's nine months, whether it's a year. That kind of stuff matters a lot. So building for that from the get-go, being able to report on that is really useful. You know, for SaaS companies, there's also, I mean, depending on what business you're building, there's a set of metrics that are going to matter and be key drivers, but it really, really just totally depends on the nature of what you're building, what your business model is driven by. Next question. What does early stage mean for you? Early stage is a great question. What's the earliest stage you're looking for? Earliest. The earliest, earliest stage. You know, we have invested in companies so early that it was just one or two people who were very, very smart and very capable, who had a great idea and what we believe to be the very clear wherewithal to execute on a particular opportunity against that idea. I'm trying to think of a good example. Have any of you heard of the company Placed? Yeah. So, Placed is a company here in Seattle led by a phenomenal CEO by the name of David Shin, who had worked, in fact, in the past with a number of companies or in a number of companies that we had invested in. Brilliant guy, super smart. I think we did a note in a very early seed stage of that company when it was just him physically incubated at Madrona. Just, you know, hacking away. And that was, you know, a very, very early stage bet on a market opportunity, on a vision for a product, but most importantly, on a person. Maybe one or two people. Classically, to your question, you know, what is early stage? You know, for us, the sweet spot is seed or series A. So, the definitions for those rounds are, you know, there's broad ranges and in fact they're getting, you know, higher in terms of total raise size, especially over the past year or two. But if you look at sort of the classic seed or series A round, that's right where our sweet spot is. And again, you know, we, we will, we are very good about following on. Lots of investors want to continue participating as the company raises more capital in order to preserve, you know, an ownership stake in the company. We have a very strong track record of continuing to follow on as the company grows in each round. That's not always the case with venture firms that you might work with. We are that way. That being said, there are times that we do make initial investments in later stage companies. Series B, in really rare instances, even Series C, not really in a sweet spot for us, not really part of what our, our general focus is. But, but it happens and when we invest, we believe a lot in, in the opportunity and we, we follow along. And, and that's been something that has been great to, great to experience because you get to see the evolution of that company over time as a very active partner for the entrepreneur. Yeah, I'm interested to see what the, the data says, like how do you follow along and that's what you guys have made after this is a kind of initial round. Because you guys always seem like you're always in the second round. Yeah, we've got a, yeah, a very strong track record with that. I don't know, I don't know the numbers, but. So the question is, some advice on raising the right amount of capital, you know, is it too much or is it too little? So, one of the best pieces of advice that I can give is to work backwards to that number. So think about what is it that you want to accomplish in the next 18 months, say, or however long it takes to achieve what you feel is a very meaningful milestone for your company. And then think, okay, what do I need to do to get there? Who do I need to hire? What do I need to invest in from a G&A standpoint? What do I need to spend from a marketing standpoint? What are the costs to, say, working capital, if you have working capital in your business? What are the costs there? And what will I need to achieve some particular goal at the end of that 18 to 24 months? Is that goal a product that is out there that has a growth rate of X? Is it, gosh, we're just going to get our first prototype done because we're a hardware business and really got to prove that the technology works. Figure out what the milestone is and then think about who you have to hire, what you have to spend, whether it be G&A, marketing, whatever it is, to get to where you want to go. And then think, okay, how much should I be raising now to achieve that? It is not a reasonable amount given what I see as a norm for fundraisers at a company at my stage. If the answer is, I need to raise $100 million, more often than not, that's probably not the right kind of math. But the fact of the matter is if you're driven by the numbers and the elements that you see as the entrepreneur as being required to achieve a particular goal and you're clear with the investor about what that goal is. One, you have a very productive conversation around, hey, is this reasonable? Two, you can have a discussion around, is this the right goal to be setting? And three, you have a way to demonstrate that you are being very thoughtful about how you want to grow this business at minimum in the near term. Next five years, what kind of technology do you want to invest in? In the next five years, what kind of technology do you want to invest in? Oh, that's a big question. Five years. Two years. You know, I would love to see more e-commerce come out of Seattle. We have so much DNA here. It is crazy if you think about it. The obvious suspects, you know, a la Amazon, etc. But you've got Starbucks and Costco and Expedia. You've got all these new large companies like Zulily. It's unbelievable that you don't see just a ton of smart, social-driven commerce, unique e-commerce models coming out and exploding given the sheer talent that we have here. From an acquisition standpoint, there might be a way around that. And I think that's a theme that you see with e-com companies coming out of here and elsewhere that have been successful in the age of Amazon. I would love to see more of that. We've got a certain handful of e-commerce companies too. Indochino is one, by the way. If you want to buy a custom-fitted suit, it's very smart about fitting you and you want to find very stylish attire men. Indochino is the way to go. Yeah. Um, they're in the white shirt. What are your thoughts on Bitcoin and blockchain-based technology? So, the question is, what's your thoughts on Bitcoin and blockchain technology? That is a category I've spent very little time in. I've run at a very high level and certainly we've had discussions in the partnership about it. I think it's a very important trend. I think it's one of the most polarizing topics of discussion right now for a lot of reasons. But, you know, it's really interesting to see the kinds of companies that are coming out and saying, "I'm accepting Bitcoin now." You can pay for parking with Bitcoin. It's unbelievable. So, I mean, I am excited about that trend. I'm not actively looking at it, but there are a lot of folks at my firm who can speak much more smartly than I can about why it's interesting and why we think it's one that could be right for investment in the short term. What about the cannabis business? Oh, we're raising a side fund for that now. It's, you know, if it were, I think it's going to be huge. Yeah. Yeah, it's going to be funny. Okay. Kind of as a follow-up to that, you mentioned a lot of Majora having local interests. He could grab some from the city or from the state and tell him these are the things we need to develop startups in the area. What would you tell them? Great question. Yeah. Would you, can you repeat that? I could try. So, if you were to talk to somebody at the city level or state level about opportunities for Seattle to take action to improve the success of the startup community, what would they be? Yeah. There are so many. I think the biggest challenge facing our community right now is the amount of talent that there is demand for. We've got an incredible community for talent, but it cannot keep pace with the demand that is coming from the fast-growing companies that are here. And that is especially the case for engineering talent. And, you know, there's two ways to think about that from, you know, sort of a high level. One, there's homegrown talent. And two, there's important talent. And you have a whole startup grind on homegrown talent and, you know, have a conversation with Ed Lazowska at the University of Washington Department of Computer Science and Engineering and talk about the, talk with them about the demand for computer science degrees and the amount of spots that they have to offer students who are completely qualified, extremely talented, and wanting to fill those spots. I think it's, you know, 4X the amount of demand is they have to supply of spots. But the fact is, even if computer science were taught in every single classroom at an early age and UW was able to accommodate the demand and all of our, you know, home-oriented institutions could create a theater of this kind of talent, but we still, we still would not be able to keep pace with the demand that there is, particularly for technical talents. You have to be able to bring it in. I like what I'm seeing in Massachusetts in terms of what they're doing with their non-compete agreements, allowing more flexibility, more fluidity, as talented engineers want to move from state to state, from company to company. I think there's also real conversations that need to be had, that need to be had around the policy around issuing special H-1 visas. That threshold needs to be raised. We need to be able to import from outside the United States too. So, it's extremely enriching to companies, to communities. That threshold's too low right now, and it's a hindrance. So, I think that there are some real policy discussions that can and hopefully will be had, and that's sort of the top of mind reaction to what kinds of conversations would be important with those kinds of people who have that kind of influence. So, what kind of due diligence do you do on a technical basis? You've talked about teams and markets and stuff like that. Do you guys dig into the kind of technologies that they're using, the architecture, or any of that kind of stuff? The question is what kind of due diligence do you on the technology side? So, digging into the code and the theory, how much do you guys do that? Yeah, absolutely. Yeah, that's critical and really important. Which means the same, it's critical. And really important, which means it's super critical. Yeah, yeah, I'll stop. So, we have on our team a number of people who have different areas of expertise and are able to contribute uniquely to any number of forms of technical diligence. We've got a couple of venture partners at Madrona who are, interestingly enough, both have ties to the University of Washington. And Dan Weld, who is a professor in the Computer Science and Engineering Department. Oren Enzioni, who I think he is, I think he's either there currently or maintains a position there, but he's currently the head of the Allen Institute of Artificial Intelligence. Extremely smart guy, who also has founded a number of companies that we've invested in as well. Those are two of the resources that we've used. I think one of the critical forms of value that you can extract from a venture firm is the network that you get access to. So, for us, having a competency in developing a strong technical network is important for us, both for what we offer our companies as well as how we understand opportunities. So, we have a number of entrepreneurs, a number of computer scientists, a number of very smart, technical-minded folks who we reach out to pretty regularly to health diligence. What we, you know, for those of us that aren't, you know, computer scientists or engineers, or data scientists, what we aren't able to do for ourselves, for our own deals that we lead. Go ahead.

interviewee_bio: As CEO of LiquidPlanner , it is Liz Pearce’s overall responsibility for accelerating the company’s growth and position in the enterprise software market.
video_title: Liz Pearce (LiquidPlanner) at Startup Grind Seattle

As an executive assistant. That's not you to choose to do that. Well, that was my first place as a talent. Yeah. You just wanted a job. I needed to make money. Yes. I had a degree in telecommunications, if you remember. This was 2001. 2001 was coincidentally the year the telecommunications industry fell out. I was like, well, I would really like this to work in the marketing department. Can you hook that up for me? And so he did, and I did get a job in the marketing department. There you go. Yeah. So you don't have to... I haven't really regretted that decision. You don't have to turn into this normal path and still get in marketing by entering the... Yeah, it was like the proverbial foot in the door. Yeah. So Google, your employee, less than, what, five years or something there? Yeah, yeah. It was crazy, I'm sure. Yeah. What did you like about that except being at Google? What were the things like, wow, this is really cool, this stuff? And so everyone kind of knew that there was a lot of potential there and like Google was going to be big. Everyone knew by then Google was going to be big. And so when I got the chance to move to Google, I thought, well, you would have to be an idiot not to do this. It was mostly, it was about print at that time. It was like advertising. That's funny. Yeah, like little handouts for sale. Yeah, because, you know, all of our marketing was to advertisers in like we would do like little inserts in magazines and stuff. I mean, not little, they were like high volume advertising. But it was still like a lot of sell sheets because we were supporting the enterprise sales folks in the AdWords unit and, you know, Google search enterprise appliance and all those things. And so it was a lot of print projects which have a lot of steps in production, you know, you have to copy and the design to the printer and the vendor and the specs and the cost and the, everybody has to know everything about everything. And it did create a lot of empathy for my future customers over the years. So let's talk about being a woman and technology. Oh, let's do it. Let's do it. So, knowing that, how much has it changed. I have never in this room right now. Knowing that, how much do you think has changed over the last, say, four or five years? Can you talk a little bit about how not 100% of your customers are going to love your product? And so you get that customer feedback, which is like, oh, I didn't like this, that or the other, and the impact of that. Keep going. Keep going. No, I think, I mean, early on in that product process, we do a lot early on on feedback and prototyping, putting stuff in people's hands. We spend a lot of time trying to understand, without our product, how do they take pictures? How do they share pictures? Like the best products, you really understand the core problems, agnostic of your solution. It's very hard because you already have a solution in your head. And so we spend a lot of time trying to understand their core needs, particularly around mobile photography. So we kind of map a whole journey. So we feel like we pretty much understand now like what people are doing, why they're doing it, how they're doing it. So now we can build better products. But I think, yeah, everybody wants people to think your product's amazing. I think early on you have to be very honest. Like is it amazing? Is it not amazing? And you have to keep tinkering with it until it is amazing. And you sometimes have to hold that back to go get press or give it to certain people because it's not ready. Particularly with hardware, like the worst in the world is a bad review. You are screwed. You are screwed. Some big tech person gives you a three star, two and a half star. It's very hard to come back from that because it costs so much money to go make a new version. You have to be very careful about that and really give it to the right people and make sure they're curating and make sure the product is really good. But feedback is super important. It's just hard to hear. One more question. So have you had to deal with people pegging you as a hardware or software company and has the development of the brand and the culture around the moment, does that kind of mitigate that? Usually the hardware or software thing only comes when you're raising money. That's usually when it's labeled like your hardware software. Which one are you? Which one are you? Yeah. I think when you're raising money, there are categories you're in. It's very important to identify which category you're in. We kind of use a simple matrix to figure out how is the market competing today and how are you going to differentiate from that market? Because you need to own a quadrant that's unique, right? And so it's very clear about that. But I think a lot of investors, they struggle from, they don't understand brand very well. Like a lot of them are function-based. Usually it's finance-based. Like just look at the phone they're using. Look at the things they do. Like and I think that is a struggle. Like if you're trying to curate brand, you're trying, it's hard. It's a much smaller list of investors that believe in that. And most venture has not been good at brand. Most brand is actually from private equity. Like they're very good at brand, understanding the value of brand. But you have to be a $10 million company doing profit so they even invest, right? So you've got to get pretty far to that point. So I think that's when it matters. But I think what a lot of them miss is who cares if it's hardware or software? It's about customers. How much do customers spend? How many do you have? How do you get another one? Whether they buy a software thing from you, a hardware thing, I think is irrelevant. As long as you're able to show you have customers who love your product, who buy more stuff. And I think in the end that's all that matters. You have to just find investors that believe that. So if someone's already trying to label your hardware or software, I wouldn't even worry about it. You're either in the wrong setup or you're thinking too much about it. It's like, yeah, we're about getting customers. You see how many we've got to get, how much we think they're going to spend. Here's the suite of products we have that we think they're going to use and buy. One more question and then I've got to get some shirts away to some people. All the way in the back. I'm not personally curious about what makes great design. What makes great design? Yeah. I think the best design is simple because it solved the core need. Like it's not necessarily about visual. Some of the best design is very crude and visual. But it does one thing really, really well. And I think the best designers are able to really solve, you know, design problems. To approach it from a design problem. To understand is this an information architecture problem? Is this a layout problem? Is this a visual problem? And then nail the essence of that. They're not afraid to use words versus objects. Like I think, and I think the best designs just really nail that core thing and do it really, really well. They make it simple. It's intuitive to use. You get it. And you like it. And it makes you feel good. And so I think that's how to judge it. Like if you play with something or use it and you're like, I like this. I get it. I connect with it. I think it's a good design. Something. Close. Let me get, I got to get these. So here's the XL. Yeah, there's an XL. Thanks, buddy. Thanks. Who do you see as your competitors? For a moment, who we see the competitors are? We haven't found anybody turns the phone and thinks of the phone as a camera. Like we found a bunch of objects. So someone makes a lens, someone makes a case, someone makes individual parts. But we think one of the theses is we can put a family of products together to turn the phone collectively into a better camera. We think no one's doing that. If you just walk on the Apple store, if you even look at the accessories that in the first product is all about phone accessories. If you look at the accessories people would use, none of them are about, oh, you love to take pictures? Let me get that for you. They're all about some function of battery or power protection. So that's one of our theses. We can build a brand that puts a family of products together. Yeah, food photography people, it's delicious. It makes me hungry every day. But yeah. Yeah. Do you think Samsung or iPhone or anybody or Apple would think that's making your advanced technology? For sure. So would that make your advanced technology? While you make, while your products are phone accessories, you are high risk. Our current product line is phone accessories. If I was, the whole product line we were going to make was phone, just phone accessories, we would have zero investors. So I think you, it's all about starting somewhere and driving a business and getting customers. And we think moment and the brand and how we go reach customers is the same. We're just going to build a whole lot of products. The first set just happened to be phone accessories. So under a chair, someone's chair, there is a sticky note that says winner. And you're going to win and Mark's going to explain. This one? That's it. There you go. Tony's a winner. So Mark, tell them what they want. They want some lenses. Some lenses. Wow. Put that on me. All right, man. Thanks, man. Thanks, man. That'll help you all night. You should go to the bar with a winner on your chest. That's a good idea. You should. Don't take that off. Don't take that off. Get a picture of that. First of all, thank you very much, Mark, for being here. Yeah, it was fun. Thanks for having me. Appreciate it. And again, thank you all for being here because it would be a whole lot, not much fun if it was just us two. The two of us with our fireplace. Exactly. It'd be warm. So again, thank you all for coming. Thank you all the guys in the green shirts for ambassadors for help making this much easier to run and for all of us to enjoy it better. And thank you for all the sponsors for helping and assisting over the years and tonight. And thank you. Be safe. Have a great week and see you next month.

interviewee_bio: Britta Jacobs is the COO of Zealyst .
video_title: Martina Welke Britta Jacobs Co Founders (Zealyst) at Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: So without any further ado, I'd like to welcome the co-founders of Z-List. Where is Jay Z? Yeah, kind of a Beyonce introduction. Are we good, Greg, on the sound? Okay. Okay, that's great. So, oh, I'm glad you got the wine. Awesome. It's a fireside chat. It's a fireside chat. Well, again, for those of you who haven't been to Start Grind, this is truly a conversation between three people. So it is really just me asking questions from an entrepreneur standpoint. And some of these questions, I've been sent via email from some people in the audience. So, you know, again, feel free to ask those questions. Hold those to the end. But it really is a conversation between the three of us. So, again, thank you for being here. We're happy to be here. We're happy to be here. I'm sorry you only have one mic, but actually it's probably better that you, here. You got lots of core. We're really good at sharing. We've practiced over the years. Okay. Okay. Good. So, tell us just some things. I don't really want to, you know, ask questions like, where'd you grow up? That's not really that important. It's more like, why'd you do that? So, tell us just in a really short kind of synopsis why you started. I mean, you're young. You weren't in the corporate world for 10 years. You were in the corporate world for 10 months, maybe. So, tell us why it started. First, how did you guys meet and then how did it start? Go for it. So, I would say our naivete was a great asset in the beginning because we had no idea what we're getting into. And Britta and I met over six years ago now when we were working at a nonprofit. And it was our first, both of our first sort of real jobs out of college. And we became fast friends there. And afterward moved on to startups separately, different startups. And that was really where we caught the bug. And about a year and a half into that stage, I went to Vermont to visit my younger sister. And we'd been, Britta and I had been talking about ideas we wanted to work on. It was kind of a fantasy. I think around that time we saw Social Network. I'm like, oh yeah, that's interesting. We could do that. But I was in Vermont and visited the Ben and Jerry's factory and heard the story of Ben and Jerry. And the story is basically that they were two friends in their mid-twenties who had jobs, had things going on, but wanted to do something together and wanted to build something together. And they liked bagels and ice cream. And bagel makers were too expensive. And it was as simple as that. They started making ice cream. And it clicked in that moment. But, you know, we didn't need to have the perfect idea. We didn't need to have everything figured out. We just needed to start building. And so Britta picked me up from the airport. And I said, I'm dropping off my bags and we're going to figure it out today. And we went for a walk. And we walked 14 miles that day around Seattle and had the idea we wanted to work on by the time we got back. And that truly is, it sounds kind of ridiculous, but that truly is how we started. We just one day decided we need to start working on this. And we didn't quit our jobs right away. So we took our time with that. I would just add that the economic state of the world at that time when we took that walk also really -- Yeah, because what year was that? That would be 2009. 2009. 10. We met in 2008. Yeah. We took the walk in 2010. But there was mad recession going on. There were not a whole lot of really exciting opportunities for folks like us with liberal arts backgrounds and who wanted to do something really cool and really great right now. So we made it for ourselves. Sure. And I think that's a big part of our story was that while the recession was really crummy in a lot of ways, I think it really laid -- created the space for us to -- at least for me to feel like it was the right thing for me to do with my time and my energy and really focus on building something from scratch. Sure. So, Britta, why don't you answer this? So, what has been your biggest learning thing in the last three years? And I'm just talking about the last three years because there was tons of stuff, I'm sure, early on. But in the last three years, what has Britta learned? Like, holy crap. This, I just didn't think I would get through this and I learned X, Y, Z. What was -- can you name one thing that was really kind of a big deal for you? I hope you don't see this as a cop-out. But it's to be amazed every day. And to be humbled every day. And to learn something new every day. Because I think the last three years have brought so much change and so much growth and so many opportunities for us to meet new people and to build new things and to stretch the way we think and imagine the impact that we can have on our world. And I think the biggest thing that I've learned is not to get attached to any one vision, to any one plan, but really to stay attached to the emotion behind it and not get too bogged down in the execution of, oh, it's day one, day 20. I am sure I will be right here. Because by day 20, it will be day one again. And that keeps it really exciting. And it took me a while to wrap my mind around that. But it's an interesting path, an interesting life that we have chosen. Not necessarily an easy one, but it is very interesting. Yeah. Yeah. Well, that's what startups are about, right? Yes. Never a dull moment. So, Martina, for you, a similar question. What have you learned on how to manage the business? Like, as a business, what have you learned? Like, what's been your thing? Like, I learned this X in managing the business. So I'm kind of trying to get more on the business side of things from you. Well, I think in a similar vein, I would say one of the mistakes I learned from early on was that we thought things would happen the way we expected them to. And we resisted some evidence and some indications that that wasn't the case for too long. And we could have adapted and grown more quickly had we not had that resistance. And so now, especially now that our team is growing and there are more ideas, there's more energy in the mix, I try really, really hard to maintain an open mind and put my ego aside. Because every day I am wrong about a lot of things. And I just have had to learn to embrace that rather than fight it. And also to know the moments when I do need to hold my ground. And I think that's the balance. You know, I'm continuing to always refine and continue to learn. But, you know, where do I need to stay true to whatever that north is and keep the team focused on that? But also knowing that a lot of the time my assumptions are going to be wrong and will be better and stronger for me and then by extension the team and kind of our philosophy, being able to recognize that and learn from that. And who has helped you in your opinion the most? Oh, just one person? Well, so there are a set of mentors. One is not here tonight, but I will say his name because he's been probably the one who's pushed us the most. So one of our advisors, Jigash Avalani, is just fantastic. And I would say he called us on BS from day one and really, really pushed us and continues to and really cuts to the core in a way that very few people in my life do. So when I reach any kind of impasse, I usually call Jigash not for comfort and love because that's not what he gets me. You're not going to get any of that. But for some straight talk. Yeah. And have you or are you mentoring anyone else? Either one of you guys mentoring anyone else? We took sort of a developed policy pretty early on of take the meeting. And that happened because in our early days we were really starry-eyed. And we reached out to a ton of people. And just about everybody said, sure, I'll sit down with you for a cup of coffee and I will listen to your ideas and I will ask you tough questions. And so that is something that is really important to me to give back. Especially when I encounter young women founders or women who want to start a business with their best friend like we did. I've got a lot of experience with that and I think it's one of the best things I've done in my life and I want to encourage other people to do it. Sure. And I think when we were starting out knowing that we weren't the only ones who ever encountered this problem or that problem or realized our monetization strategy needed to change or anything else. Knowing that we weren't the only people who had ever been in that situation was a huge comfort to me. So I try to spread that out as much as I can. Since we've gotten a little farther away from our early days, it's not, you know, our calendars are more packed. But I still try to take the meeting. If people will reach out to me, I still very much try to talk to them and listen to them and answer their questions and ask them tough questions and follow up. A few months later, hey, are you still doing it? Because sometimes they aren't. Sometimes they aren't. Sometimes, you know, there are two women I know who are trying to start a business and I follow up with them every quarter and I ask, are you still doing it? And they say, well, I'm so glad you wrote because I've been working on this problem and I would love to hear your feedback on this. And then, you know, those little pushes were so helpful for us in the beginning. I very much tried to pay that forward. Yeah. So let's talk about partners, best friends, co-founders. It's hard. I mean, it's hard. We're tight. Yeah, exactly. Yeah. Obviously. So let's go. What's the last argument you had? Oh. Do you remember? Where this was from? On our way here. I said, no, it's at the Wells Fargo building. No. No. Okay. So let me go. What was the last meaningful argument you had? And how was it, you know, frame it for us and then how was it solved? Sure. So I would actually say, in all honesty, and this isn't to gloss over kind of, not to share your code or anything, but I would say we rarely argue. However, we do have moments of high tension and difficult communication because neither of us are really kind of the shout, you know, battle it out kind of people. So our arguments don't look like what you might expect an argument to look like. But the last big one was not too long ago, just a few weeks ago. We had a fairly big shift in the direction for the company. And not, I mean, it's all worked out really well. But I think whenever those moments happen where there's a big change that happens pretty suddenly. Sure. There's a lot of emotion. There's a lot of different perspectives on how that decision's made. And we've made a commitment since the very beginning, before we even had any team around us, that we needed to address those moments of tension head on before they became insidious and much, much bigger than they needed to be. So let me just stop right there. So learning, this is a learning event. That is a learning thing right there. When you, co-founders, when you have an issue, fix it immediately. Because it boils over or boils too long, it gets really ugly. I mean, I can attest to that many times. So that is, I just wanted to say that because it's really important that everyone understands that. And it's really hard. It's really, really hard to do. In this particular instance, I mean, we've gotten it down to a pretty good rhythm now. But I waited probably 24 hours longer than I should have to bring up a conversation of something that was bothering me that it just got bigger than it needed to be in that 24 hours. And we ended up getting through it pretty quickly. But whenever you let that emotion build and whenever you let that fester, it just, it's so much, it makes it so much more difficult than it needs to be. And so, you know, we really trust each other and know that, although Britta just told me she has secret fears about me just quitting the company publicly. So I'm not going to, I'm not going to quit tonight if that's. But we know, we know that's not going to happen. We trust one another to get through those moments. And so it makes it easier to approach the difficult moments. Did you structure anything when you, when you started out? Like, did you, did you talk about like, hey, when we have a big decision, we should do X? Did that, was that? Yeah, to some degree. I wouldn't say we did as much as we should have, but we actually had a third co-founder when we started as well. And so amongst the three of us, we made kind of a charter and a plan for communication. And actually it served us very well when our third co-founder left, because we actually had a plan for what would happen in the event of one of us bowing out. And it happened really gracefully and we're all still friends. And now that it's the two of us, I think we've continued to refine that. And actually new challenges have come up to surprise us along the way. So every time we think we've got it and we're totally on top of it, you know, some surprise comes along that knocks us down a little bit again. And, you know, just recently we actually had to start scheduling one-on-ones with each other. We've never done that before, but it's got to the point where that wasn't happening naturally anymore. We used to just spend all our time together and we still do, but usually now there are other people around, there's activity, you know, we don't get as much one-on-one time. And so we've actually started putting that on the calendar. And that's just within the last couple of months. So it's always evolving the tactics and strategies that we employ. But fundamentally it's about just making sure that we're communicating both about the business and about what's going on personally because that very much impacts how we make decisions and the energy we bring to our work. And so I know some of the inner stories, right? So tell us, the audience, what made you determine the big pivot you did two years ago or so? Tell us. There were two biggins. Yeah, I know. The first biggin. The first biggin. Yeah. Tell us how that came about. And I mean, yeah, just tell us that. I think the first big pivot was brought on by opportunity. And the second big pivot was more desperation. Realizing what we were doing wasn't working. And so for those of you who aren't familiar with what Z-List is or what Z-List was, on that long walk, Martina and I talked about what we loved and what we did well and where we saw a market opportunity. And it all kept coming back to people and introductions. Really good at introducing people who wouldn't have met but needed to meet and needed the right push to have the conversation to take their relationship into the next level. Whether it was, you know, two people who wanted to run a marathon together and never knew that that other person also had that interest. Or, you know, we had a marriage result from one of our first events. That's right. The fourth event. They got married. And so we were using games and data analysis to connect people in event settings that we called huddles. And we had a really great group of people who came to those huddles. A handful of them are here today, which is wonderful. And those members of Z-List in the early phase were really devoted and loyal and invited us to bring what we had developed, this strategic matching and games to help facilitate conversation into the organizations where they volunteered and worked. And so it was through our members that we were invited to work with the University of Washington and a handful of nonprofits. And when we were invited to work with Amazon, we realized, oh, shoot, this is a really big opportunity. And we had been going into doing these events before thinking, we've seen startup spill because they get distracted. We don't want to get distracted. So we'll do this because these members are really important to us. Just this one time. Just this one time. Yeah. Just this one time. And then we saw our games being played by Amazonians and saw the connections being made and saw this really beautiful humanity happening because of what we built and realized this is not a distraction. This is a direction. And so that's what brought on the first pivot. Yeah. And was there 100% agreement between the two of you? I think it took time for both of us to fully embrace it because there was some warning in... I mean, our original vision was that we wanted to take a consumer route and reach a really broad spectrum of people all over and have these huddles happening in cities and a very kind of grassroots thing. And for different, maybe for different reasons, but in the same vein, we both just went through a period of letting go of that. But we were both on board with... By the time the Amazon huddle came around, we both knew that was the best direction for the business. And then there was just sort of the transition to work through and to get the team at that time on board with a new direction. But I don't think there was any fundamental disagreement that that was the way we should go. I always joke that opportunity knocked and knocked and knocked and then banged on the door by the way. Okay. So by that time we were ready for it. Yeah. Yeah. And so, again, describe the second big one. I think that the second pivot, and I've had a handful of conversations with other founders about the really dark time that precedes a pivot. And, you know, at first it's a few things that aren't working out that someone won't call you back or they say, they keep saying, they keep blowing you off. And then more of that happens where you can't get the client deals and your conversations go cold. And then I started blaming everybody else. Oh, well, they just don't know. They're not forward thinking enough to realize that... This is the greatest thing since the wheel. Exactly. Yeah. And you know what? It's my job to think that it's the greatest thing since the wheel. And I believe that what we've built is the greatest thing since the wheel. But I wasn't listening to everybody else and I wasn't experiencing the rest of the world. And so it was a while of being in that space before we realized or even talked about in earnest, what if we cut out the live piece? What if we did that? And I think we had to get pretty far down to the bottom before that was something that we were really willing to consider because it was such a big part of our initial vision. Yeah. Because that's what it was. You can talk about the retreat. Yeah. Well, I was just going to add to that that I think what compounded that even more than just kind of an attachment to the vision was there was a period of time where it was pretty glamorous and exciting. I mean, we started flying around the country doing these things for corporations. We were making a decent amount of money and it was exciting and it was really cool to see it take on that kind of life. But yeah, we went on a retreat in late 2013 and had this, we just reconciled that it wasn't what we actually wanted to create and it wasn't, there were problems with what clients were saying and sort of what we wanted, you know, it didn't quite fit together. And it wasn't ultimately the business we wanted to create. It was a very different direction, a very different set of possibilities facing us that didn't excite us anymore. And so it took a while and again, I just think back to, oh, I wish I would have realized it sooner, but it is what it is and I think you have to learn in the way that you experience it. But in retrospect, I think that that glamorous allure, we got caught up in that and it masked a lot of the things that weren't working. Yeah, sure. It was sexy for a while. Yeah. Yeah. So talk about the experience of that period of time, not having money and not knowing where you're going to get money. Yeah. So that was tough. I'd say one of the things that was crazy about that time is we suddenly realized there really are no rules because we got to this point where everything, every kind of logical assumption would be just, just give up. You know, this is, this is the end. Come on, just accept it. And that's actually, so we went to one of our board members' cabins on Whidbey Island to kind of have that conversation. We locked ourselves in a cabin and said, is this the end basically? And reassessed everything that we'd experienced that we're feeling and came to a few interesting conclusions. And one was that we were hearing that clients really loved what we were doing but wanted something more scalable and sustainable. And we, and that's one of the things we resisted because it didn't quite fit into the paradigm that we imagined. Um, and so we started talking about what, what would that look like? If we just let go of our own resistance to that, what would that look like? We started for the first time imagining that. Um, and, and that's what led to what Zealist is today. Um, and the other thing that happened was we were really honest about, with each other, about how unhappy we were. We were both really unhappy at that time. And Britta said at one point, you know, I'd really like to get married eventually. She'd been with her boyfriend at the time for six or seven years. And I said, yeah, I'd really like to go on a date. Like, we'd love to have a life at some point. Marriage. You know. And so, um, we were honest about things that fundamentally weren't working on the business side and also weren't working in our lives. And, um, speaking that aloud to one another was kind of scary. Because I think both of us had been afraid to admit that, um, we didn't want to seem weak. We didn't want to seem like our commitment was lacking. But once we had that conversation, we could talk about what we wanted to create instead. Um, both in terms of the business and in terms of how we structured our lives. I think the other, the other big heart to heart that we had on that retreat was, um, we turned to each other and asked, do you still have it in you? Do you want to keep doing this? Do you have enough to keep, to figure out how to make this work? And we were both very open and very willing for the other one to say no. You know, I, I might have been secretly hoping that she said, no, I'm tired. I'm done. It's time to get a paycheck. I'm sure both of you. Some glory. Your body, some part of your body, both of you were like saying, I hope she says no. We're done. But neither of us did. Both of us said, let's keep doing it. Let's figure out a way to make this work. Let's be open to things that we weren't before. And I think all of that was really important in getting us to where we are now. So in that time, what were the things that you, you know, what were the things that made it work? Like trying to find the thing that I could share with someone that said, you know, I did these two things that really helped us kind of get over the hump. Describe maybe some of the raising money challenges you had because I know there were plenty, so. I'm going to let you take that, that one. One of my tangible suggestions for anyone starting a business, I think especially women. And it's, and it's especially important to have these in place before you hit the bottom or start circling the bottom. Is find people in your life who value you for things outside of your business. Because it was so easy and we totally let our identities wrap themselves around this zebra striped business that we had started. And it was around that time that I realized that that was one of the things that was making me so unhappy was that I had put all of my eggs in this basket. And when it started to, you know, be rough, it was an empty basket and I needed another basket. I needed another basket with eggs in it. And so that's one of the things that I recommend to anybody is find people in your life who like you for you. And where in those relationships you can talk about your business because it's such a big part of your life. But where you know they, they like you for you separate from it. And where that's a very strong feeling that you get because there will be times when you need validation for a different part of your life. And that will give you strength to keep doing the business. But you need to have, you need to value yourself and have other people who also celebrate you as you. You need to stay grounded in that, right? To understand that it's the end of the world is not necessarily the closing of your business. Right. Yeah. That's kind of weirdly empowering too when you get to that place and get through it. You're like, oh, all right. I can do that. It's liberating in a sense. And I would say on the business side, I, I struggled with two things at that time that I've since completely reframed personally. One was that we hadn't raised all that much money. We were essentially operating as a consultancy. And so, you know, month to month we had cashflow positive months. And so she will, you know, break down her day to the point where she's making sure she has those infusions of energy. Maybe it's every third day. But take that time. And for me, I like to play basketball and I love to ride my bike. And, Brittick, maybe I don't know if you want to talk about how your role is sort of evolving, but a lot of those pieces are dynamic, especially as the company grows and that we're trying to put our hats on other people and start to let everyone focus on what they do best and what they love and where we can shine and work together. And it sounds a little kumbaya, but I think it's really important, especially once you have that well oiled machine up and running is to let go of some of the responsibilities you used to have and spend more time devoted to things that are most important to get done. It's kind of that big rock, little rock analogy people talk about too, where you can really focus on the big important rocks and not get bogged down with all the little tedious tasks that sometimes take up so much of our time. I would just add to that that I think it depends on your business and it depends on your company's values. So for our company, people have, and valuing people have always been really, really important to us. And so as soon as we brought on other people, it really became about honoring and celebrating and figuring out what everybody we were bringing into the fold had that was special, and how we could develop and celebrate everybody to be the best version of themselves that they can, both personally and professionally, because it's such a good investment from our end. So I think it often has to do with natural skills and sometimes doesn't. Sometimes you got to do the things that you don't really want to do, but it's a process. And I'm happy to talk with you more about that after. So one last question, just one. Just one last question. What's your exit strategy? What's your exit strategy? Sure. So we've always had the philosophy that if we build a great company and we're really performing in the market and we get noticed that we'll attract the attention of companies who might want to be a part of that. And that's already, you know, proving to be true to some extent. And so we're really focused on maximizing our reach and our efficacy. And I'm very confident, more confident by the day actually, which is exciting, that that will attract the right opportunity for exit when it's right. And something we're really committed to is waiting for when it's right, both for our investors and our company. You know, we've already recognized that there are certain companies that probably wouldn't be a good fit, wouldn't be a good place for Zealist to live and are starting to build a better sense of what would be. And so it's a fun process of just building a really great company and then trusting that that's going to attract the attention of the people we want to work with. So first, thank you to the sponsors for letting us sit up here for an hour, a few minutes. Thanks for the ambassadors to helping make all of our jobs easier. But most of all, thanks to Britta and Martina for being here. Thank you very much.

And now when she talks to people about joining us you know when she's recruiting she can really have a pretty compelling story about. Because she moved like she moved her her husband up here. She hurt hurt her daughter is like her best friend and she left her daughter had moved across the country to live with her in the Bay Area. And she left her daughter to come up here after her daughter had just moved across the country to live by her. So it was she had every reason to not come to gravity. But she only had one reason to come and that was good enough for her. In the back. Hi. Hi Kelly. Hey Kelly. So just a quick question. As an entrepreneur which is in your blood from the time of high school. Is the business matured? Do you find yourself itchy to do something new? Absolutely not. But maybe I will. What I tell people is I've woken up every day hoping that I can do this for the rest of my life. But I don't know if I'm going to wake up and feel that way tomorrow. So far I have and it's been a while. Now I want to do new things. Absolutely. But I want to do new things with the people that are around me right now with the company that's around me. And the reason why I turned Madrona down. The reason why I was so scared about preferred stock is they have a 10 year fund life. They have to return the funds to the investors. And so their investment period is like a couple years and then most of their investments they really need liquidity in six, seven years. And of course you could flip it to private equity and flip it to another private equity. Go public. Go through that whole rigmarole. But I said well I want to do this for the next 20 or 30, 40 years and I could be in a situation where I have to sell the company if I take this money. And so the guy, he's a friend of mine actually but he likes to pick on me. The guy that covers me for Esquire, he's a contributing editor at Rolling Stone. His name is Steven Roderick. He went on NPR and he was talking about me. And he was kind of like bad mouthing me a little bit because you kind of have to gossip in that industry a little bit. And he's like well I'm pretty sure Dan like really sees himself doing something else someday. You know kind of thing. And he's like I'm pretty sure he wants to be like Elon Musk or something like that. And I was like Steven that's like saying you want to be Walter Isaacson. Like duh. Of course I want to do stuff like what Elon Musk is doing. But can you do it kind of like where you are. And for me I'm okay if I can accomplish less but it's a little bit more pure to what I care about. Than accomplishing more and having to make compromises. Now if any of you have read a book that probably isn't fully true called Hatching Twitter. Which is a very entertaining book by Nick Bilton who writes for the New York Times and Vanity Fair. Ev Williams the original founder of Twitter. His first company Odeo. He was like me. He held on to it. He was very protective. He ended up taking an investment but he really didn't want to. And he ended up selling the company. And he took a very high control I want to have this forever approach. His second company was what became Twitter. And he took exactly the opposite approach. He wanted to push away a lot of the decision making. He wanted to raise as much money as you can. And I think in some cases like a very binary strategy on these types of issues can be appealing. So I'm going really high on control, longevity. But I think there's a case to be made. A lot of my best friends just want to, you know, have a big exit every seven years. And then take six months off and start over. And they love it. They're so happy. So I think either path can work for you. Can we? She's been trying for a while. Shanika? Shanika. Shanika. Nice name. Shanika. Shanika. Shanika. Shanika. Shanika. Shanika. You have a lot to dig the world. For me, I felt that if you went down the VC path, and then, you know, seven or ten years, you'd build this company and you would have had a great exit with the hub or without the hub. And then you would be a VC and then have that preferred thing taken out and have, you know, a lot to bring in so that that would be actually a huge impact on all the entrepreneurs when they would kind of love. I mean, I have a feeling that, you know, that could have been another part, but it's maybe that, you know, that was not something you could afford to do. I agree with you. I think where I came from and where I was raised, I had very limited visibility. My idea was, you know, kind of like a small business owner. And so I built a business that served initially and still today small business owners. Those were the people I idolized. And I didn't even necessarily want to be one of them, but I just wanted to help them out and like be in their life in a meaningful way and be trusted. And so that was my direction. And it would have been such a pivot from that such a big step to all of a sudden get involved in the financial world. And so I absolutely think you're right that there's a way to sequence it and engineer it for maximum benefit. But I don't really regret that because things could always be better or bigger and things could always be worse or smaller. And I don't necessarily think better and bigger are equivalent. Right. And that probably might also have made that you might have had to change your client base or the customers that you serve and all that other stuff. Thank you. I get very attached. Right. I get attached to the people I'm working with, my clients, my ideas. Sometimes people don't get attached to me. So that's nice. Last question, guys. Last question. Oh. Right here. Hi. My name is Walter. Hey, Walter. I've been a photographer for a very, very long time. And over the years, I've seen a gradual and in the past maybe 10 years more precipitate drop in the value of imagery. And in the industry I'm in, there's discussion about, because of the commoditization of images and information being free or being perceived as free, that quality doesn't matter anymore. And you made a statement earlier tonight about the triumph of style over substance. In the past maybe year or so, I've noticed a gradual uptick where the buying segment of people that buy imagery are getting sick of the commoditization and would pay a little more if I could just get something that's some quality. Yeah. As a wide brushstroke in business, do you feel that turn or is this a commoditized situation? I totally agree with you. I think that the pendulum swings back and forth and there's a reaction. A good analogy of this would be when David Axelrod was thinking through when then state senator or senator, I can't remember, Obama might have a shot at some election cycle. He noticed how in presidential history, a lot of times the temperament or the style or the substance or something about the president that's elected is the opposite of the previous president. Because the pendulum swings back and forth and so he thought that Obama in some ways represented certain parts of the opposite of George Bush. George Bush was very warm and he connected with people. Obama was kind of cold and surgical and scientific and professorial. And what he missed was he said he was always pooh-poohing the idea that Trump could have a chance to be president or even get the nomination. But when he thought about, well, then what's the natural reaction to Obama, which is kind of always like measured and cool and like very, very much like team organized and like going slow, getting criticized for never taking a stand on anything, never really saying what he believes. And all of a sudden you have a guy up there who seems to be the exact opposite of all those things. And so I do think the pendulum is swinging back and forth and I do think it's swinging toward we've pushed so hard on low cost. And I think that there's more and more emphasis now on quality and on value. And I feel less pressure. I'm in a commoditized industry that's very competitive. I feel less pricing pressure today in some ways than I ever have in an industry where it's getting more commoditized every day because people in my industry, people are starting to say, well, the real commodity is trust. Right. And so I do think there's an X factor there that will continue to grow. But what's happening that really hit me is there's a lot of people that are being left behind where they just don't have a choice to participate in that anymore. And then you have other people that have so much money that literally they have more like more money than they could ever do anything practical with. So they're driving some of that. But I think culturally, even somebody that's getting started that's not far from the bottom of the of the economic ladder has a desire to be able to buy value, pay for quality and like have things be artistic. You know, just even in our company in the last few months, we started to say, hey, if you're going to write a paper or if you're going to write a blog post or if you're going to design something or if you're going to take a photograph, why don't you sign it? Right. And like just that simple thing, it doesn't seem like a big deal. And it might seem like, oh, that's way off brand. Why would you have an individual employee who might leave sign their piece? But what does that say to them? Yeah, it says it's art, right? It says it's important. And so now all of a sudden they have to take an artistic creators approach to that thing. And it sounds like you and I both hope that that's where we're all headed. So I hope that's true. Yeah. Great answer. Thank you. Well, thank you guys for being here.
