interviewee_bio: Aaron Bird is the CEO & Founder of Bizible , changing the way B2B marketers define success one company at a time.
video_title: Mike Grabham, Startup Grind Eastside w/ Aaron Bird, CEO/Founder, Bizible
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Grime. Without any further ado, I'd like to welcome Aaron Bird, CEO of Visible. Come on up. Russell Wilson. I hate Russell Wilson. Yeah, yeah. Just hold that down. You'll be on. There you go. Awesome. You guys can hear me okay? There's a, fine. Okay. Well, thanks for coming. I appreciate you all being here. And thank you for being here. Yeah. Yeah. I'm excited. Yeah, it's good. Aaron and I have known each other for, wow, six years-ish. I think that's about right. Yeah, yeah. So, I remember what... You're like my sales mentor early on. Yeah, early on we were talking. Like, how do we sell things? We need help. Yeah, exactly. How do we sell this thing? We didn't have a thing yet. Well, so, let's just kind of start. You know, what I always thought, I think, is interesting about your story, one is because I know it well, but two is that you went at it kind of just, you just went at it like, I'm just going to do this. I don't necessarily know how to do it, but we're just going to do it. Right. Let's find smart people to help me and we'll just figure it out along the way, which is great. I mean, that's the kind of true, you know, characteristics of an entrepreneur. So, tell us kind of some of that early stage stuff, like, you know, before it had... Let's go pre-model, before it were the business model you're in now. Kind of tell us that side, how that started. Yeah. Even Microsoft and, you know. Yeah, I'll give you, like, even the pre-leaving Microsoft because there's some context there. Like, I always knew I was going to start a company and I had this kind of grand vision of I'm going to work as a kind of software developer, managing teams, save up a bunch of money and then I'm going to take that money and go start a company. Right. So, that was, like, my... I got out of school in my early 20s and that was my plan. I'm like, oh, I'll start a company maybe when I'm 30 and I want to save up a decent amount so I can use my money to start a company. So, I'd saved up a bunch of money. I actually had... I had moved to a job at Microsoft up here in Seattle. So, I had a house. I had a condo in LA that I moved from that I was renting. I had a condo in Seattle as well. And I'd saved up, like, $250,000 in cash plus these condos, right? So, I was getting close. I was like, I want about a half a million before I start a company. So, 2008 happens and the housing market in both go 50%. Sure, yeah. The renter that my house in LA sues me. She, like, slips and falls. It's, like, this huge disaster. So, I went from having, like, $250,000 in the public stock market and some assets in two condos to literally zero. It all got wiped out. I had to take... I actually had to borrow $20,000 from my parents to close the condo to sell the condo in LA. So, I was negative $20,000, you know, coming out of, like, 2009 or whatever it was. So, it was, like... So, lesson one was plans are irrelevant in startups. Like, your perfect plan sucks. It's not going to work. Don't do it. Like, just jump, right? So, that was... And this was even before I left Microsoft. So, that was the first thing I was, like, how... You know, it's... I spent a year while paying my parents back and then also just thinking, like, what... My whole plan. There's no way I'm going to save up money. It's going to take me five, six years, right? Like, or whatever. It's just... Yeah, there's no way. So, I need something different. So, anyway. So, the end of 2010, I was, you know, taking kind of the Christmas break off, thinking through everything. And I was, like, I just have to jump. I just have to go start a company. I can't... The planning doesn't work. And so, came back to Microsoft, put in my notice, and that was that. So, the... Now, one thing I did get to do is when I was... When I over break, I called some people that, like... Since I told a bunch of people, hey, I'm going to start a company someday. And I had a bunch of friends and family that were, like, whenever you do, call me. You know, I think you do well. And so, I was able to raise a little bit of money from friends and family so that we could just kind of get going, you know. Because I didn't have any money because it was all gone, right? So... And you needed money. Yes. Yeah, exactly. So, anyway. So, that was the... That was kind of the first lesson. And I left Microsoft with, like, six, like, half a dozen or so ideas, the things that I had seen along the way. Visible, the way it is today, was actually kind of one of those that... And something I had seen when I was in Bing and Bing ads. But... So, I had... And they were all different. Like, it was, like, how to solve the mobile app distribution problem, how to, you know, help businesses measure ROI. You know, we had lots of... Like, how small businesses could get found online, which you remember that one. So, anyway. So, some of them, help charities raise money, like, you know. That was the... Yeah. That one. Give it in. So, anyway. So, I had these ideas. And it was... And we had read about the lean startup methodology, which I highly recommend if anyone's starting a company or wants to. It's pretty well known at this point. But... So, anyway. I had read about that. And I was like, we're going to follow this. And we're just going to, like, hammer through, you know, what are the assumptions? MVP, right? Not waste time. Like, the whole philosophy there. But even with that intent, we still ended up wasting a lot of time with bad ideas. And... You don't know... The problem is you don't know when the bad ideas are bad ideas. Right. Until you go through them and like, oh, shit, that was a bad idea. Exactly. Yeah. It's... It's... Yeah. And it's hard. Like, they can... It's like they have to check, you know, five boxes. And if they check four, you're excited. And then, it turns out you can't acquire customers for the, you know, the price that it takes, right? And things like that. So... Yeah. Yeah. So, we spent a year and a half just trying different things, like, before we actually got revenue. Yeah. And then we had our first business, which was the one that, you know, the first version of Visible. Yeah. Yeah. So, so, so explain the first version, like... Yeah. Yeah, like, because then there's some context of, like, where it went, right? Yeah. So, explain the... Explain V.1 of Visible. Yeah. So, one of the insights... So, my wife's a wedding planner. And so, I know a bunch of small business owners in Seattle. So, I have a friend that runs, like, a painting company and photographers and all that stuff. Right. Yeah. Yeah. The painting company was, like, our use case. So, the... So, anyway, so, I had seen, kind of, these small businesses try to get business and try to grow. And so, I'd even helped, like, figured out SEO and how to get... How to figure, like, all the hacks on Yelp and, like, all the different things you need to do as a small business to get found online. And so, that was... The first version of Visible was actually... I mean, it wasn't even... It's not Visible. We named it Visible. Sure. But it's nothing like it is today. But we... It was to help small businesses get found online. And so, we... And it was a software platform to do that. And we launched that at the beginning of 2012. And we got to 20,000 of MRR within eight months, something like that. And there was, like, seven of us at the time. And was that pre-Techstars? Mm-hmm. That was pre-Techstars. Yeah. So, then we applied to Techstars. And so, one thing that we realized is that we were helping businesses get found online. These are small businesses, like, owner-operator. They're not... They didn't... They weren't... They weren't good at tracking where their business came from. And so, we would actually boost their business, but we didn't have a way to prove it. And we knew it was working because we were measuring all the analytics on the background. But we're like, we need a way to show... We're going to hook into QuickBooks, and we're going to tie the stuff we do to the revenue in QuickBooks so we can show the ROI. So, we started building out all this, like, ROI tracking stuff for these small businesses to justify the marketing platform that we were selling to them. And in the process, we had a business, and we were marketing online, and we were trying to get found. And I was having a hard time measuring, like, the ROI of our marketing. And so, I was like... So, this goes back to kind of the... We had this insight in Bing, and it was, like, helping B2B companies measure. And so, we had the pain firsthand, and then we realized that, you know what? We should just ditch this marketing platform. It's hard to sell to small businesses. Let's take the measurement platform that we've built and go upmarket and sell to much bigger businesses. And so, that was... That was, you know, through Techstars and then post-Techstars, and then we raised our round through Madrona. Yeah, post-Techstars. Yeah. And in those early days, Aaron, where were the things that you were able to kind of iterate quickly from? Where were the things you... What did you learn how to learn quickly? Yeah. Because I remember you and Peter, you know, just hacking away at it, and his co-founder is a coder as well. So, yeah. Yeah. I think the... So, we took Lean Startup, and eventually, after we really kind of mastered it, took it one step further. And it wasn't just about creating an MVP. It was actually about selling before you build. So, we had this philosophy of, the only way you know that someone will buy it, that they'll buy it at a price point that you can cost-effectively build it and acquire them for, and that you're selling to the right buyer, that they can actually sign and buy, like all the go-to-market problems that can happen. The only way you can kind of do an MVP all the way to the end is to get somebody to sign a check. And so, after about a year of kind of really figuring out how to do startups and going through all these ideas, it was like, we're only selling before we build. And so, the first version of Visible, we got three checks before we started writing code. And so, even today, I mean, now we have hundreds of customers, but we still have that mentality, and we'll, you know, we want to validate in the market before we build something. And we oftentimes sell before we build. I mean, we'll tell them, like, you know, we don't have this today, we're going to build it, we'll have it done by this date, and now we have a bigger reputation and stuff, so people are much more likely to believe it and stuff like that, or we have customers that we have goodwill with. So, it's a lot harder when you're like, you know, not only do we not have a product, we don't have a business, but I want you to give me a check so I can go build that thing. Yeah, exactly. And by the way, you don't know who we are. Yeah, and you don't know who we are. But I think it's like, startups are hard, and it's like, pick which hard problem you want to solve, right? You know, building it is hard, but we realize that figuring out the hard problem of how to sell before you build to whatever market you're in, that's hard, but it's worth spending the time, because then if you can do that, like, you know you've figured, it's better than MEP, right? Like, you've proven everything out. Yeah, yeah, that's that trick that, you know, before you ever have your service, put an ad on Facebook or Google or whatever, and just, well, people click on it and expect to get something. I mean, that's what you should, I mean, that saves you so much headaches. Yeah, yeah, exactly, like, yeah, if you have something that can be sold completely online, then you can run ads, you can have a website that says it's going to do what it's going to do, and then you can say, you know, sign up here and put in your credit card, and just throw their credit card away. Obviously, you're not going to bill it because you don't have anything done yet, but just see, will they put in their credit card? Like, essentially, you've sold it now, right? And then you email them, and you're like, okay, well, it's going to be three weeks before we deliver this or whatever it is, you know, but if you can do that, yeah, you learn so much more. We spent so much time building in a direction that was wrong, you know, and that cuts it out. Yeah, yeah, it absolutely cuts it out. So, you know, what things did you, you know, in those early days when it's, you know, you and Alex and Andy, you know, what did you guys learn that you're still doing today? So, let me give some context because the audience, so, they had an office down in Soto, and, you know, it was like seven guys and four interns and, you know, people running around this crappy little office. Right by the dump. Right by the dump. You can smell the dump. And the restaurant place, you can smell the job. Yeah, yeah, yeah. It was a direct marketing place that had a bunch of, like, Asian food just was reeked through the office. Right, they would, like, cook in the building. It was a mess. It was so gross. When it would rain, the walls would, like, bleed with water, so, like, water would seep through the wall, and the paint would bubble out. Like, Alex still has a video of him, like, pressing one of those things and water shooting out. Yeah, it was an awesome place. Yeah, yeah. Good choice. It was a good deal, though. It was a great deal. Yeah, it was a great deal. Low rent. So, in those early days, what did you learn there that you're still doing today? Like, what one or two things? Or maybe there's nothing. I think the sell before you build is still true, right? I mean, like, even though we know way more about our market, we have customers, we have revenue, we still can make a mistake, right? We could still make an assumption and be wrong about it. So, I think making sure someone will buy what you're selling, right, before you start building it. You can't always do exactly that as you get bigger, but we still do it as much as we can. And some people think, like, you're doing your customer a disservice if you sell them something that doesn't exist. The reality is you're doing them a service because it's a good thing because you're trying to figure out what they actually want, right? The worst thing you could do is go and build something for them that they don't actually want. And so, anyway, it's kind of counterintuitive there. So, we still definitely do that. And, you know, I think being lean in general, just kind of really watching costs. And we've always done that, you know, hence the rent at the place by the town. And then being connected to customers. You know, I think as you get bigger, it's easy to kind of not talk to customers. Especially as, like, a CEO. It's easy to, like, oh, I have a VP of product. Like, Brewster's going to take care of this or whatever. I still need to be talking to customers. Like, I still have to stay in tune with the market and, like, how our product's being perceived and everything. So, like, really being customer focused, we've always had that. And I think even if we're 10,000 employees, it's something we'll still have. Yeah, exactly. And, you know, where in the, again, those early days, where were the things that you look back now on and say, okay, here are the things that we did really, really well. And then here are the things that we just totally screwed the pooch on. Like, we just, you know, obviously one of them is you spent too much time. But, like, kind of like more operational things or cultural things or hiring the wrong people or all those things you have to do when you're small. Yeah. What were the things that you would give advice to somebody saying, here's the mistakes I made. Don't do these. Yeah. I think on the hiring front, when you really, when you have a really pressing hire and you have someone you really have to, like, you're feeling pain as an organization because this person's not in that role yet. It's really easy to say when you're interviewing candidates and you're getting people that are good but not great, it's really easy to say, you know what, in the next six months we'll be better off if I have someone good in this role than if I have no one in this role, right? Yeah. And that's true for the next six months. The problem is after that. Seven through. Yeah, yeah, through infinity or whatever, right? So the cost of having to replace someone, both for you and them, is way too high. And so I think it's hard to resist that urge and just say to the team, like, we're going to keep, you know, we're going to keep this pain going because we haven't found the perfect person yet. But I think that's one that we made mistakes early on just because it seemed like the easy out. Well, yeah, you feel like you needed a body, right? Yeah. Yeah, like the cost of not having them is so high, but in the end it's much worse. So I think keeping a high bar for hiring, even if it means, you know, all the things that aren't happening because you don't have that person yet. Yeah. Because it's hard, right? Especially early. It's hard to recruit people. They're, you know, why are they going to bet on you? And so I think that's one of the biggest challenges in early stage startups. Yeah. Is this getting the, well, I mean, getting them to even sit down with you to discuss is sometimes even, hard, right? So where is the, you know, let's talk about that hiring piece because that is interesting because, you know, when you're talking about four or five people, right? Yeah. And you're, you know, hiring number six. That's number six is one-sixth of the company. Right. It's a pretty significant hire, right? So what things did you learn then in that process? I think for, you know, this may be obvious to you, but people use the term like athletes, right? And not like physical athletes, but people that can do a lot of stuff, right? Sure. Generalists. Yeah. Generalists. That, you know, you could put them on marketing, put them on sales. Like, I think in the early days, hiring for culture and then hiring for that, you know, generalist kind of athlete role. Because you don't know two months from now what you're going to be doing or what you need them doing. And specializing too early, you go get like the perfect content marketing person because you need content marketing. Well, and then you tell them like, I need you to go to a field event and work a booth, right? And they're like, I've never done that before. So, you know, that may be obvious, but I think just really early on just hiring, not so much for their, even their skills or their resume, but just like, you know, can you hand them the ball and they'll run with it? And then the culture fit is really big too. Like, do they have the same values? I think perseverance early on is everything. So somebody that's going to, you know, really be in for the long haul. And I think we got really lucky. You know, Alex? Yeah. Yeah. Even, I mean, our first like 20 hires, like even today, I think we've been really fortunate with just having a culture of people that are really all in for the company. But your first, you know, 10 people have to all be like that. And it's going to be hard. Like maybe you're going to miss payroll, right? And you're going to say, hey guys, I can't pay you this, like this, you know, this month. Like I'm going to make it up to you next month or once we close this deal or everyone's going to have to take half as much or whatever. And, you know, hopefully that doesn't happen. But you need people that like, you know, are going to be in it and be able to do that. It happens. It happens. I've done, I mean, I've totally done that. It's a brutal conversation, but part of the deal. And, you know, in the product development, I mean, you've got, you know, Peter to help you, you know, build stuff. Is there anything that you would say, you know, when you're not building, not the first one, because you're, like I say, let's just assume that we've got something we know people will pay for. But to get from that stage to something that can start to scale at some level, not necessarily at breakneck speed. But what are some tricks to get from, oh, I know people will pay for this, got a couple customers. Yeah. To now I can have a thousand customers, right? What are some tricks that you guys have done? And, again, because you were lean, you know, the whole time. Yeah. I think on the product side, I think most entrepreneurs can follow their instincts on, like, what do customers need? What do I need to build? The hard part, I think, is the go-to-market or the customer acquisition, kind of if you're in B2C or B2B. But, and I think the, yeah, and it just, it takes a lot of hustle. Like, you have to find, you have to find areas that are kind of unexploited for whatever reason. You know, if you, like, buying AdWords is probably not going to work for most startups, right? It's like, A, you don't have the money, and B, even if you did, it's like, yeah, you're competing against established companies that have lots of resources so they can bid a lot higher. So you have to find areas where, like, I think of it a lot, I think of marketing investments a lot like the stock market. In the stock market, if you want to do better than market returns, you have to do things, you can't just buy the S&P 500, right? That'll do what the market does. So you have to find things that other people think are undervalued, or that are undervalued because other people think that they're not worth what they're actually worth. And so, and the reason is, look, I mean, you know, I say, look, first of all, I mean, we just met, you know, you spent 40 minutes telling me, I'm not your expert in this field, but I'm not charging you for the advice. It's totally free, you should value it accordingly. But, you know, look, I mean, time is our mutual enemy, okay? You're only going to be able to work on so many opportunities and projects, and I'm only going to be able to invest in so many opportunities. And, you know, I just share that I'm very sort of known in this community for being very upfront. And I will tell you what I think about your project and why it works or doesn't work, okay? And you can do with it what you like, but it's amazing how many people have come back to me over the years saying, you know, that thing you told me, you know, that's what happened. And, you know, it's just like there's a lot of pattern recognition that does happen. You can just see, so I'll just give you one very simple one that, so a lot of companies will come to me and say, hey, I've got this really great technology, and I'm going to use a channel, an indirect approach to get to market, okay? And I just told you about one that's working, so the rarity. But I would tell you that 99.9% of enterprise technologies do not. And the way I tell it, it's like the desert is really hot. Water is really heavy. Nobody is going to carry your water across the desert, okay? Nobody, okay? You've got to figure out how to get in front of your buyers and, you know, get those first sales. Now, once you've got demand and, you know, yes, there are lots of channels that want to fulfill demand, but channels will not create your first customers, you know? So, yeah, so, you know, like any company that's telling me, hey, I've got this thing and I've just signed this deal with XYZ and they're going to resell it and, you know, first-year sales are going to be, you know, $14 million, you know, like, you know, no way. Not going to happen. And I've never had an entrepreneur come back and tell me, you know, you were wrong. That happened. I was one of those entrepreneurs who want a company. I thought I was going to be a reseller. Yeah. Didn't work. Didn't work at all. Did you properly introduce our guests? No, we didn't. Because he came up and I didn't want to, you know. I didn't want to embarrass him. Because when we introduce guests to people that haven't been to a start- Oh, we do the little clap, clap, clap. We scream and yell like he's a rock star. Oh, that's right. I do understand. Bill's not a, he's a humble guy. He's like, it's kind of a, like, I'd rather not do that if I had a choice. Well, thank you very much for being here.

interviewee_bio: Dan launched Maveron , in 1998, with Howard Schultz, former CEO and Executive Chairman of Starbucks Coffee Company.
video_title: Mike Grabham, Startup Grind Eastside w/ Dan Levitan, Founder, Maveron
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Grind. Dude, I'd like to welcome Dan Levitan from Averon. Come on up, Dan. Here you go, my friend. I feel like Russell. Yes. Okay. Y'all, you can't all hear me, okay? Y'all? They've been hearing you for the last, like, 20 minutes, I know. I thought we came here to hear Mike grab him. Yeah. It gets longer every month, it seems like. Well, thank you for coming to spend some time today with us. I appreciate that. It's always nice to see you. Smile and face. With pleasure. Yes. So, let's give your, kind of give your elevator pitch for Maveron so everyone clearly knows what you guys do over there. I mean, I can tell them that you can do a better job. Sure. The elevator pitch for Maveron is, for the last 19 years, we've been a consumer-only venture capital firm. And I was talking with Pam earlier. I think what's happened in the last few decades is that the phone has all integrated into consumers' lives and created businesses that never really had the capability to one-to-one directly connect with consumers. And so, we think that the world didn't need another venture capital firm that's undifferentiated and just says they're opportunistic. So, we are a consumer-only venture firm and we're early stage, which means we do seeds and series A. And because we're a consumer, we look, we have offices in San Francisco and Seattle, and I would say that probably New York and L.A. are the fourth, the third and fourth markets that we focus on. And in the last, like, what, say, let's say three years, what have been some of the, I guess, successes? Like, what would you, off the top of your head? Well, here in town, our biggest success in the last few years was we were fortunate enough to have Zulily start in our office by Daryl and Mark. And, you know, we've had an overnight 10-year success in True Panyan, which is a medical insurance for cats and dogs companies, which is now approaching $700 million in market cap. But it's taken a long time. It's a long road to get there. 400 employees down in Georgetown, but kind of a consistent grower. And frankly, it's reframed. I think there's a lot of glory to the go big fast. And I meet a lot of entrepreneurs, and some of their ideas are not going to blow up and be perfect overnight for venture capitalists. And I think they do themselves and their investors a disservice if they try to turn the company into something that it isn't or shouldn't be. And I've seen lots of companies get screwed up by the wrong investors. And so I think, you know, one of the things that everyone really doesn't talk about a lot in my mind in the startup world is the glory is, well, you know, how much money have you raised? I don't really think that that's the way most businesses should be judged. Because what if you don't ever make money and you've raised, you know, $50 million? By the way, it's the same way for venture capital firms. Everyone talks about how much money have you raised, what's the size of your last fund? And very few people are really consistently disclosing returns because it's a damn hard business. Exactly. That sounds easy. It's easier to raise the money than it is to replicate or remake it. Let's talk about that, I guess, the difference between the company that should be funded by a Maveron and the company that shouldn't even go there, right? Like, what's the difference in there? I mean, there's people in this room. How many people have started a company, like, in the last 12 months and are either starting a company in the next six months? Like, so we've got, you know, a dozen, maybe 15 of those people sitting in this room, right? So how do we talk to those 15 people and say, you know what? You're not a VC. Don't try to kill yourself. That's not your model. Like, how do we know that? How do they? What's a good idea? Don't kill yourself. Your startup can't succeed if you're dead. Well, there's a few people, actually, in this audience who have come to talk to me. First, the notion that I would dispel is that it's black or white. It's either VC or it isn't. I think in a lot of cases, it should start out, perhaps, without a lot of funding and test and learn and see where there are inflection points. Trupanion started out as a guy with a dream in Canada on an island called Bowen Island outside of Vancouver. And for seven years, he just raised friends and family money. And we were the, in 2007, we were his first institutional round. But he had gone from zero pets to 6,000 pets, insured. So I think the first thing is, I think you need the appropriate capital strategy at the time for the idea and you and the team, as opposed to over-capitalizing it. But that said, I think the kinds of things that are VC-oriented. I mean, does everyone in the room kind of understand the economics of why VCs push for super big ideas? Well, so in a private equity business, you're going to, a private equity fund, you raise a couple hundred million bucks and, you know, you hope you don't have any write-offs. But all your returns are kind of between, say, you get half your money back and, say, a 5x. And if you can do that, that blends out to an IRR that hopefully is between 10% and 20% and gets above the 8% hurdle for them to get carry. And they're happy. Venture is not like that. Venture is a much more wild ride. In our first fund, which was started in 1998, eBay returned the fund. One investment. And we had one other thing that was successful. And then we had a bunch of things because 2,000 rolled around in 2001 that went to zero. So in our best funds, there's one or two businesses that have outperformed everything else combined. So in our fourth fund, you know, Zulily was, you know, I mean, when you're lucky enough to partner with great entrepreneurs and you can get in at a single-digit pre-money and it becomes a multi-billion-dollar business, you look a lot smarter than you really are. Exactly. Thankfully. But so the VCs are looking for those kind of investments. And most investments don't fit into that. Yeah, they don't have the potential to be a billion dollars or even a half a billion dollars. And our investors and ourselves are willing to lose money completely and move on, whereas in the private equity world, they don't really like to do that. And I will say that's been an acquired taste for me. I kind of started in the business not readily kind of writing it off. Willing to write it off. And not willing to say, you know, maybe we shouldn't participate in this big because we haven't found product-market fit. There's been a lot of turnover in the team. Maybe the thesis wasn't right. And so I think a good venture capitalist is aligned up front with the team and the founders of the company and then helps the team and the founders of the company interpret the data that gives you information about was the original thesis right or not. So many times I'm two or three years into something and the original thesis is imprinted on the entrepreneur's mind. And I appreciate that, but change happens. And the sooner we kind of say, well, we thought this but that, the better off we are and we can make a better decision. Yeah. So I think one of the things that is really important there is the fact that it's okay to change your mind. It's totally okay. And actually, how many out of 100 companies, how many do you think that when they started out, their idea is that's what they ended up? How many of the 100 actually happened like that? You're just a guess. 10? 20? Many of our biggest successes started and then kind of went the way they thought. I think the Googles of the world are rare. You know, I've pivoted and lo and behold, it gets to be. But I would say every business has lots of bumps in the road. And, you know, one of the questions really is, do you have the grit and the determination to get through it? And the scary thing is, frankly, for those of you who have been in this game since 2010, this is not going to be the environment forever. If you're starting a company today, it's most likely that you're going to face an environment that is radically different than what it's been like from 2010 to 2017. I can't tell you when, and I'm not a doomsday guy, but these markets go in cycles. And, you know, we started in 98. Everyone thought we were brilliant investing in consumer companies. Not in 2000. 2000, 2001, they thought we were complete idiots. Then, in 2007, we were back to brilliant. And in 2009, everyone was shutting down consumer funds. But, interestingly enough, 2009, a company like Zulalie walks into our office, doesn't talk to any other venture firm, and great things happen. And, you know, many, many, many, many significant companies were created in 2009, 10, and 11. Now, why is that? Because those were the most determined entrepreneurs. Those were the grittiest. Those were the most disciplined on spending. And I think there's a lot of lessons to be learned. And for those of you who are starting a company, I would say, regardless of how much money is in your bank account, run the business in a way that is different than that. Last night, Mike was saying he was on a red eye. I was out drinking with one of my portfolio companies. So, we had this event in L.A., and after it, we went out drinking. And then, these guys are going back to their hotel. And, you know, the CEO says to me, I said, where are you staying? And they gave me the name of some L.A. hotel that I had never heard of. And they said, you know, well, we tell our head of admin in our company that $150 maximum per night. The company is very successful. But the CEO, two CEOs, how they act and the message that they send permeates the company. And, you know, I mean, I think Jeff Bezos lives that today, one of the most successful companies in the world, and partly because of that level of frugality. That's why he keeps referring to the doors, you know, and day one and all that stuff. And so, for those of you, I think a lot of entrepreneurs confuse initial success with changing that attitude. And I think that's unhealthy. And where do you think, like, in the current environment of where we are today, with just the funding environment, where do you think that, if we look back in the last 15, I guess the 17, so look back in the last 20 years, do you think it compares any of those years that we can, like, say, you know it's similar to X a year? Do you think it's similar in any historical stuff we've already been through? Let's say the last 20 years, because 20 years, most of these people were wondering around. Well, 98, 99 was such a go-go. And 01, 02 was so down. I mean, if there was a time, I'd say it's 06, 07. But I don't think we have the mortgage problem. We'll talk about that later. Would you disagree? Well, I just, yeah, I think 2006, 2007, it was pretty frothy. I mean, it was, like, the housing market was... We have 165 multi-billion dollar valued private companies. That is not a sustainable equilibrium. It isn't. You're right. You know, excuse me? Have you seen the housing market today? Well, housing market around here, definitely. But we had dinner on Monday night with a guy who runs $75 billion for a large money manager. And, you know, we had just looked at Blue Apron. And there are just a lot of private companies out there that are being valued at prices that would be lower were they either sold or taken to the public markets. Now, that's been a theme that's been happening for the last three years. But, and it doesn't directly impact the startup world, but it's crazy. It's a trend. Yeah. Yeah, it's actually a trend. And where do you... And so, my point is, that's frothy. Every bit as frothy to me as 2006. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Okay. Who's driving the froth? I think there aren't very many asset classes where you can kind of get the venture type returns. And so, people look for, are willing to take a, take risk in this bucket. And, you know, I think there is an argument that if you don't have to go public, why should you go public? Grow the company if it's growing super fast. There's probably more faster growing companies that are of substance. I mean, I don't think this is 2000 in terms of, it's not an eyeballs thing. I mean, many of these companies have real businesses. But, I think, you know, also we're living in a world of platforms. And dominant leadership positions in industries seem to command an exponentially high percentage of the rewards. And where, and for, you know, the person who's starting the company, you know, today, or last week, or in the last three or four months, where do you think, you know, where do you think you would spend your time? Like, what would be the one or two things you would, like, here's the things I would be extremely concerned about? And I'm not saying bad things. I'm just saying to run the business and where it's going to go. What are the couple things you would say, here's one or two things I would focus on the first six months to a year of your company? Well, I mean, regardless of the environment, I think the first thing I would focus on is getting a great team. At the end of the day, teams win. And one of the things we look at is what's been the senior hires over a period of six to 18 months from the founding. And if there's no ads, you have to ask yourself why. And, you know, there's all sorts of reasons why. Sometimes the idea isn't working. But if the idea is working and they're not adding people, you know, one of the things I've discovered a few times is we made the wrong CEO back. That person thinks he or she can do too much of it. And so I think hiring is important. In any great consumer business, I think getting your values clear, articulated, aligned, and agreed to is super important. And, you know, I think that great brands are built from the inside out. So what I learned at Starbucks was Howard created a psychological contract. First, amongst the leadership team, what this company was going to stand for. Second, amongst the leadership team and the rest of the company. And the employees knew what they were signing up for. They opted in. I'm seeing B Corps now attract people they shouldn't technically be attracting, in my mind, because they're a value system. And they're willing to incur extra time, extra money, because they stand for something. So I think getting that psychological contract right with your employees. And then beginning to understand what the brand is all about with your customers. And what do you stand for in terms of customer service, in terms of product, in terms of whatever else. So I'd say those are important. And obviously getting a product that people want to integrate into consumers' lives. At the end of the day, I think the great businesses have products that people like and repeat and use. And, you know, how many of you took an Uber or a Lyft over here? Okay, very few. But it blows my mind, though, right? I mean, on the one hand, it's amazing how in 10 years we've integrated the cell phone into our lives. But, you know, seven years, you know, I remember hearing that, you know, there was a black car company called Uber. And, you know, shame on us, frankly, if we view ourselves as leading consumer investors, and we completely miss that. That was a super learning. But, you know, what does that company have? And before we get into, if we do, any of the issues it has, what it had was a super high addressable market, right? And it kind of created that addressable market. But it's a business that, you know, is so vast. And so we love to find disruptible, large categories with super passionate people who are attacking those opportunities. Yeah. And, like, for you to say, that's something I want to, you know, learn more about. You know, like, we'll talk about the guy I introduced you to, you know, the light bulb guy. Yeah. Right? Everybody needs light bulbs. Like, how do you evaluate some of these factors that are coming up? Because they're not, it's not like, oh, it's like X. It's like, there isn't like, it isn't like X. It's like that. So, like, my product is the same way. Like, mine doesn't fit in any of the box yet. But how do you evaluate that for, you know, just your company? How do you look at things and say, this potentially could be the thing? Because it's not a thing yet. Right. You know, I mean, our business is very humbling because you're sometimes right, you're sometimes wrong, but you have to never be uncertain. We have to give a yes or a no to an entrepreneur. And frankly, we're talking amongst ourselves on trying to improve the commitment we make on the speed of giving that no. But it's humbling to think, well, could that be really big? And, you know, an example for me of that is I never, ever thought I would think about investing in a shoe company as a venture investment. And then we see, I don't know, three, four startup shoe companies a year. But never invested in one. And I was having breakfast with one of the co-founders of Warby Parker in New York. And I said, so we weren't smart enough to invest in you, which we had the opportunity to pursue, but that was another miss. What should I be looking at? And he tells me the name of this company called Allbirds. And, you know, since Graubham was plugging all his stuff, I will, these are the shoes, they're wool shoes. Anyhow, I thought to myself, you know, well, we never invest in a shoe company. And then you start doing the work to say, how many billion dollar shoe companies have there been? Surprising amount. And are there extensions off of shoes? You know, I mean, a bunch of companies have proven, you know, like Nike, like Under Armour, like Lulu, you know. So, I think you've got to be open-minded with us, but think about this. There's 3,000 new businesses that are venture-funded a year. I don't know what percentage of them are in the consumer world, but let's say half, 40%. There's probably five to at most 10 venture exits every year in the consumer world worth talking about. So, that's a 1,500 to 5 funnel. Odds are against you. So, we've come to use a phrase called, we want to invest in non-normal entrepreneurs. And it came from, my team is sick of the analogies that I have on Duke basketball. Yeah. Duke. Yeah. Did you, and just, yeah, this is why Dan did go to Duke, just in case you didn't know that. But it, it, Krzyzewski was talking one day, and Duke was playing in December, and it was right after exams, and the kids were kind of flat. And he was talking about how, on the one hand, it's completely normal that in the beginning of the season, in December, you have a game against, he didn't say this, but it was a weak opponent. And so, the kids were flat, and he said, the kids acted normal tonight, but they want to win a championship. And if you want to win a championship, you have to be non-normal. And you're going to play 40 games, and every game, you've got to play for 40 minutes like the best you can play. And so, what we're looking for is those non-normal people we deem to be non-normal entrepreneurs, because we think that's what it takes in terms of the unexpected things that come up. That's good. And where, like, I know you're not a, you're not a trend guy, per se, but what are some trends or just things that you like? What is Dan like in the way of industries that are just interesting to you? Like, it doesn't even have to be in your consumer space. Is it, maybe it's AR, I don't know, but, like, what are some things that you like just because you like it? Well, I think there's so many interesting things going on today. I mean, what a period of innovation we're living in, right? But, you know, you mentioned AR, VR. Yesterday, I was in Los Angeles, and I saw a company that takes live video of people and then marries that up with voice recognition, such that it looks like you're interviewing, you're asking questions to those people. And in this case, they did it with Holocaust survivors. So they took seven days of films with this Holocaust survivor, and they asked him everything about that part of his life. And so they said, go ahead and ask him a question. And you ask him virtually anything, and the voice recognition then takes you to this video, which is incredibly real. And it looks like they've caught, they pull up the time when he answered a question similar to that. So I think, you know, it's incredible, like, think about the teaching or the, or, you know, this was an L.A. company, so they were highly focused on celebrities wanting this, which isn't my thing. But I think AR and VR is super interesting. And we made a bet here in a company called Pluto VR, which is a really exciting early-stage team out of PopCap Games and Pixar Studios. So I think that's one area which is super interesting. I think, you know, I don't know if anyone saw today, the CEO of Pepsi was complaining about, you know, all the changes that are going on in the consumer packaged goods world. And I think that's a super interesting space now. Think about, you know, I mean, Amazon Whole Foods is tangentially related to that. But I think Amazon's acquisition of that company is as much a fact that, you know, when you're a $500 billion market cap, what's going to move the needle for you? You've got to go into huge pieces of the economy. And so everyone's, like, making all sorts of bets about Amazon. I think the reality is they weren't expecting to do this. This wasn't on their roadmap. And John Mackey called them up and said, you know, would you be willing to buy us? And so you're Bezos and you're Wilkie and you ask yourself, okay, you know, I've got to make a decision in three weeks. Am I willing to do that? And, you know, if you have a $500 billion market cap and you can borrow $14 billion, you know, overnight and groceries already in your roadmap, maybe the answer is yes. So, anyhow, I mean, I think the consumer packaged goods world is super interesting. I don't know. Did anyone see the article about brandless yesterday? You know, so that's a company we talked to a bunch. You know, for those of you who don't know, every item is $3. And they're trying to promote subscription. And it's interesting how they say it's brandless, but they really are trying to build a brand, as best as I can tell. And they've kind of positioned it somewhere between it's better for you and higher quality ingredients than the traditional manufacturers, but it's cheaper than Whole Foods 365 brand. So, I think, you know, that's super interesting. And you now, in the last couple of years, had Dollar Shave Club. And, you know, here in town, It Cosmetics, a bunch of different companies have been purchased. And I get back to, I mean, think about Dollar Shave Club. Did any of the dudes in this room have Dollar Shave razors? Not one? Oh, not one. How were the razors? Were they great? Why'd you use it? So, you know, that guy was brilliant in kind of creating a brand. That was him. And, you know, we all shared that video a few years ago. Really super good marketing. But, you know, if you're Unilever, you have no idea who your real customers are. And here's a startup company that's got, you know, 100,000 subscribers or whatever. Wow. I mean, directionally, if you can't go in that world, you're going to perish. I think how many businesses are cutting out the middleman in the consumer world is super interesting. You know, we're an investor in a company called Everlane. Anyone buy their stuff? Okay, great. You know, and people like the concept of radical transparency. Why do you have all these middlemen? So, I think there's a ton of stuff going on. Some of it based on technology. And in the consumer world, some of it based on, you know, we haven't even gotten to cars. That's a whole other world. That's a whole other world. I would say business models utilizing software are cutting out the middleman. Absolutely. We see that all the time. Yeah, Tesla would be a good example. You know, I mean, Allbirds, as an example, they were asked. So, they launched in March of 2016, and they got a ton of press saying that it was the most comfortable shoe in the world. And as a result of that, Shinola, which is a brand, asked them to do a pop-up store in the Shinola store in San Francisco. And that went really well. So, now they opened their first store. So, think about starting a shoe company 20 years ago. You'd go to Nordstrom as the premier account, the validator. And if you're going to sell Allbirds or $95, you'd sell a $95 shoe to Nordstrom for $47.50. And we don't really talk about the cogs, but let's just assume that if Allbirds opens its own store, they can use social media to drive people into the store. And instead of selling that shoe for $47.50, they're selling it for $95. Completely changes the margin structure of what they can do in terms of building that product. So, yeah. Now, ultimately, if it gets really big, is it going to have to go to retail? Sure. But it's going to go to retail well down the line when it has, you know, a lot of momentum. Hold your question. Just hold the question. Yeah. To the end. So, when you go to an entrepreneur and, you know, say you've met with them once or twice, and you think it's something that you're still talking to, so you have an interest in it, what is the one or two questions that you want to know? Excuse me. What are the one or two answers you want to know from that entrepreneur about his or her company? What is it that she or he can tell you that's critically important to hear from that person? I don't think it's really one or two things. I would say much more than many other VCs, we are focused when we invest early on the people. Some VCs are successful in saying they're going to find something where the technology is so great that tossing the people wouldn't be a problem. We've not had that experience, the reboot costs of going through the pain of founding CEO leaving and recruiting and all that. So, the first thing is really understanding the people and the team. The second is really understanding the product differentiation. How is it different than everyone else's? And the third is understanding the ambition. And that can relate to TAM, but, you know, there was no Uber TAM when you were investing in black cars. So, the TAM turns out to be a whole lot bigger than what everyone thought. So, I think it's, I met with a niche, a guy yesterday who had a particular niche, frankly, for cats. And I'm not a cat guy. And he said, I know you're not a cat guy. You're a dog guy, right? And I said, yes, I am. But then he kind of very thoughtfully laid out, well, here's what the first product is going to do for cats and here's what the next products are going to do. And then, here's how it transfers to dogs. And, oh, by the way, there's 160 million, as you know from Chupanian, cats and dogs in America. And so, it was interesting that he kind of brought me along a journey where he convinced me, first, that he could get share in his entry category. Cats. This is something we talk to entrepreneurs a lot. People value companies, in my mind, most cases, based on share, not based on breadth. So, one of the things we see a lot of other investors, a lot of the question is, how broadly should you go to new products and how quickly? And, in my experience, when I talk to the people who I know at Unilever, as an example, why did you buy Dollar Shave Club? They proved that they could get share in the razor category. And, if you could, that's more important than proving that there are three other categories that you can get insignificant sales. So, it was really interesting listening to this entrepreneur tell the story and then give me the data of how this is what we focused on and this is the exponential growth. And, down the road, we'll focus on this and here's why we have license to go there. I mean, I think, you know, Amazon, Starbucks, these companies are this big because they were fortunate enough to kind of hit a well of their first thing but then test and learn always where the consumer gives them license to go. Yeah, yeah, exactly. And, that story you're talking about, the entrepreneur telling, how important is it for the team to be able to tell the story? I mean, here's, and I ask that question because I get, you know, a fair amount of people asking me to sit and have a coffee with them about their company, blah, blah, blah, and many, many times I hear no story. I don't hear, like, it's just facts and figures. There's no, like, story behind it. And, how do you think, like, does that affect the growth of that company? Does that affect anything, not being able to tell the story or a story? Well, I thought you were saying all members on the team, and I don't think it's necessary. One, somebody's going to tell a story, I'm assuming. It may not be the CEO. It could be somebody else. I'm just saying someone from the team. I think building a company is storytelling. Think about it. One of the criteria that I outlined before is the ability to attract people who logically shouldn't go to such a small company. What are you doing? You're selling a story to somebody else about what this could be. You know, Howard sold Oren Smith, who at the time was budget director of the state of Washington, to come in and be the CFO of a company that had 30 stores called Starbucks Coffee Company. You don't get somebody who had that platform by telling them, you know, I'm going to open up 100 stores. And so absolutely, I think the CEO and the founder is the most visible person when he or she walks in to the office every day that connotes all sorts of stuff. I mean, we had a, I had a coach come in and help me at Mavron. And one of the things he told me, the coach told me after talking to everyone on our team was, you know, once a month, you come in grouchy and everyone hates it. And, and the productivity goes down that day because of the way you're acting. And you can be a person who has feelings, but you can't, when you walk in the door, if you're the leader, you have to, as a professional, you have to act that way. And I think every CEO has to realize that. Bringing the vicissitudes of life into the workplace, I think is a, it's easy to say it. It's harder to do it. But, you know, you've got to, not only you've got to tell a story, but you've got to walk the walk. Walk the walk. What word did you just use? Vicissitudes. What's that mean? Ups and downs. Thank you very much. Ups and downs. That's good. For a startup guy, you should know that word. Yeah, yeah. Yeah, I just use ups and downs. So, so let's talk about something that you and I just briefly talked about. It is, it's a touchy subject, you know, what's going on within the venture community, specifically, you know, the sexual harassment and things like that. Like, it's just, it's interesting because you and I, and Michelle and I were talking about it before as well. Could you give us the two, just can you give us an elevator what you think is, what is that, where's it coming from? We know it's been going on for, you know, I'm going to say hundreds of years, as long as there's been humans on the planet. But now we're starting to talk about it, which I think is great, obviously, but, like, what's going to happen? What do you think is going to, what's going to be the outcome of this over the next two, three years? Are we going to get the right outcome, I guess is a good question? Are we going to get that? Well, I think there's arguments to be had that you've had a better outcome already than where you were before. You know, I think this Masters of the Universe mentality is prevalent in a number of industries. I was on Wall Street in the 80s, and everyone in New York who was an investment banker. I think when you start thinking of yourself as omnipotent and successful, as opposed to just lucky in a point in time, you rewrite some of the rules. And I think it's, I mean, I thought I understood, maybe this will sound naive, but I thought I understood the typical VC startup stuff. And I would say some of the allegations, and now they seem to be facts, of repeated offenses and using financing as a weapon. I think that's hard to believe. And, you know, we have a number of females on our team, and we knew this story was coming out. We just didn't know when. And I think to the credit of the information, they spent a whole lot of time researching. And, you know, once you talk to six people, you know, it's pretty convincing. So I think we're in a better place now. I think it's got to continue. I'm not sure where it goes. One of the things that we've tried to talk about is, as a firm, what should we do, and what should our manifesto or our true north commitment be? I will say that I think Mavron's benefited from having diversity and females around our investment table. We've got the best. I feel like I can go to my partners and ask for help and understanding on investments better now than ever because we've got more diversity now than we ever had. And, you know, we're also investing in consumer businesses. So it always was interesting to me how many partnerships you're investing in a business that's 80% women. And many partnerships don't have anyone sitting at the table. And I think, you know, some of the best VCs have now kind of realized that that's not a good equilibrium. So directionally, I think we're better off. Where does this all end up? I don't know. But I think the most interesting thing that's happened is we've gone from, well, first of all, I would say, how could you not admire the women who came forward, particularly the ones who spoke publicly? I admire that a lot. And that's courageous. I think we've also begun to get into a culture now where if you know it's a problem and you've tolerated it, then by definition, you're guilty. And I think that's probably healthy. So, but it's interesting to watch it in the entrepreneur world also because we've gone from Travis is the ideal entrepreneur to Travis is the horror of an entrepreneur very quick. And, you know, I think, I would say that I think we need people who are disruptive and willing to break a lot of glass to create great companies. Somehow, though, they need to kind of have a modulation. And for some people, that's hard. Yeah, that's not easy. That's not easy. Yeah, that's something that actually there was more than one person in this room. So, you know, I'd love to hear, like, what the thoughts are around that. So thanks for sharing that because that is just an interesting topic. So, you know, I was a board member of a public company. And we had an African-American CEO, and he wanted more diversity on the board. And while I was lead director, we recruited a number of people, three new people, no, four new people. And two of them were African-American females. They brought a really good perspective to that board that the rest of us needed to hear. And, you know, you see that time and time again. Yeah, no, it's glad now that it's actually, the white elephant in the room is actually being talked about, which is great to see. So we've got about 10, 12, 15 minutes. So open it up for questions. Do you have a question? I have a question about the lifetime of a VP firm kind of makes the time to an end that kind of artificially short in the company. So you were talking about building a company, the volunteer sector, and that's the 10-year lifetime of a fund. Sure. So let me read the question. So he's asking about the life of a fund. There is some, actually, some milestones that happen within that fund. Well, every, or typical VC fund is a 10-year fund. The first two to five years you make new commitments, and it's a 10-year fund with three one-year extensions. That's the way Maveron works. What I would say is, by year six, seven, or eight, we typically know, do we think this company, by year two or three or four, we typically know, does it have what we call VC optionality? By year seven or eight or nine, we kind of have a pretty good idea of what we think is the likely outcome. Sometimes we're wrong. But I think that, I'm not sure I'm a buyer, guyer, of that it's the VC time constraint. It's not the fund lifetime that forces people to be, to push entrepreneurs to move faster. It's the hits nature of the business and the need to put up numbers. So, you know, you hit that J-curve in the first year or two, and the best firms are hoping that they don't. And I think it's more the impulsiveness of trying to manage returns than it is the 10-year problem. So, Trupanion, we still own two-thirds of our position in Trupanion. It's in a 2004 fund, and our institutional investors said, Dan, you've been on the board 10 years. Whatever you think, let us know. And when you think it's time to distribute, great, and there's no rush. Now, you know, that's one that's worked. But I don't think it's the lifetime of the fund that's the problem, is, I guess, my point. And most funds are now extending, now. More than, what, one year up? Oh, yeah. You know, I mean, we have one company left in our 2,000 fund. Now, the LPs say, we explain, what do you want us to do? You know, and they say, keep managing it, and, you know, let's try and get out. And we say, we're trying. Yeah. Go ahead. The first question, did you have an opportunity to invest in Uber? You mentioned it earlier. We, anyone had an opportunity to invest in Uber because it was on AngelList. Oh. But no, we didn't, we didn't see it. Okay. And the other question is, you said that in many given years, there's about 1,500 companies that's a consumer business like you could invest in, and there's probably only five good ones. How do you go about ensuring that you have a fair shot at evaluating all of them? I hear VCs say that they only listen to people that say in buyout and production. So how do you cover those 15-year opportunities? Do you see people are now doing this in production? I'm just wondering what the results are of that. Well, I think you try to put yourself in the position where if you've built a brand that people want, hopefully, A, the vast majority of those 1,500 or whatever the number is are going to come to you. B, the I only want to talk to references is partly a screen to see how resourceful the entrepreneur is. Once I invest in you, I want to make sure that you can get to people, that you can recruit people, that you can find the best in breed companies in that sector and get to know them. So if you can't figure out a way to get to me, you're kind of, you've probably failed the test. And I know that sometimes that's hard, but in this world of LinkedIn and everything else, going to the partners and going to LinkedIn and trying to find someone. Now, you know, is there a one in a 10,000 where somebody hasn't been around and is brand new? Perhaps. But I would say that in general, like, for instance, I'm always impressed by how our best companies are finding people, and hopefully we're introducing many of them, but on their own, they're finding people as resources to help their company. They're finding people who have been there, who have done that before them. And so I think that's just another test. Yeah. And, you know, just a couple of things there, because this question gets asked me a lot just because I know a lot of people, right? So every VC does this, every one of them. If you can't get to them via some human being other than them, then you are failing, because everyone can do that. There's way too many people in this world, and it's just a little work. So that is the wrong thing to think. Like, you can always get to some other human being by going to these events, you know, LinkedIn people. I mean, that should be just the first step of the process. And every VC I've ever met, that's what they use it for. It's a filter. Particularly the world we live in, which is so connected. Yeah. 100% we do outbound. 100%. I think once you start waiting for what comes, by definition, you're complacent. You know, candidly, every person has a slightly different orientation of how they do outbound. Some are much more analytical. Some are much more networking and people. But, you know, the 20 and something, 20 and 30 something people that we have in our firm, they're meeting five to eight companies a day. And some of that is inbound and a lot of it is outbound. There's all sorts of data sources that are out there now that shows when companies start getting credit card traction and things like that. So we do some of that. And, you know, you build a network as a VC of people that you've invested with before and who are hopefully out there sourcing for you. Yeah, absolutely. Go ahead. Thank you. So I've got a question for you. So back in 1993, when Starbucks went public. 92. 92. You asked Howard Schultz, you know, you're having fun and I'm not. What's up? Right. You said, well, you don't put yourself in a position to win. So that was interesting. When I read that, it got me thinking. So first question, are you having fun? Absolutely. More than ever, thankfully. What did that mean to you first? Second of all, what did you do to put yourself in a position to win? And then what do you define when you did? Sure. Well, I think the first question that Howard forced me to, and I don't know, how many people have heard that story? Can you fill up the background? Yeah, sure. The background of the story is I was sitting with Howard in 1993, a year after the company went public. And it was still a super tiny company, $400 million market cap. And I said to Howard, how come you're having all the fun? You seem to be loving this. I'm traveling. I'm an investment banker. On the one hand, I'm getting paid a lot more than you. But on the other hand, I didn't feel like I was happy. And he said, well, you haven't put yourself in a position to win. And there were two implications of that. Really understanding, not for him, but for me, what winning was. And I think this is a thing in life that, frankly, I try to get my kids to think about a lot. I have a 15 and a 17-year-old. What's winning for you? Not winning for anyone else, but really forcing yourself to ask, what do you do well? Not what do you wish you did well? And so I think getting in touch with what you do well and what you like and what winning would be is the first step. And then the second... This was before you joined Starbucks or after you joined... I never joined Starbucks. This was while I was an investment banker. Okay. But here I am working with Starbucks, and they're a product company. They sell a physical product. And I was really impressed by the way they treated their people. And I was in an investment banking firm. And in the continuum of investment banking firms, I was in a good one. But I was not impressed when I compared it to Starbucks. So I came to understand that part of winning for me was having that psychology of treating people in a different way. So it opened my mind up to how do I find that. And I think in terms of how do you put yourself in a position to win, you have to take chance and you have to take risks. And Howard's whole thing to me when he told me the story was, you know, his father-in-law said to him, Howard, you've been at this for a few years. It's really not working. It's super hard to raise money. You know, why are you doing this? And kind of his father-in-law kind of gave him the impression that he was taking his daughter down the wrong path. And, you know, Howard just kind of realized that it was a risky thing. But if it worked, and no one has certainty that any of these things work, if it worked, he would, in his mind, win. So I think risk is harder to make bold moves the later on in life you are. I've observed that. I've had tens of investment bankers, maybe, no, I'd say tens, of 40 to 50-year-old investment bankers say, you know, I hate this, but I don't have the courage to leave. Help me. And I'm like, you know, you've got to do it yourself. But I think you don't want to get to a point where you're 40 or 50 years old, and you're either living someone else's life, or you're not doing the combination of what you love and what you do well. So I think it tends to be easier to take risks earlier. And, you know, as an MBA, I was taught not to take risks. Right? I went to HBS, and they try to tell you to analyze everything. And, you know, would the HBS framework have led me at 40 years old to quit my job that was pretty nice in terms of W-2 income and move across the country with no clarity on what I was going to do at age 40? No. But I kind of had that gut that said I was going to take a shot. So, you know, I frequently talk about a poem called Man in the Glass, which is a poem that was written about 100 years ago. And it basically says that there's only one person that you have to be true to when you're later in life, and that's the person you're looking at in the mirror. And make sure you don't cheat that person. Because if you've cheated that person, you really hurt yourself. Go ahead. I was just kind of wondering, do you think that the hashing aspect of being a form is important, but at the same time, it sounds like you said that the ability to pivot is also important, because it kind of seems like those might have a trade-off. Is there, if so, is there any way of noticing the floor itself or what I'm trying to do with one or is this together? The way I try to help people think about this is, 100%, I think, great brands are built by a combination of heart and brain. And if you're starting a consumer business now, you should architect it from the very beginning to measure everything. And you should have 100% passion about the choices you pick up front. But you need to test and learn. And if you're six months in and your hypothesis is, I should have sold a lot of this and I'm not selling it, then you have to look at the data. And you have to ask yourself why. And you have to be able to pivot. And I think the best entrepreneurs are completely passionate, but they're open to being kind of proven wrong. And so I think it's a data speaks kind of thing. It doesn't have to speak in the first week or the first month. But most of the time when we see businesses that aren't working, you know, over a six to 12-month period, okay, the data speaks. Now what are we going to do to change? And re-ignite that passion when you've tweaked it? Does that answer the question? Kind of, I mean, I mean, how much data is? Does it come back to the important numbers or is it based on analytics and kind of forecasts? Well, I think it should be both. You know, when you back somebody, it's like, okay, what are you going to do in the next year? And if you do a tenth of that, why? And just believing that you're going to make a small tweak. I have a situation now with one of our companies where love the team, love the market, couldn't be more pleased with the results, but we've had a hard time selling it. And we just all need to step back and say, we came in thinking it was this, and we've got to be open-minded and broaden our opportunities to test how the hell we're going to sell it. And that includes changing the product, it includes changing the price point, and it includes changing the channels that we're selling it through. And I think part of a good venture capitalist is to thoughtfully work with a team to ask questions. Ultimately, it's the team that typically needs to find the right strategy, but our job is to be thought-provoking, supportive, but being willing to say, you know, hey, we've been looking at this, we've been looking at this for X, and here's what we got. Does this make sense? One more question, guys. Come back. What's your strategy in using social media platforms like Instagram to YouTube for your YouTube? So that's a very good question, and candidly, we're spending a bunch of time talking about that now. Like how to use it? You know, we live in a world now where you can create your brand in ways that are different than ever before. And we vacillate between letting our brand speak for itself in terms of the help we give to entrepreneurs and the results of the company, and then feeling like it's our job to be more visible. So I think we are looking at what a lot of our competitors are doing and trying to figure out what is our differentiation. There's a great book called Different, which was written by a woman named Youngmi Moon, who's one of our advisors, HBS prof, and she talks about brands, and she talks about the willingness of companies to pick a point of differentiation and really trumpet it. And three years ago when we came up with consumer-only VC as a tagline, one of the concerns some of the people around our table had was what are we giving up, you know, opportunistically? Are we going to lose stuff, and I think the answer to that is with high certainty we're going to get different deal flow. But if our job is to find 1,500 companies a year, we're going to evaluate the 1,500 relevant companies better. And if we communicate to CEOs that all we do is early-stage consumer, we're going to get a better view of that. So I think social media is an extension of articulating and penetrating that brand presence. And, you know, I mean, some of the best firms who have multiple exits don't really need to do that because they can use earned media to do that. Yeah, but you guys have been by, I don't know if it's by strategy, but you guys have been fairly quiet. I mean, you don't necessarily tell anyone. Like, yeah, you just, right. I mean, just, I would say that if we look on the outside. I don't, I don't hear, like, like, you just don't, like, people say who's Maveron and you're like, really? Well, you know, I'll think about that. Last question. No, no, no, no. Here we go. One question, we're done. I'm very interested in finding out how you view the subject matter expertise in the consumer space, in a CEO, as you know, you know, in cloud companies, enterprise and all that, you know, you have to have the pedigree and all that other stuff, right? But in consumer, how do you, how do you, how do you kind of really measure whether that CEO got it or not? I mean, I would think that it would be less relevant, but I would like to know what were those, either instinctive or actually, you know, factual or material criteria that you would view, sorry, consider to sort of, you know, evaluate the CEO as well as the other team. I've changed as I've been doing this for 19 years. I've been doing this for 19 years, my thoughts on this have kind of evolved, and I would say the most disruptive CEO's typically aren't clouded by past experience. The shoe guys at all birds, one was a professional soccer player from New Zealand, and the other was a business development guy in sustainable materials. What they have done is recruit a level below them of tremendous pattern recognition. So they just recruited the guy who ran the supply chain for Under Armour. So your seven years at Under Armour, you saw a lot in the growth of that company, and you come into all birds, which is 16 months old. The amount that you can help that team, astronomical, and, you know, we haven't really talked about how great a recruiter is the founding team, but I was super impressed whether or not it's board members or company C-level guys and gals that this team can recruit. So I would say that's one side of the coin. On the other side, you know, one of our most successful in terms of dollar-created company was Zulily, and here's two entrepreneurs who did Blue Nile, understood everything there was to understand about e-commerce, and kind of took what they had learned at Blue Nile and repositioned it against, you know, a new business model of flash sales for moms and kids. And in that particular case, that worked out very well. So I think it's, it's not a black or white question. If they have industry experience, it's, are they always talking about the rules in the industry or are they trying to break the rules? And if they don't have it, it's, can they recruit people who can really inform the team how things are typically done, but rethink it. There's a great Daryl Cabin story that I like to tell on Zulily. Daryl's working out of our office and he comes up with this, we had introduced them to a company called One King's Lane and we decided, they decided that that wasn't a fit. And so Daryl decides he's going to take a look at a, at a flash sale, different vertical, and he comes upon kids. And so he's working his way through the economics and he starts talking to photographers. And I don't know how many of you are familiar with the Zulily model, but they take hundreds of events a day. And so the cost to photograph the merchandise is a huge element of the cost to deliver the service. And Daryl talks to professional photographers who told them, you know, you can't shoot it for under, I forget what the number was, but it was a super high number. And Daryl knew that the business model didn't work if it was that he ended up doing it for a tenth of what the industry insiders told them was the cost. And that's just taking a fresh approach and being disruptive. So here he is in e-commerce, which is a category he knows, but really being disruptive. And I think it's that mix and match that is critical. Awesome. Well, thank you guys for being here.
