interviewee_bio: David Israel is the Founder of BFY Food Group .
video_title: Mike Grabham, Startup Grind Eastside w/ David Israel, CEO/Founder BFY Foods
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Grind. Without any further ado, I'd like to welcome David Israel, our guest tonight. That's what made you run, right? Where do you want me? Thank you. It's funny, David, because there's been a couple guests where that was the scariest part of the night. I was like, oh no, I don't want these people to have to do that. So yeah, that's what we do. But again, for those of you who have not been to the Startup Grind, it really is just a conversation between David and I. It's just a discussion about his experiences, what he's done, obviously around startups and starting a company or companies because he's started several. And so we'll have an interview for 45-ish minutes, and then we'll have 15 minutes of Q&A. So hold your questions at the end. Feel free to get up, grab wine, beer, food, drink. You're not going to interrupt us. Obviously, it's videotaped, so it'll be online in about a week or so, 10 days. Yeah, that's it. So again, thanks for coming. I'm glad to be here. Honored to be here. Appreciate it. Yeah, yeah. So the reason why we connected was, one, you have an interesting background. I do. But two, the volume of work that you've done is pretty interesting. You know, why not, you know, I don't necessarily care about some of the things like I did specifically, but kind of give us a general idea of the last three or four companies that you have started and have grown. I mean, the last two have been, you're still working on actually. We're still working on quite a few. I mean, I think my biggest, so my first big company was called Pond Exchange. It's not one that I'm really proud of today. I mean, it was a very successful company. It did exactly what I wanted to do. And kind of, I mean, can I take a step back? So when I was about, oh, I think I was 26 or 27, and my wife and I had just been married, we moved, we came back to Seattle, and a friend of mine said, who had sold a large chain of jewelry stores, he asked me to come in and be his partner. And I knew nothing really about the business, but we opened up, or he opened up, in a place called Aurora Village at the time. Back in the- So this was what year? 88? Late, late 80s. 89? Yeah. And we moved in, and everybody else started moving out. Nordstrom's closed, what's called Frederick and Nelson's, which is another great department store here. They moved out, Sears shut down, everybody's leaving, and it's becoming this ghost town. And for me, at the time, I mean, I had made some money doing some deals, and I put everything I had into this thing. And I was like, oh my God, this is not good. So, turns out, you know, we signed a five-year lease, and we were there for maybe nine months or a year. And we're, like, the last four months, I'm just, like, ready to pull my hair out, my wife's ready to kill me. And, well, our five-year lease turned out to be worth a lot, because everybody's moving out because they're not renewing leases, because they want to build a Costco and a Home Depot there. Oh, really? Yes. So, all of a sudden, you know, the developer comes to us and says, hey, you know what, we need to take you out of your lease. So, I have no idea what I'm doing. My partner, who's from a very successful family, he really didn't know what he was doing either, but he was pretty arrogant. So, he put a price tag out there, and you know what? They took it. So, I turned out, you know, walking away with a pretty good chunk of money for a guy who was about to lose everything, and all these jewelry stores started to call me up. Thought you knew what you were doing. No, they wanted to borrow money. Because jewelry stores, if you're not a major chain, you're a family-run business, and it's really difficult to get financing, because inventory control, management, I mean, banks aren't really financing you. Interesting. So, they came to me, and I started to loan them money, but I wanted collateral, because I didn't trust them. That led to coin shops, then precious metal dealers, and then pawn shops, and I was loaning money to pawn shops, well, and all these other folks up and down the I-5 corridor, and I was like, how do these guys make money? They treat their customers like crap. Their stores look like crap. Everything's overpriced. I mean, their hours suck, and it was one day I read in Fortune Magazine about this company. There was a company called Cash America out of Dallas, Texas that went public, and they were getting all these accolades, and they were buying old mom-and-pop shops and consolidating them and turning them into retail presents, right? Shirts and ties, you know, really nice stores, you know, everything was priced right, and they were going haywire, and they said they were going to grow. So I was like, well, this is something I could do. So I wrote a business plan with the help of Deloitte Touche, and then decided that's what I was into. Went down to Cash America, looked at all their stores in Dallas, you know, took pictures, came back, replicated it here. And, look, I just thought, at the time, I knew it was a slam-dunk easy deal, kind of brainless. It wasn't. I mean, I didn't know what I was doing, so I could loan money on jewelry, but my first loan happened to be a Sawzall. So I was like, uh-oh. You know, how do I test that to see if it's real, right? So, anyway, it did exactly what I wanted. The goal was to build this chain and sell it to Cash America, and we built it from one store in 1993. We had about 16 stores by 1996. You know, Howard Schultz, Jeff Brodman, all the, the majority of all the top executives at Starbucks and Costco and their boards were my investors. So I raised about $4 million in 96, and I encountered a little hiccup, but that later, about eight years later, nine years later, sold to Cash America for about $80 million. So it did exactly what I wanted, but there were some other things along the way. Yeah, exactly. So, so tell us, like, in that process of, like, the sale of the company, which is always a, it's a, I mean, I went through three myself. They're super hard. They're super frustrating normally. Like, what were the things that you could have done better? Well, we could have held out, number one. Well, sure. Because the value of our business was getting stronger. But the thing was, is by the nine years later or 10 years later, I had stepped aside because of, which I'm sure we'll get to in cover, some hiccups in my process there. Look, I think we did everything exactly right, except hold out for, you know, hold our ground for a higher dollar number. Yeah. So you, you just, other than that, you would have, like, just said, you know, let's wait, wait a few more years. It was so profitable. I mean, that business is very lucrative if you operate properly. We made it even more profitable because we were managing our, our money and our finances. And we figured out the formula of how to really be successful was, I mean, loans are great. I mean, I just wanted people to know what was coming. I captured their interest. And you know what? They say, "When can you ship?" I can... You place an order. I'll have the bags here in eight weeks. It's off to you. Okay? We just did things really quickly. They didn't like that. I had other people that said, you can't... You know, when I was in O Magazine, okay? Literally a day after it came out, I get a call. And I'm in this really small place in Renton. And I answer the phone. And it's, "Hi, who would I speak to about placing?" I said, "Well, I can help you." "Okay. This is, you know, this is Elizabeth with TJ Maxx. And I want to order your popcorn." I saw it in O Magazine. I'm like, "Okay." I'm like, "Who is this?" "Who's pulling the joke?" "Yeah, this is..." I go, "You guys don't carry food." She goes, "We carry lots of food." I go, "You do?" She goes, "And we have 2,500 stores that I'll put you in." I go, "Okay." She goes, "Can you deliver 2,500 stores?" I go, "Absolutely." "No." We have a loaning doc. I mean, we went from, at that time, we went from 11 employees to 30 employees, like literally in a week, because I took the order. I hung up, and this person I had hired who had 30 years' experience, she came from Starbucks, said, "You can't sell it to TJ Maxx." I said, "Well, why not?" She goes, "Well, if you sell to TJ Maxx, it's a discounter. None of the other specialty stores will want to buy you." I go, "Wow." I go, "Well, I'll tell you what." We'll give it a shot. "No." I said, "You have two hours to come back with a replacement order for 2,500 stores, and I won't ship them." And she goes, "Well, that's not possible." I go, "You're right." So we shipped them, and you know what happened? Honest to God. I mean, she was like, "You're going to be sorry. You're going to be sorry." I'm like, "You know what?" Won't even use her name. I said, "I want to sell product, okay? I have investors. I want to get this product out there. I'm not saying no to 2,500 stores." First of all, our margins were better than any other place at TJ Maxx because they paid more. We don't have a distributor, and they picked it up. So I didn't have freight. I was in the stores a week, and I started getting phone calls from Dean and DeLuca because their buyer was in TJ Maxx and said, "We want to carry your product." And I started getting buyers that way from TJ Maxx, and it grew that way organically. And distributors. I mean, it just took off. She was dead wrong. So I was so ignorant. I didn't know. I just wanted to do business. That way a business worked. Nobody discounted me. I mean, as far as because I was in TJ Maxx. They didn't care. And if you go to TJ Maxx now, everybody's in there. There's not that fear of being in there. Dilettante, they want to grow that business. They're great partners. So I'm hearing what you're saying about trust is like, one, you really trust yourself. Have to. And you really trust when you've got a good feeling about something and stuff happens, and you're excited about it, and you want to do it, you put your word on doing it. You're just going to do it. Yeah. And if somebody can bring something that's more exciting than that and bring you a better feeling that's also exciting to you, yeah, I think you'll go with that. But if they're just like raining on your parade. Yeah. Like, hey. Look, it's your dream. And just let me clarify this a bit. Okay. The relationships I have with companies, those are different, okay? You know, I mean, now I'm dealing with crafts and Kellogg's and stuff like that. And all these different, they're all individual. You have to respect how they do business, okay? And you build a mutual trust and it's based on integrity, okay? Hoi Fung is obviously a unique one. So I've learned a lot. People that you're bringing into your own culture have to be a good fit. They need to earn your trust. It's a two-way street, right? I mean, the four guys that are with me now, well, one just got out of prison. I met him in an entrepreneurial program that I put on there. So he's there and he's a -- I keep saying he's a killer. He's not a killer, but he's killing it, okay? I mean -- He's killing it. Yeah. I go, this guy is a killer. I'm like, whoa, really? I'm like, no, no. I didn't mean it that way. The other three guys are people that have worked with me from almost day one at Pop Grommet. One was my VP of sales. He came with me. The other one was -- his name is Sachin. He was my second employee. He grew into a VP position. He's there with me. And the other woman, she was like my third employee. So we built trust. And I know that they're going to support me. And they know that I'm just going to go do it. I mean, they can laugh at me a lot. I mean, I got a lot of quirks, right? I mean, they're like, you can't do that. I'm like, well, I'm going to try it. So they get who I am. But we get who each other are and we support each other to be successful. That's what you need, okay? I mean, resumes -- sorry. You know, I think the most important -- our resume is important in financial, you know, CFOs. But they also have to support your goals. They have to understand your goals, your culture. It's very important, right? You can't just trust guys and say, all the guys that said they knew how to run a plant -- I mean, I might as well just shot myself. We don't even have a plant. PopCormade does not have a production plant anymore. Okay? We built four. And the last one cost us about $9 million. Down the toilet. Okay? I trusted the wrong people. I believed in resumes. It doesn't work. Can we add on a high note? Sure. Can you talk to me about some of the rock stars, the experiences that you've had with people? Well, you know what? I mean, literally can run around and pinch myself with the guys that -- the people that I had brought in that never had jobs or their first jobs out of high school, a lot of these guys that were transitioning are so intelligent they just never knew how to direct that energy and how to apply it or no one would ever trust them. One of my lead investors, his son, he dropped out of college. I mean, he's a very wealthy family. He's like, "Hey, will you give him a shot?" I said, "Sure." The guy came to me. He goes, "Look, my dad's friend over here put me in the warehouse. My dad's friend over there had me doing clerk jobs." I go, "Not here. It works the same way here for everybody. I want you to try to get involved in everything. And we're going to figure out where your strengths are. And that's how we're going to apply it." So I think I see a lot of people go on and do great things. One of the guys went on and he's on the board at What's Next. And he was the first job he ever had outside of prison. And now he's helped running an organization. So just the connections that I've made across the board. Me learning not to be fearful of who I am or being insecure or having an ego. I mean, you know, ego should just go out the window, right? I just love doing this and I love connecting with people, which is why when I go to trade shows, I am behind that booth. I mean, I just go, I leave literally. The only reason I leave is to go to the restroom, okay? I don't walk around the shows. I like connecting with the buyers. I like collecting with it because I like the feedback. I mean, it's thrilling to me. So, you know, just learning how to deal with people and all the people that have grown with me, it's amazing. It really is. I mean, this whole thing, I travel around the world and it's just, I mean, I never thought, I mean, who would think I have partners in a plant in Russia or building a plant in India or these guys in Japan? I mean, I was out of prison, you know, where nobody even wanted to talk to me, okay? I was isolated. And, you know, it's just a great place to see that you can change your life. It's up to you, right? It doesn't matter the cards you're dealt, where you come from, what you've been through, what anybody thinks of you. If you decide to live a good life and do things right and you want to be successful and you put your, you know, you're really committed and honest and have integrity, you know? It all works out. It sure does. Yeah, absolutely works out. Well, David, you've been absolutely amazing. Well, thank you. Awesome. I appreciate your sharing that story. It's a great one and just your honesty and forthrightness. It's amazing. Thank you. Thank you, Mike. You're awesome. It's great to be here. Yeah, thanks. Thank you.

video_title: Mike Grabham, Startup Grind Eastside w/ Hansen Hosein, Journalist/Story Teller
transcript_text: Welcome to the Startup Grind. So without any further ado, I'd like to welcome our guest tonight, Hanson Hosen. Come on up, Hanson. Come on. Thank you, sir. It works. Thanks very much. Thank you. It does work. I feel awake. Yes. We do that for some, I was telling Hanson, we do that for a couple reasons. One, because most of our guests deserve it. I say most. And two, it's always good to just add a little energy to the room right before we sit down in our little comfy chairs. Yeah, I do a lot of this onstage stuff, and I always see it as a transference of energy between the audience. And you have to compete with everybody on their devices, too, right? So you have to make sure that they're tuned in somehow. Yeah, the smartphones. Compete with the smartphone. So, again, for the people who have not gone to a, you know, been to a Startup Grind event, this is really, truly an interview. It's just two people talking, you know, we're talking about some of his journey, you know, how does it relate to starting companies, you know, why maybe he made certain decisions and why he didn't make certain decisions. So it really is that just really open discussion. And then we'll have the last 15 minutes, 10, 15 minutes, we'll have an open Q&A, so feel free to answer. And we have a, what's cool about here is we have a cool little mic that we can just toss at you, so someone has to catch this thing. This is so cool. I don't know how you guys have seen this yet, but this really is cool. So, yeah. So you have to ask a question, I can throw that at you. It's a mic, by the way. So, yeah, so that's the last 10 or 15 minutes, so hold your questions until the end. Feel free to get up and whine. You're not going to interrupt anything, beer or anything. So, you know, just relax and enjoy yourself. So, Hanson, first of all, thanks for being here. Hey, Mike, thanks for inviting me. I think you booked me back in August, I think. Way back. You really planned far ahead. I try to plan far ahead because it takes a lot of pressure off me. Yeah. Yeah, the further ahead I do it, I don't have to worry about it, right? Well, I had five UPS boxes on my front porch today, and none of them were stolen, but I do have a camera to keep an eye out on who's driving. Yeah, we recommend getting a camera and a package guard, two things. That's the perfect deterrent for package theft. Well, so, I mean, you've had an extremely interesting background. I mean, this is, you're probably one of, definitely an outlier in our graph of people we've had on stage. I'm not Russell Wilson, though. No, you're not Russell Wilson. But probably interesting, more interesting story than Russell Wilson, actually. You know, tell us just some, you know, you talk about Drop the Mic, which I really like. You did a TED Talk on that, I think, or, I mean, I read something about it, and then I saw a little snippet of a video. Tell us, like, some of the things that, why you dropped the mic, and you might, you know, kind of give some context to the group. Well, just to give you a context about this, I gave a, I've given a number of TEDx presentations over the last few years, and the most recent one was at Oregon State University in front of 1,200 people. And I was, I, just like this event, I was wondering why on earth I was invited. I mean, like, what else, what do I actually have to say? And so I decided, you know, I'm a former journalist, I used to work for NBC News, I'm a documentary filmmaker, I'm supposed to know what communication is all about. And so I fundamentally always ask myself, not what is my message and who is my audience. My question is always, how can I serve my audience? You know, what do they need right now that I can be of any value to them? And so that talk was thinking, well, probably the predominant demographic in that audience was going to be millennial, probably recent graduates of college. And the thing that I could probably deliver most to them was some kind of professional advice. You know, what could I tell them that they would not hear from anybody else? And just before that, somebody had told me, you know what, just looking at what you do, the thing that you do the most is that you know how to drop the mic. And I had no idea what that meant. I'm part of that generation that drop the mic doesn't mean anything to me. So I looked it up and I asked them, they said, no, it means that you're, you know, when you're done, you can leave in a high point. And I said, you know, that actually really is demonstrative of my professional life. I have shot myself in the foot so many times as a professional in terms of a career. You know, my first job was at NBC News and my first boss, you're probably all too young for this, but Tom Brokaw, who was the anchor of the NBC Nightly News, was my first boss. And so I did that for three and a half years and I moved to Israel to be the Middle East producer for NBC. I had an Emmy and an overseas press club award and I was given stock options. And I quit at the height of all that because it just wasn't meaningful to me anymore. Got no more non-words you said. No, it's just weird because, you know, all the things on paper made a lot of sense. And I was at the top of the mountain, as I like to say. But I felt like the things that I really wanted to do, I wanted to tell really great stories. I felt that the technology that was coming, this was early 2000s, NBC was not interested in any of this, the digital stuff, smaller cameras, telling different kinds of stories. And they weren't interested in me doing it despite me being their golden boy at the time. So I quit. And it was almost kind of a spur of the moment quitting thing. And, you know, I felt great for about a day. And then I said, what the hell did I just do? But that began a pattern for me of this idea that I would do something and explore it with great intellectual curiosity and desire because I feel like I'm a Wild West kind of person. And I don't build settlements. And I'm the cowboy because it's meaningful to me. I have a great intellectual curiosity. And when I'm done understanding the system, I'll start to misbehave within the system. And I know it's time to leave. And so that's what's happened. And so whether it's as I have my own company or I'm the director of a graduate program at the University of Washington in communication and digital media, I always know when I've peaked and when it's time to move on before it starts to go downhill. So along those lines of knowing when you peak, right, how would you coach someone, you know, like, here's the two or three clues that you should look for, at least for you personally, and it probably equates to some of us, I would hope, that kind of tells you you're kind of there. Yeah. Well, I actually, because I have a lot of students, they come to me for advice. And my students are multi-generational. The program I lead at the University of Washington is for professionals. And so I like to joke that, you know, there's this whole hero's journey that Star Wars is based on, right? And so, and there's this hero who is called to action. So Luke Skywalker's called in to do something that he was, he wanted to stay on the farm, the robots arrive, and all of a sudden he's got to go off on this thing, so he's a reluctant hero. And so I always thought I was the hero of my own professional story, but along the way in the hero's journey is also this old guy who helps you out. He's kind of your mentor or your guide. So think of Gandalf or Dumbledore or Obi-Wan Kenobi. And so I realized very recently that I'm no longer Luke Skywalker in my own story, I'm Obi-Wan Kenobi. So my Instagram handle is greybeard and a hoodie. Which means that that's the role I have to play now. People are coming to me for advice all the time. So I had the greybeard, no hoodie today. And I actually ask two questions always of somebody who's coming to me, whether they're 22 or 42. And it's not about when they're supposed to move on, it's really like what is their value and who are they supposed to be. And the two questions I asked are really simple. The first one is, if the economy crashed or you lost your job and you just had to make ends meet to just put food on the table till tomorrow. What's the one thing somebody would pay you for? What's the one skill or thing that you have that the outside world recognizes as worth transacting for? Interesting. And so it could be, you know, I'm great with Photoshop, I can make a video, I'm a really good writer. Just really basic stuff, right? And then the second question is almost the inverse. It's, alright, when you get home at the end of your day, what's the one thing that you just can't help yourself to do and you don't care that you're not getting paid to do it? You know, so basically it speaks to your heart, right? What is the thing you're most passionate about? You don't really want the money for it because you'll do it anyway. Just do it. And the two never correspond, right? But in an ideal professional situation, you have both of those things. You can get paid for the thing that you're really good at and you can own. And you can do the thing that you're super passionate about that you would do even if they didn't pay you. And so that's what I encourage my students to do. And I always start with a disclaimer. You're going to talk to me and tell me what you're thinking about and all the options you have. And my disclaimer is that I'm always going to choose the riskiest option. And I'm going to give you permission for that. And that's what I did at the TEDx talk too. A lot of people came up to me after and said, you know, nobody's ever said that I can actually think this way. And so I want people to have the permission to go do crazy things because it is a thing they would have done if somebody had said it's okay to do that. Because, you know, I used to be a war journalist. I used to run in one direction when everybody else was running in the other direction. You know, I've covered suicide bombings. I spent time in Iraq. I've been to Syria. I've been in an earthquake. So it's all that stuff. Let me ask a question about that. Because this is really interesting. So when you're in that, and I'm saying when you're in it, when you're going towards it, everybody else is running away from you. It's actually like crossing the 520 bridge in rush hour in that direction. So when you're going into that, like, where's your head at? Like, what's in your head? First of all, I don't do it anymore, which is why I've suddenly had kids. Because the universe said it's okay for you to procreate because you're not going to risk yourself anymore. But in my head, it actually is a weird extreme of that intellectual curiosity. I want to see what's going on that is putting people at such an extreme situation. And when I covered stories of war and terrorism and darkness, my greatest interest was not in the adrenaline that comes with that. It was actually to find the light in the darkness. So, for example, one of my favorite stories that I did during the Palestinian-Israeli conflict, I went down to the desert and I hung out with these Bedouin who were hosting a Jewish family who needed help. And so I love those stories. I love the fact – I'm looking for hope for humanity when most of it looks so dire and dark. Bleak. Yes. So when you are in that, let's go back to just that situation you talked about. Yeah. And you're there with those people. Yeah. What's your goal? My goal, especially if you're doing television or you've got a smartphone with you, my goal is to get people to forget the technology. My goal is for them to look at me and forget that I'm even a stranger. My goal is to get them to open their hearts and tell me exactly what's in that heart of theirs. It's the same goal when I'm meeting my students. I want them to feel so in a bubble with me that they're willing to tell me exactly what they're thinking and feeling. And when I'm on stage and I have people here, I know when somebody's looking at a device, I don't have a look at them. I can feel it when somebody's distracted and I can tell when people are paying attention to me. Yeah. That's a really important thing. And that's, again, that transference of energy that's so important. So equate that to storytelling is paramount when you start a company, in my opinion. Yeah. It's super critical, right? Because if you can't tell your story about the company where the product exists, then no one's going to remember it. Yeah, it's fundamentally, it's a narrative about aligning your values with your mission. Yeah. All right. And you have to have a really good narrative. Exactly. So when you're coaching someone about storytelling, you know, I call it storytelling 101, whatever you want to call it. But where, what are the things that, what are the pieces, let's just say there's six pieces, what are the pieces, the main pieces they need to have to even tell the story? Well, storytelling is fundamentally about transformation. It's about a journey, right? That's why they call it the hero's journey. And the reason why storytelling works so well, and it sounds so trite because it's become such a buzzword, especially in corporate America, is that storytelling is embedded in our DNA because if you think about what a three-act story, all stories, as Aristotle says, have a beginning, a middle, and an end, our lives have a beginning, middle, and end as well, right? And so there's the beginning where we don't know what's going on, there's the middle where we're at the height of our powers, and we all decline in the end, the denouement. And so fundamentally, a story has to be about a status quo or a baseline and some kind of tension or change that comes at us that forces us to transform, and we start the story very, very differently from the way that we start, and the story differently from how we started. And so when I'm looking at this in the professional context, and companies come to me or individuals come to me and say, what is my story? Or what's, you know, how do I get this out there? I say, well, the best way to do it is to identify that transformation. You know, what are you doing that is going to bring change into the world? I met with, the University of Washington has an amazing innovation arm where they nurture a lot of startups, and I met with somebody who runs Co-Motion Labs a couple days ago, and she said the biggest challenge that entrepreneurs have, especially in Seattle right now, is that they're really good with the pitch deck, but they're really bad at actually the bigger narrative that's going to inspire somebody to open their walls, especially in very conservative Seattle when it comes to money. And she said the biggest thing that somebody can do to convince somebody to do that is to say, I know what the future is going to look like, and I'm going to make it happen. And so you have to convince people both of the credibility of your vision of the future, and the fact that you have that transformative technology and the tension that's around it is going to change the status quo to something else. And that's fundamentally what a story is about, and we want to be inspired by that, and we want to be part of what that outcome is. Yeah, so one of the things that is probably missing more often than not is that inspiration. I'm assuming, because that's a bit harder, at least I feel it's a little harder for me when I'm telling a story to inspire. Well, it's missing too. The inspiration is the outcome. The fundamental thing, and I think where bad movies fail is motivation. Why does Luke Skywalker, sorry for all the nerdy science fiction references, but I think it's a good commonality. Why does Luke Skywalker really want to leave the farm to put himself into a situation of danger and uncertainty? And if you explain that well enough, if you tell me it's because his friends have gone off, he wasn't supposed to be there, and then later we learn that he was actually the heir to some dark lord. Well, that's actually pretty interesting. And I want to know how this is going to end. That is the essence of good storytelling, is that you have created enough tension. This is what Aristotle talks about. It's basically like a winding of a knot. You created enough tension that you've brought the audience along that they want to know how it's going to end. And you can't create that tension if you haven't put together a credible motivation for why that character is doing that. And so when you think about this in the business setting, you want to make sure that the thing that you're trying to build and put out there actually makes sense to the world and that you're bringing people along for a journey. And when you do deliver that product, it's going to change things for the consumer or whatever else. And so you have to build that credibility and that motivation up front. Yes, so it's that up front piece where you have your ducks in a row or your facts and figures in line, whatever you want to call it, that support the product, right? Well, that's still the solution for the problem. I mean, strategically, if you look at Starbucks, for example, and I interviewed Howard Schultz, the founder of Starbucks, a few years ago. And I asked him, he said, you know, when I look at the mission of your company, and I can't tell you what the mission is, right? You might know the mission off the top of your head, but it's not about we're going to sell a lot of coffee to make money for shareholders. That is not the mission. No, no, they basically, their mission is to create this incredible environment for people to come together and to feel and think differently, essentially. It's the third place, right? Yeah. And it's to the benefit of their customers, but it's actually to the benefit of their employees. That's highly inspiring. That's motivating. That's a really powerful narrative. Yeah. And it's not tactical. It's big picture and it's epic. And that's what people want. They want to fall in love with these things, and you have to get them with the story. Yeah. And the story helps them, the story helps them start to feel… It builds a relationship of credibility. If you've seen a great movie and the director has sort of entertained you and felt you feel good about it, you're more likely to watch that director's next movie because they have convinced you that the time you've invested in their narrative is worthwhile. And so it is about credibility and that relationship, a trusted relationship. Yeah. So I wrote a book about five years ago called Storyteller Uprising, Trust and, I forgot the other word, Trust and Persuasion in the Digital Age. It's like what has happened in the last 30 years is that we've moved away from institutions that have held the keys to the gates around information to everybody being able to do this, right? Which is great and it's democratized it and Donald Trump is president. However, or is that but? I don't know. That's another conversation. But what the challenge is is that because we're all communicating, we don't know who to trust anymore. Yeah. And so if you can actually find a narrative that is powerful enough to convince people that they should tune in to what you have to say and they want to stay around long enough to see how it's going to end, that's the trust. That's the relationship. So it's not just about creating a great story. It's about, it's actually taking us back thousands of years to the traveling minstrels and storytellers in Ireland or wherever else where you actually are connecting to a community and you're convincing people that you're a credible person to have something that's worth their investing your time in and you actually might build a longer lasting relationship because of that story. What, how do you, as a founder of a company that you have a product that you, that no one's, doesn't exist. It's a, doesn't, the product's never, the concept of the product just hasn't existed until that day, right? How do you start to educate and tell that story when it's a light bulb? Everyone knows what a light bulb is. Yeah. But no one knows what this X is. How do you start to, like, there's no heuristics there. There's no... No, and that's exactly right because especially now we're seeing storytelling communication, professional communication changing before our eyes. Whole marketing departments are being wiped out because we all of a sudden have access to real-time data. Yeah. And my concern about that, I understand why, how that's important and you can actually get, you can make real-time decisions based on how consumers are behaving. The problem is, is that it's still, even if it's instantaneous, it's still in the past. And it's just, it's kind of proving things that have already come. And if you want to be like, say, Steve Jobs and create a market that didn't exist, if we're gonna, if you're gonna rely entirely on data, you're not gonna create the next iPod or iPhone. Because there's no data to support it. And so, to me, storytelling is very risk, it's riskier, but it's much more forward-facing than being, been focusing on data. And so, what I tell, what I really advise people to do when they're looking at stories is to think as big picture and epically as possible. It's not about this really small change that you want to make. You fundamentally want to create a change. In fact, the mission statement to my graduate program that I lead, which has got storytelling at its base, is that we are a connected learning community, fearlessly tackling challenges to create change using story. And so, it's that fearlessness, it's that emotion, it's that, it's gotta be big enough. It's gotta be a mountain big enough to climb that you wanna chop down with your product that's gonna make people sort of say, "Yes, I wanna be part of that." So, it has to be epic. You have to sort of say, not necessarily I wanna change the world, which is a cliche, but I want this status quo to go away and I'm gonna make it happen with this product that I'm putting out there. And that's the big picture. And then ultimately it's gonna be, this is what this product's gonna do with you. That's the marketing that comes with it. Yeah, yeah, yeah. But you wanna get people in the gate with that kind of big picture thinking. Yeah, so it's that, so that big picture piece is the part that's so hard for people 'cause it's not, you don't get taught that. No. It doesn't like, well maybe in your class, but your class is just unique. Well, it's fundamentally listening. It's amazing. It's exactly what I used to do as a journalist when I'm talking to the Bedouin or I'm dealing with Hezbollah in Lebanon. It's about getting them to forget who they are or who I am and really tell me what they need. video_title: Mike Grabham, Startup Grind Eastside w/ Mark Mader, CEO, Smartsheet
transcript_text: Again, for those of you who have not been to a startup ground event, it really is just a conversation between a couple of people for about 45 minutes-ish, and then we'll open up for Q&A at the end, so feel free to ask questions towards the end of the thing, and we'll, you know, whatever you, whatever you, Mark said I'll answer anything, whatever it is, within reason. No comment. Exactly, no comment. So, Mark, let's start off by just asking a simple thing, like, because I actually had one person ask me, like, what is Smartsheet? So, now you think, oh, everyone knows about Smartsheet, but everyone doesn't know about Smartsheet, so can you tell us, like, tell us about the pilot, what it is, like, the layman terms. Okay, so what does work they're doing mean? So work they're doing means stuff that we traditionally used in Excel or a Google Sheet to track, plan, manage, and report out on stuff. So these are lists that cover events, they cover processes, they cover customer lists, they cover issues, they cover enhancement requests, you get the point, thousands of use cases. And what we believed is that, with about a billion people globally having used spreadsheets before, we fully recognized in our past company that we used those spreadsheets for things well beyond financials. We used them to track work, and work is like non-numerics. So Smartsheet handles that a lot more elegantly in the cloud than Excel or Google Sheets. Very well. How many times have you said that thing? A few times. Yeah, okay. And tell us the kind of the journey for Smartsheet, like from the early days. Rocket ship right out of the gate. Yeah, that is a lie. Speaking of investors' vertical experience, your experience in digital advertising, do you invest in digital advertising? I try not to. You know, and I think it's probably, I don't, I don't know. Like a lot of that stuff just applies to markets overall. Like, yeah, there's no black box. Thank you. See how unbright I am. I couldn't think of that word. And so it's, so I found that, you know, porting experience from there, it helps me see more into the more opaque markets that maybe are less, less mature and different. I don't know if that even answers the question. Sorry. Two questions. Go ahead. So which other areas that you are looking forward to investing? Areas? Again, like I don't have, I don't, I don't have a thesis yet. I mean, it's, you know, I, I have stuff I like. I mean, I kind of, I tend, I think, I think for any sort of, any angel investor who's in, you know, enough deals to kind of think they know something, but not enough deals to have patterns, is going to say they like stuff that is working well. So I have three companies doing follow on financing. So probably stuff in those areas, like, right? I don't know. And I think, like, investing on the back of those types of trends is, is interesting. But, no, I mean, I, I mean, I, I, no, I, it's, it's literally like it's, it's, you know, HoloLens virtual reality. And then like the next thing is like a, an influencer platform. But I think if you would, if you would ask me whether Seattle invests more aggressively in B2B versus B2C, I would say yes, B2B like a million times over. interviewee_bio: Nick Huzar is the CEO of OfferUp , the largest mobile marketplace in the U.S.
video_title: Nick Huzar (OfferUp) at Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: Without any further ado, I'd like to welcome the CEO and founder of OfferUp, Nick Hoos. Come on up. Thank you. That was wonderful. That's great. Thank you. Okay. So again, for the people who have not been here before, this is truly a conversation between two humans. I have been lucky enough to know Nick for a few years, even when he was early, early, early on in this company. So I've got a list of questions that I've created. Some of them have actually been sent by some of you in the audience. Feel free to again tweet questions to me. I'll get those to me from -- Jack will get those to me. I'll go till about eight o'clock-ish and then I'll open up for questions. So hold the questions till about then, but feel free to ask questions at that point. So let's just start off with the easy stuff. I know it's been a fairly long road. A little. A little. That would be an understatement. So tell us -- like, I know you started the -- really the Daily Deals was kind of the first iteration of this company. What made you change -- so I guess start off by telling the audience when you started the Daily Deals thing and then I'll ask a question about that. Yeah, so well -- so I started a company called Dill Springer and the idea being that merchants could post anything for sale. And it wasn't really Daily Deals. It was more of -- I wanted to empower stores to be able to just take a picture and sell something. The problem was we started this when Daily Deals were big and we had the name Deal. So imagine talking to a store with the name Deal in front of it when you've got, like, 5,000 Groupons, right? No one wanted to really even talk to me. So I think that, you know, that was five years ago, maybe -- it was a while ago. You know, and I think ultimately, you know, after doing that for about a year, we built a lot of technology that was interesting, but we kind of had the chicken and the egg problem where, you know, the store would post something and no one would really engage. And so I remember telling some of my early kind of co-founders, you know, why -- well, how can we get what we're building on everyone's phone? And that's when the idea for consumer came into play. It's like, let's take what we build and rebrand this. And if we can get it on in everyone's pocket, then when that merchant finally posts something, they'll have something there. So, you know, now you fast forward and we have merchants all the time that use it. The good thing is we don't have to even really engage them. They just do it because the audience is there now. So that was a good pivot we had. It was tough because we lost a co-founder in the mix. He thought we were crazy to kind of take on Craigslist. He was right. But ultimately, you know, we're making strides where I think a lot of other people have failed. And so, you know, I mean, at the end of the day, I think you just got to believe in what you're doing. I think it was the right, obviously the right call. Yeah, I would agree. So, what was the thing or maybe the, you know, the aha moment that said, hey, we're going from this daily deals thing to this mobile, more of a mobile platform. Yeah. What was the, was there an aha moment or was it just five guys in a room saying, yeah. I mean, I mean, it was a lot of like, hey, this isn't working. People are posting. No one's engaging. This sucks. You know, I was one of those guys, by the way. Yeah. Yeah. Groupon's raised billions of dollars. This is, it's too noisy. And then, you know, so it was like, can you, do you just throw in the towel? Right, there's many of those conversations, like you just throw it in or is there a bigger opportunity here that's even bigger than the stores? And so, you got to do, when you have a moment like that, when you're debating and throwing it in or going whole hog some other direction, there's a lot of soul searching when you have to really think of, you know, is, you know, is this more of an internal decision? Like, hey, should we really go ahead and do this? And like I said, it was sitting in a coffee shop, actually it was sitting in a park when we sat down and one guy's like, you're crazy. And the other guy's like, oh, maybe. So it took a few weeks. Had you raised some money before? No, we had raised nothing at that point. Yeah. It was just, it was just you guys. So we were eating like, you know, top ramen and, you know, and then we were in the Sahara for a year with no sign of water. Yeah. Yeah. So looking back in the last, that's probably five, five and a half years ago. Yeah. From the start of that. So looking back the last, say, five years, what was the biggest adjustment that you had to make? And I want to say more on a personal level versus a professional level. And then I'll get back to the professional level. Like what was... Personally. What was the big adjustment Nick had to make as a human being? Well, I have two kids. I have a four year old and I want a half year old. And I think, you know, when you're starting a company, like I think a lot of people will tell you it's as you get older, it doesn't get easier. That's for sure. And so I think, you know, the biggest adjustment is how do you fit? How do you balance your life where before I could sit down when I was younger and crank nonstop? You can't do that when you get two kids. So I think personally, that was a big, that was a big thing. I had to kind of figure out how to, how do I weave in my personal life and give time to my family? And luckily they sleep really well. They sleep through the night. So I think that's something that I've been very fortunate. I don't know if I could have pulled it off if my kids woke up all through the night. Sure. They sleep 13 hours a night and that's... It's wonderful. That's been great, yeah. And so let's now look at your professional side. What was the big adjustment, the biggest adjustment that you've done over the last five years in the business kind of setting? I can name 100. Give me three. You know, I mean, one, obviously pivoting from an idea that I felt was pretty futile in the end. That was a big one because ultimately that, you know, ever since that pivot, we never pivoted again. So the last four years, not one pivot, but that was a big pivot. You know, and I think the other one was, you know, raising capital and realizing that as as some of you may find here in Seattle, when you have big, crazy consumer ideas, this is not the place to feed. Trust me. And ultimately, I kind of felt pretty tapped out in terms of, I was really aggressive in networking and trying to meet the right people to bring on this journey. And I got an airplane. I went down to the valley 15 times in 12 months. And that was tough. I mean, it was to the point I was telling my wife, I'm like, I might move down there. And this is when I only had my daughter. But, you know, so imagine moving down there for a month or so when you have a kid. And ultimately, that was helpful because I had, you know, out of 15 trips, 14 were all rejections. And one led to my Series A. Sure. And... It only takes one. Yeah, it only takes one. But that was, again, where I was at Wits End saying, man, if I can't raise money, we're lights out here. And I just started getting on a plane. And so that was, like I said, that was tough because it was pulling away. At the time, I was coding. I was designing. I was hiring. You know, I was doing everything. And I think that was a big adjustment. And then I think more recently is like, how do you hire the best people you can find? So we have 45 employees now. And that's, you know, challenging where, you know, back in the day, I would go raise money. So I mean, I draw icons at night. Now I'm a little bit more detached. And so I think just kind of finding ways to be involved without, you know, micromanaging, pretty much. Yeah. And you're learning that, I guess, as you go. Yeah. As you go. Yeah. Yeah. Which is great. So here's a question I ask literally every single guest. Where did you earn your first dollar that wasn't from a relative? What? That wasn't from a relative? How did you earn your first dollar? When I was younger. Well, obviously. Oh. Younger than you are now. I know that. Where did you earn it? What did you do to earn the first dollar that wasn't from a relative like, oh, Nick, here's a dollar. What did you do to earn it? I'm mowing lawns. I did it when I was 12. So I think one thing my parents did well is instill work ethic in me. And so at 12, I worked during the summer. Like I wanted a Nintendo Entertainment System. They wouldn't buy it for me. Exactly. I thought you could go work for it. But you could go more lawns for it. It might have been child labor. I don't know. Yeah. Anyway, I think that helped. I mean, I think about it. I think that keeps that drive in you. So I think, yeah. At an early age, I was doing a lot of that. You'd be surprised how many entrepreneurs had jobs really young, like 10, 11, 12 years old. Like real, kind of real jobs. Yeah. Can you tell us some things about Connects, which you were CTO before you were CEO. So tell us, give us some, just some thoughts about Connects and what you did there. You know, give the audience, I mean, I know because I know your story, but they don't know your story. Give us a two-minute spiel about Connects. I'll give you the two minutes on what not to do because that's a story of what not to do. So in a nutshell, I started Connects because I had this idea we'd all connect online one day. And at the time, I could only look at dating sites. There was no word of social media. There was nothing like that. And just like, you know, how do we all naturally connect? You know, networking is inefficient. Like it takes, I can't go and talk to every one of you during this event and really learn a lot about you. But back when I started Connects, there was nothing. There was no LinkedIn. There was no Facebook. And so I had this idea we'd all connect online, filed a patent on how I thought people naturally networked. And then I went and built this. And again, struggled to pitch this idea in Seattle. And went from what I call kind of coding in my underwear phase, moonlighting at work. And then ultimately, it raised a little bit of money and really got it going. You know, this was pre Friendster. And I've talked to people. And they're like, what's Friendster? How many in the audience knows what Friendster is? Oh, okay, good. Yeah. So that's interesting. But you talk to young kids. They have no clue. And so I think the things I learned, I'll just give you the cliff note on that. You know, I had this idea we'd all connect online. And I think one thing, I was really early with the idea. And a lot of things in startups are really around timing. So I'd say it started about a year or so before Friendster. And by the time we started raising money, it was almost too late. Like Friendster and MySpace and Facebook really took off. And, you know, ultimately we ended up raising about $4 or $5 million. And, you know, I think a few things that happened. One, I was really young. I didn't know about scaling and leading. So probably I've learned a lot from that. Two is I didn't, we just focused on growing users. We didn't care even what they did. Like we didn't even look at what they did. We were just like, oh, 3,000 people signed up today. That's great. And then in the end we kind of realized, like, hey, it's not that great when you get a million users that sign up and really don't do anything. And also I think along with the wrong investors. Like a lot of my investors were big real estate guys. And they're great at real estate. But what did they know about tech? And I think sometimes you'll see like these trends where somebody in a space gets funded and then everyone copies the Me Too. So you could look at that in Uber, right? There's an Uber for everything now or Groupon. There's Groupon for everything. And I think that's not a good position to be in from an investor standpoint. You want to be investing in the guy that's in the front spot. Not, oh, I'm going to invest in Kinex now because they're doing something similar to Facebook. And so we started to get a lot of people investing in us. And, you know, I think it was kind of too late at that point. And we hired the wrong folks. We hired, we would hire like somebody, you know, we'd interview two people. Like that guy's great. We'll hire him. We'll have two. Versus now we'll interview 20 people for one role. And so I think I take a lot of the painful lessons that I learned from Kinex and I don't make them now. You know, like focus, really focused on metrics, focus on, you know, what happens when a user comes in. So when someone comes in OfferUp, I remember back in the day, you'd post an item for sale. I would show up to your house. I didn't tell you I worked at OfferUp. I'd buy it from you. Like one at a time. One at a time. So imagine an office with 500, you know, 500 square foot office for five people and just tons of stuff in it. That's what, that's kind of what it looked like. But that was the discipline because I knew that that, you know, we weren't that successful at Kinex. And then I think the same thing with recruiting. Like, I love my team. My team I think is just a notch above, but I don't know if I would have been that disciplined had I not gone through, you know, what I did there. Yeah. Wow. So. Yeah. It's always good to either fail or at least do it before. Yeah. So take your time in hiring. I can't reiterate that enough because it, yeah, it can, it, it feels good when you make the hire when you need them really, really bad, but fast forward six months. Yeah. Might as well not have them. Yeah. Um, what makes you a good CEO? Time will tell. I don't know. I don't know. I mean, I think that I'm very passionate about what we're doing. I think I'm very clear. I can see really far into the future where I want to take this. Uh, and hopefully I empower my team to kind of do what they need to do and I kind of get out of the way. Um, you'd have probably have to ask them. What makes a bad CEO? What makes a bad or me a bad one. You can take, I think, I think, you know, I think especially in this day and age when you think of how people used to work versus now, like we don't have office hours. We don't even have a vacation policy. I think that is so passe. Uh, and I just think that, you know, find the right people, empower them and get out of their way. I think that is the new way people work. Uh, you know, I don't say, I think showing up nine to five is, uh, it doesn't make sense. And the way, the way that people work now and the community and all that, um, I just think, you know, people like, I just, I feel like work in my life just intertwine. And I think, I hope people work the same kind of way where, you know, work should be fun and you should enjoy it. Um, but I think, you know, bad CEOs are very, you gotta, you gotta have FaceTime. I've interviewed people like, what's, they're paranoid about the FaceTime in the office because they had a CEO that actually called it that. You have to be here between this time and that doesn't make sense. I also think micromanaging and being a dictator saying, Hey, I'm the one with the ideas. Now go do my work. Um, I don't think that really makes good companies. Um, so it makes bad culture though. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. I think it would make bad culture. Um, when you were at Microsoft and T-Mobile back in the early days, did you know then that was, you were not going to be a corporate guy? Yeah. So this may leave this room, but I'll show the story now. Yeah. So T-Mobile, I used to skate out at work all the time. Like my work probably for a year and a half, maybe 20 hour weeks. Yeah. And I go across the street at the Starbucks and work on connects. I mean, uh, I honestly, they could have fired me that I was Peter from office space. I really was. Uh, and here's the, the challenge of big companies is I remember when I started working there, I was managing the website and I had all this responsibility, but two years went by and not my box got smaller and smaller. So before I knew it, I feel like I could just with my pinky do the job. Uh, and it wasn't like I wasn't doing my job when I was going across the street. I actually got promoted.
