And who's on your advisory board slash investor list, whatever that you rely on to get some quality feedback? I mean, there could be a couple guys. Yeah. So I don't have an advisory board. I do have a formal board. Sure. You know, and they're really, I mean, pretty helpful. I mean, there's a guy on our board that, you know, used to be at eBay. He was at PayPal and Open Table and he's on the board of Airbnb. And so, you know, he has a lot of perspective and, you know, he's been dealing with things and growth challenges. And, you know, that's pretty helpful. And I would also say anytime, even when you're young, fine people, they can give you feedback in your company and, you know, and sometimes, especially with advisors. An advisor that may help you two years ago is not the same person that maybe will help you, you know, today. And you outgrow advisors just like you. Yeah. And that's fine, you know, and that's, and that's okay. So, but I think finding people that have been there and done that, there's not a lot of people I can talk to. I can't go home. You know, I go home and I'm like, man, this is exciting or man, this sucks. My wife's like, hey, you got to change Asher's diaper. Yeah. She doesn't care. Yeah, exactly. She doesn't care. She doesn't give a crap at what you do. I mean, she loves it. She's a supporter. But, you know, when I get home, it's more of like, oh, there's a diaper for you. Yeah. Yeah. Get on that there. Yeah. Yeah, that's, that's your. That's your world, Nick. I can't, I can't delegate that. Yeah, exactly. Yeah. So, one of the questions on Twitter is how do you get users, marketing-wise, how do you get your users? Yeah. So, I won't tell you exactly how we do that. But I will tell, maybe I'll give you some story, because this will help. You know, so how we grew in the markets is, was pain. It was a year of pain and suffering. And, but I knew that if we couldn't figure that out, we were dead. And so, you know, me and, you know, my co-founder and some of the early guys, we got really disciplined and we looked at metrics and we tried a lot of experiments. And, you know, I think in the world of startups, it's very anti-education. You know, when you're young, it's all about getting an A and not making mistakes. In the world of startups, if that's your, if that's how you go about it, you're screwed. Right? You have to make a lot of mistakes, but learn from those mistakes and evolve. So, we literally got really disciplined. We tried a zillion different experiments and we just tried to figure out how can we get ways for people to buy and sell. And like I said, one of the experiments we did was, you know, you would post them and I would buy it from you. Like I said, I didn't tell you worked it off, but just do it. You know, and one of the stats, like I mentioned earlier, was one in four people with a two-car garage can't park in their garage. Now, I'll actually give you the lowest of the low. This is maybe deter anyone from ever doing a startup, but this is my birthday two years ago, January 11th. And it was the day I was like, I don't know if I'm going to do this. And because one in four people can't park in their garage, I go, well, let's just go to the densest neighborhood we can find and hand out balloons. And we'll put it on their door that says, try our app, you know, and it had this big branding on it. And so it was freezing cold the day before and we had literally filled up one of those long, like moving trucks with a thousand hillion balloons. And, you know, we're in the back of this thing with heaters and filling it all up. And we said, okay, tomorrow I'm going to drive this truck around and we're going to go door to door. And we went to Issaquah, kind of Highlands, you know, big neighborhoods out there. And we're like, we're going to hand out all these balloons. And so I drove the truck. We had like handlers passing out balloons and we had runners going door to door. And it looked really cool. Like imagine coming home. Imagine coming home and there's offer up, your company's branding everywhere you look. I mean, it looked really cool. It didn't do shit. It didn't do shit. Not one thing. To make matters worse, the guy that I ran the truck from showed me how to set the brake. And I was on a slight hill and I didn't do it right and I took my foot off for a split second. And the truck actually rolled back a few feet and I stomped the brake and I launched somebody out of the back of the truck. And she had to go to the hospital. So that was a wonderful day. Well, the worst part about it was the time that took me from getting out of the truck to walk to the back. My heart was pounding. Because there were screams in the back. Sure. I killed somebody. Every step, I'm slowly working. I can hear my heart pounding. I'm like, somebody's dead back here. And I remember going out to dinner with my wife that night. I'm like, I don't know. Because we had no traction. We had no money. Hardly any money in the bank. And I'm like, I don't know. I can't figure it out. But ultimately, I think through all these experiments and trying all these things, if you're lucky, you'll find a few things that kind of work. And so that's kind of, I think, what led to our success. But it's just like the same thing with fundraising. It was pure persistence. It was a lot of just grinding it out. And luckily, you kind of stick to it. And it worked. That's true. It doesn't matter what you're raising money for. Rarely is it like, oh, yeah. Everybody says yes. I think I heard Jeff Bezos pitched 140 VCs before one person gave him money. If you're going to sell books online, you're an idiot. Yeah. Yeah. I bet there's 139 that said no, we're all crying today. Yeah. Exactly. So audience questions, feel free to ask questions whenever. Go right ahead. What's your question? Yes. We're at a point where we have absolutely no money coming in. We're at the very beginning of the startup. And you went down to Silicon Valley 15 times. What did you take with you when you went down there? Did you have just what kind of metrics in terms of people visiting the site? Or did you have any kind of elaborate business plan or, you know, how formal was it? Or were you just nonchalantly talking, you know, schmoozing with people? Yeah. So let me, let me repeat the question so everyone can hear it. So what he's asking is basically when you, when you try, when Nick traveled down to the Bay Area to start to raise money, what did you bring with Nick? Was it, tell us what was your ammo and your gun? Yeah. A laptop and a PowerPoint deck. I think in this day and age, you don't really see a lot of business plans. I think the key, you know, if you think of most, especially venture firms, you know, I think the stat I heard is the average partner may get pitched 800 times and invest in eight companies in a year. So I, I, I think, you know, I brought a deck and I told a story and early on I didn't have any traction. And that was why, why a lot of people told me, no, like, this is a great idea. I love the, I, you know, I love the vision and the story, but you know, you need to have, you need to demonstrate results. Like how many people are signing up and using it? Do you have liquidity? Do you have people buying and selling? Or do you just have, you know, thousands of people signing up that don't do anything? Um, so I think, and I also think the good thing when you present is like, there's always a learning experience there. Uh, and in fact, I would pick is, you know, if I was a guy in pitch, I would say, go find the people that you're not that enthusiastic about. And pitch them because they're going to give you feedback and you're not going to burn that bridge. You know, save the ones you really want to be good for until you've gone through the ringer a few times. Because your story will get that, will get better. So anytime I'm pitching anybody, I think the best thing is you've got to be a good, you tell a really good story. And the reason I say that is because imagine sitting on the other side of the table and being an adventure guy and hearing, oh, here's my 700th pitch. Great, yeah, I like your Excel. You know, why you? Like, what's exciting about what you're trying to do? Like, you've got to kind of really make that message crisp. And I'd say early on it wasn't very good. I mean, I used to pitch the green angle of what we're doing and how good that is. And I think it's a great byproduct of what, you know, when I think about how much we've, you know, removed from landfills or where we've been, it's amazing. But you can't, you don't lead with that. And that's what I used to lead with. So, you know, I think that over the years my story is real good now because I've had to do it so many times. Yeah, so I think telling a story, really being clear on the opportunity, how big it is, really being clear on how big the problem is. Like those stats I was telling you guys. That was literally my first deck. My first problem slide was why does stuff sit? And I basically told them those stats because I wanted them to go, oh, yeah, why is all this stuff sitting? Why aren't people selling on Craigslist? Because that was always the first people to tell me, oh, you're trying to compete with Craigslist. Good luck with that, right? So I wanted to highlight that, hey, if everyone's using Craigslist, then why are these stats going up? You know, why aren't they getting smaller? And then ultimately it was clear on the solution. And then, you know, over time we had traction that became compelling that made it easier. So I think early on, all you got is your story early on and yourself. And so you got to, you know, I'd say even start earlier in that process because it'll help with your story. It'll help, you know, you guys really understand what the things you need to focus on, you know, are. I can't. I mean, my history, it's all been about story. If you generate story, people will actually listen to you. If you have a bad story, no one gives a crap. It just doesn't matter. The story is just so critical. Yeah. Whatever the idea is. Go ahead, Holly. You mentioned hiring and recruiting quite a bit. And you mentioned something I've never heard before, which is innovate as you scale. You're maintaining culture as you scale. Yeah. But what would your employees say about you if you were asking them how you would be able to innovate as you scale? So the question is, what would Nick say about how he innovates to scale? Or how are you innovating as you scale? How are you? I guess that's a better one. Yeah. Yeah. How are you innovating to scale? How are you empowering people and what are your employees? Oh. Yeah, empower them, yeah. Well, I think when you're scaling that fast, a lot of it is just kind of figuring it out. And my belief is, you know, find great people and kind of get out of their way. But you can't, you know, you have to have a little process where, you know, when we were seven people, we didn't have to have many. And now we do. And so I think some of it is don't overthink it. You know, right now we're learning all the time. And I think the great thing is if you've got the right people, they'll come together and come up with their own solutions. You know, I can't tell, you know, an engineer how to talk better to a product guy. I don't know. I can say, hey, you're not going fast enough. Let's go faster. Let's do things. And, you know, hey, we said we were going to do this in this timeline. It took way longer. You know, why? I can kind of, you know, talk at a very high level. But, you know, ultimately I think if you have the right people, they'll come together and figure those things out. So, you know, I do that. I hear a lot. My one-on-ones are helpful. I think those are really key as you scale because you learn a lot. And then, you know, every Monday morning meeting, I bring it front and center. It's not like we hide from the problem. We just throw it out there and squash it and kind of move on. And I think if you have an open, transparent kind of environment, that's, you know, pretty helpful. And transparency is not just people being really open with each other. I mean, even the way we design the office. We're literally all in one big open space. I always tell my team, if I ever have an office, just, you know, punch me in the face and fire me. Because then I'm disconnected. And I think that, you know, that's kind of, I think that, you know, because I'm not as involved in the details, but just because we're in an open area actually helps. Because I'll hear things and little bits and it helps kind of, you know, guide the process. So. Why Bellevue? The question is why Bellevue? Why Seattle? No. I mean, half my team's in Seattle, half my team's in Bellevue. Me and my co-founder live in Bellevue. We're literally right off I-90. We're in an amazing office park. Real estate's a lot cheaper there. Bellevue's up and coming. It's got, you know, it's got a lot of things now. And, you know, I think that's, I love it over there. I mean, I, so, I think that's why we started there. It was me and my co-founder were, we lived there. Yeah, you're close and you didn't need an office so you're. We don't get to pay for parking. Nice. Yeah. Go ahead. So you mentioned, you know, we all know Silicon Valley is not Seattle and vice versa. What does Seattle have that Silicon Valley doesn't? What does Silicon Valley have that Seattle does not? Or the other way. The other way. Or no, excuse me. What's, okay. What's Seattle have that Silicon Valley does not? Yeah, I mean, you know, I think if you look at what's happening, especially in tech, look at what's happening in Silicon Valley, like the cost of living, there's people paying $1,500 to rent out someone living in someone's garage now. And so it's, it's, it's a little insane there. You know, but in many ways they have, I think a really robust ecosystem where, where founders and investors will make money and it continues to push back. And I think we're kind of at the precipice of that, of that here, but it's not as robust. You know, I think we've got ideas. We've got a lot of talent. We just need more people putting capital back into our market. But you see that. Look at how many Valley firms continue to come up here and open up offices. And I think part of the reason is, you know, it's the cost of doing business and, you know, having an office here and, you know, a staff is cheaper. I think people are more loyal here, which is great. You know, you go to the Valley and you're like, you know, we're, you know, it's loyalty is, I think it's just because, you know, work somewhere for a while and you're like, oh, are we going IPO this year? Oh, no. Okay. Well, I'm going to go to some other company. You know, I think if, I think that's some of the benefits of kind of being here. Yeah. So, I mean, I'm born and raised here. I would never move to the Valley. I'm more passionate about building this here and, and, and helping influence our local environment. And, you know, but I, I think if we want to be big and robust as a, you know, as a local tech community, we need more of that, what I call kind of waterfall effect, where you have that more money going into the economy. And it, and it doesn't take, I don't think it takes a hundred companies. It only takes a handful. Some big exits. Some big exits. Some big exits. And, you know, I, I really think the Valley, a lot of what the Valley is now is actually because of PayPal. You've got Elon Musk. You've got Reid Hoffman. They did LinkedIn and it, you know, works at Greylock. You've got Dave McClure. You've got Peter Thale. Yeah. They all came out of one company. And I guarantee because of those guys, I mean, how many companies have been created? Yeah. Hundreds. Well, yeah. And they're, and it's an old, the old boys network with those. Yeah. The mafia, PayPal mafia. So. Sorry. Yeah. Yeah. Sorry about that, but yeah. So we need to form our own mafia is what you're saying? We might have to. We're going to have to start a Seattle mafia, my friend. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Good. Are you different from Craigslist? What's that? I didn't hear it. How do we differ? From Craigslist. Yeah. I mean, you know, I, I think like I was saying earlier, the biggest things we focus on is simplicity and safety. Like if I wanted to offer up this chair right now, I could do it in 30 seconds while I'm sitting here talking to you. On Craigslist, that would take you probably 13 minutes just to post it. And then communication back and forth. For example, you're not giving out your email and your phone number. It's just like in-app messaging. So, you know, pretend you lived in Bellevue and I was going to meet up and sell you this chair right now. Ultimately, you'll probably give out your phone number because you would say, Hey, meet me maybe at a target or at my house. Well, here's my number. And when you get here, I'll kind of help you. On OfferUp, you're communicating all within the apps. You're not giving out that private information. We have a program called True You where you can actually scan your ID and we validate if you are who you say you are and you get a badge that says that. We have a rating system. You know, on Craigslist, it's all about being anonymous. And I think that, you know, that's what makes it a lot different. We're visual. So I think, you know, if you go to Craigslist, it's all text. You go to OfferUp. It's just pictures. It's literally just pictures. It's Pinterest. Yep. There's no text. It's your app. It has a logo. And it tells you your location. All you see is pictures. And so I think a more visual experience, which I think is how people shop. You know, you don't go into Nordstrom's and read all the tags first. You actually look at the items. And so, you know, and we're mobile first. Craigslist is still, you know, a desktop application. Two questions, guys. Bear? You touched on storytelling because I've heard that entrepreneurs here aren't as good as storytellers as the Bay Area. Do you have any suggestions or recommendations for people? How can we be better storytellers? It sounds like you need more chest pumping going on over here. Well, maybe. But, you know, he was asking about PR. And I would say I don't do any chest thumping. No, for raising money. Oh, for raising money, maybe. You know, one of the things I evolved with is, like, I believe that you want to be real and genuine. And, you know, the reason I started this is my wife and I were expecting it. I had a room full of stuff I wanted to sell. And I remember thinking that I'd rather kill myself than try to sell this on Craigslist. It was that. I was like, God, life is, we're too valuable. What am I going to do with all this stuff? And that was kind of the beginning. But my early decks didn't say that. My early decks just said, "Offer up, buy so simple." So one day I just happened to be outside and I actually took a picture of my daughter. I didn't plan to incorporate it into my deck. You're going to sell her? Yeah, no. That's come up, though. I pitched, like, are you selling children on here? No. But, you know, I have a picture of her running down the street and the sun setting, and it shows a house in the neighborhood. We're all about community and local. And I'm like, that's, you know, it just happened to be a good photo. So now at the top front of my deck, when I open up my pitch, if I'm talking to somebody, I go, let me tell you about why I'm here and how this started. I'm like, that happens to be a picture of my daughter. And that happens to be the room. And so I just, you know, I kind of walk them through a visual, you know, journey more than anything. And I think that's, again, pretty -- and it might just be who I am. Like, I designed a lot of the first bit of the app, and I'm more visual, and that's how I think about things. And I like things simple. And, you know, and so I think that helps a lot. When I put decks together, it's literally I want them focusing on me and what I like. I don't have a lot of text at all. It's mostly pictures. It's more about the passion. Yeah. I mean, you have to weave and have a compelling story as you do it. But the last thing they want to see again when they've seen 800 pitches is just yet again another, you know, PowerPoint business model. Like, I actually -- I've never presented with a forward-looking business model. Because it's all BS anyhow. And so -- A hockey puck. Yeah. A hockey stick, sorry. Yeah. One more question, guys. Over here. A hockey stick. You mentioned that your wife was working at the time to bring in an income when you left T-Mobile. What happens if she's not a picture? Are you still at T-Mobile? She's going to divorce me? No, sorry. If you have that safety net, right? What's he know that you don't? Oh. Would you still be at T-Mobile today if you didn't have that income coming in? If I didn't have -- so back -- oh, just fast-forwarding. I wasn't at T-Mobile. That was my last company. I already had money. It's this company. Okay. Yeah. So I actually, you know, took time off. I wasn't sure what I wanted to do and kind of, you know, started this one. But I had to convince her, you know, with a kid on the way. And it was rough because I don't want to be a dirtbag dad with no income. But I was for a year. And ultimately, I think she believed in what we were doing and kind of did that. So, I mean, that's tough. I mean, my last company had to take on debt and things like that. I was fortunate this time I didn't have to. And sometimes you've got to -- if you believe in something, you take those risks. But, Nick, you were a damn good dirtbag dad. I was. I changed diapers. Yeah, you were good, man. Okay. So, thank you to all the sponsors for being here and supporting us and allowing us to sit up here and chat for a little over an hour. And most of all, thank you guys for showing up because it would be kind of boring if it was just Nick and I. Not that we wouldn't do that. Yeah. And if you have questions after this, come chat with me. Yeah. Chat with him. But, again, thanks for being here. We'll be here next month on Tuesday night. Speaker to be determined. And thanks for Galvanize. General Assembly. And send in blue. Lose the monkey. Join send in blue. What else do I got? And most of all, thank you, Nick. Thanks for being here. Thank you. Great to be here.

I think, you know, there's this notion of flow. And you can measure it in lots of different ways. You can talk to your engineers. You can talk to your salespeople and ask them if they're happy or not. And, you know, I think we take this notion of flow seriously. We use a program called, we use a piece of software called Tiny Pulse. Sure, just ask people. You know, are you happy? Yeah. And if they're not, and they'll tell you because it's anonymous, something's not going right. Yeah. So that's the technique that we use. Yeah. Which is, I mean, I've been in a couple of companies that we use the same product. Yeah. And, you know, flow is things are happening at velocity, at scale. Yeah, yeah. Exactly. So here you are, knowing that this is not right. Yeah. But what is? That's the hard part. You have to do both. Yeah. And that, if you don't have the solution, the new thing, then I think it would have been much harder to kill them. Yeah. But you already knew where to spend resources. And again, in this process of figuring things out, what things would you or should you be looking for as a leader? What things would you be looking for to identify when you have hit on the right one? Ultimately, that's what's going to keep you going. When you don't have business metrics, it's talking to people. Yeah. And, you know, my recommendation is in the first few years, raise money once a year. Because one, it will force you to think through what it is you're pitching. Sure. And two, you'll get rejected so often that you will, you will start pitching the right thing. Yeah, yeah. Because you'll learn. Because the fear of rejection is a great motivator. Exactly. Yeah. We did raise money the first three years, every year we raised money. Yeah. And then the fourth time was, you know, two years later. Yeah. So you're constantly testing your idea with people who are programmed to say no. Yeah. And while that's hard, it refines your thinking. Yeah. And that's the key there. I think the thing that's the super critical piece there is, one, talking to humans. Because you can't, you know, you can't give feedback without talking to somebody that's buying. That's actually writing a check for it. That's the difference. That's the difference. That's the difference. Are they going to write a check or are they just going to say it's a good idea? Because people say it's a good idea, it doesn't mean anything. Yeah. Don't talk to your friends. No, no. That's what I say. Only people, complete strangers are going to give you the best feedback. Yeah. And, you know, the first, when we were raising our seed round, I talked to 18 people and 17 of them ponied out. So that's good indication. Yeah. I mean, that's a pretty decent indication. Sure. Part of it is because they like you. No, and part of it is. But they still have a. Yeah, there is. But, you know, you're still asking them for money. Yeah. Yeah. They have to give you something. That's the key. Yeah. Yeah. Because I think one of the things that I've seen, you know, especially as I've gotten older, is just the people, they come in and, you know, they're asking questions. That doesn't tell you anything. It must be 600 people. Like, that's the thing that you can't do. You can't do too much of talking to potential customers. It's literally impossible. Because the more you talk, the better feedback you get. Better details. In your personal kind of journey, what's been the hardest thing for you to do? But now, I mean, rejection now is no big deal. Sure. Right. I think the hardest thing to do is to control your emotions. It's all inside. The hardest problems are inside. Yeah. Or is it harder for you to do it in front of a customer? Where is it hardest for you? It's actually hardest to do when you're alone. When you're alone. Yeah. When you get an email and you're alone. Sure. It is really hard to kind of just take a moment and let it pass. Yeah. The last thing I have learned, this is where the character building comes in. Sure. Is to ignore it for a little while. Okay. I was going to say, ignore that you read it or just ignore the reaction? Ignore the reaction. Ignore the reaction. Ignore the reaction. Yeah. And come back. Don't react. Come back. And the whole thing is so much better. So much easier. So much easier. Yeah. Even better. The first thing that happens is, I mean, the moment you get this. Yeah, that's what I'm saying. And your character has grown in a positive sense. In a positive sense. In a positive sense. Yeah. Communication. So, I will see the negatives of a particular situation. It is remarkable if you talk to somebody, two other people, any two other people, about the same situation, they will see it differently. And they're usually right. Say that again. Oh, okay. As a glass half full or even a glass full full and you go, yeah, they're right. The thing is, if you don't talk about it, the demons are having a great day. Yeah. Right? Yeah, yeah. Which is hard when your inner circle is like three people or four people. Or at least you feel like it is. Because founders are lonely at the top. We always hear they're lonely at the top. But they don't have to be lonely at the top. No, it's not even lonely because it's not lonely. Especially, I mean, I have three other co-founders. Sure. But there's a couple who are very much more mature than I am. And, you know, about three weeks ago, I shared an article by Ben Horowitz. It's called The Struggle. It's called The Struggle. It's very simply The Struggle. Search for it. Read it. And I said, you probably felt this way. And they all did. Right? But we all feel that way at different times and about different things. But when you put it together, we all feel positive about what that person felt negative about. So we all kind of raise the bar. Oh, sure. We help each other out. So it works out. Yeah. And in the mechanics of, you know, starting a company. And when I say mechanics, I'm talking about, you know, HR stuff, legal stuff. Marketing stuff. You know, just the mechanics of running the company. What parts were the most important for you when you started? And then I'll ask another question once you end up. Sure. I mean, the three most important things I do are hire the leaders, raise the money. Sure. So you need oxygen. Right? And set the direction. Yeah. And now step back five years ago, four years ago when you started the company. Did you know all that then? Kind of. Yeah. Because that's what I did at Microsoft. I mean, I started new divisions. Yeah. So it wasn't a huge step. Right. But the hiring people was much harder than at Microsoft. At Microsoft, if you had a good idea and enough people know about it, you'd get. The right people. You'd get enough people who are bored with their stuff who come to join you. Here, you have to go sell. I mean, there isn't a pool to harvest. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And what do you think is the, you know, the hardest thing when you're early on in the mechanics? Like, what do you think is really, you know, it's just a difficult process? video_title: Sarah Bird, CEO (Moz) at Startup Grind Seattle
transcript_text: And one of the things, you know, month of May in the Startup Grind community, literally all around the world, we do a Women Founder Month for the month of May, so almost every one of our chapters is doing an interview with a woman, what I call a woman rock star, so, you know, other cities are doing a similar event, so, Sarah, thank you very much for being here, number one, because I know we're all busy, and two, again, for those of you not been here, this is really just an interview between two people, we're going to talk about, you know, Sarah's journey, what she's doing to make decisions, what she's not doing to make decisions, I'm not going to ask her about, you know, her kids, or, you know, where she went to college, or any of that stuff, because you can look that up on the internet, so, we'll have an interesting chat, and at, you know, a few minutes before eight, I'll open up for Q&A, so you can ask questions at the end, and, you know, feel free to do whatever you want on the question side towards the end, so Sarah, how's your day? You know, it's good, it's good, I'm on my, towards the end of my second glass of wine here, just come up a long weekend, so it's pretty good, sat with one-on-one, so I'm getting, like, the pulse of the business, get my head back in the game, that's good, good, so, so tell us, you've been, that's the first time CEO, did you call your mom when you, when you came to see you, did you call your mom? I know, I don't talk to my parents about my work, almost at all, I'm not sure they know what I do. Yeah, exactly, and if they didn't know, they wouldn't know what you did, they would just know you, you had a title. Oh, yeah, I actually, I was nominated for an award, and there's this big, there's this big banquet, it's CEO of the Year Awards, you couldn't even say it, it's so embarrassing, and I was, it didn't even occur to me to invite my parents to come to this award show, and then someone at work, I think was my assistant, was like, well, well, so I'll let your parents know that, I'm like, why, you know, it didn't, it didn't occur to me, she's like, because they're your parents, you know, I was like, oh, well, all right, let me think about it, and then, and then I did invite them, but. So, so, it's an adjustment, obviously, coming from not being a CEO to being a CEO, there's just, it's just different, you know, it is what it is. Can I say more about that before you even go on to finish, because I don't actually think it's as different as people think it is, in my mind, but I could be doing it wrong. I don't know if there's a right way to do it. Well, so, fair enough, I mean, from my experience, what I thought would be different, right, when I was given the opportunity and Rand said, hey, I'd like you to do this role, and I was thinking about it, I thought, wow, it's going to be so much more work, it's going to be so much harder, and that isn't true, it's different work, it's different work, but it's not more work, I don't know about you guys, but I'm already working very hard anyway, as COO and president, I was already working very hard, and so in some ways, the job got easier, which I was not expecting, but when you are a key executive, you have got to, you only have your influence to make things happen in the organization, right, so it's all about, well, how well can I use my influence and my ideas and my personal leadership skills to make something happen? When you're a CEO, you still have to do that, and you can also just say, well, that's what we're going to do, and people are like, yeah, that's what we're going to do, so that actually simplifies a lot, a lot of things, it's kind of amazing. Yeah, it makes the decision, well, the bigger decisions a bit easier, yeah, yeah, and so you've been in that role for two years. What was the one thing that surprised you that you didn't expect? There's a couple things, I suppose. One, I didn't expect it to get to, in some ways, be easier. That was a pleasant surprise. What else surprised me? That everyone told me it is very lonely at the top. I was prepared for that. It's sort of a truism. It's so lonely at the top. I haven't found that to be true, but again, maybe I'm doing it wrong, so like, it's only been two years, and I could be doing it wrong, but I have found that it's only lonely if I make it lonely. It stops being lonely the moment I share the problems with the team, and I'm like, I don't know, what do you guys think? What is this? What do you know? What's going on here, right? And then it becomes all of our problem, and so I haven't yet had that loneliness feeling. I will say something that was harder about it. I actually, I really hate being on television and radio where I'm recorded, no offense, it's all fine, but I don't like it. I've never liked it from the time I was a little girl, and that is a part of the job. That is a part of the job, so I just fake that part. I just fake like it's no big deal, and I'm totally fine with it, but I actually hate it. That's funny. It was one of my questions, because I knew you didn't like it, so I was going to ask you how you've adjusted to that, just doing it, and just like you say, you just do it. Because it's not public speaking. I'm perfectly fine public speaking. It's the recorded artifact that for some reason there's this part of my brain that's like, this is going to be used against me sometime. I don't know when. I don't know what for, but undoubtedly it will be used against me. It will be used only for evil. So you and Rand have what most people call is an extraordinary relationship. I mean, you've been at the company for basically forever. So how has that relationship with Rand, and I've interviewed Rand three times in different things, right? So I definitely understand the quirkiness and who he is. How has that helped you, just in your position, having that? Because that actually is unique, to have that kind of relationship and to be at the top and have, it's just, that's unique. Yeah, absolutely. So Rand inspires me in many ways, and he always has. I would say Rand inspires me to be more transparent than is my nature. Because he's, for him, it's like, and he says this himself, it's like not real until it's on the internet, even if it's his own life, right? If he hasn't blogged about it yet, it's not like a true feeling yet, you know? I mean, it is, it's how he lives, like so transparency. There's no separation between like what's on the internet and how he's feeling in the moment. And like, that's pretty radical, right? I mean, that pushes even me, who I'm a pretty open person. There are very few things that are off limits for me. And that's good. I need that. I like that, right? And he continually pushes me in that way. He's also a bigger supporter of me than I am of myself, most of the time. It would never have occurred to me to be the CEO of a tech company, let alone Moz, when I started. Sure. Or even when he said I should be multiple times, I was still like, you're crazy. We've got one. He's good. You're fine. So those things have helped. And then as we made the transition, as I had to change my frame of reference for why the transition was happening and how I could help Brand, that relationship changed. And in some ways, we were both disappointed by some things that didn't change and relieved by things that did. For those of you who don't know, and again, you can read about it. Brand's very public. He was suffering from depression. And that was one of the main drivers of his reasons to step out of the CEO role. Plus, he hates most of the stuff that a CEO does. Like, he hates it. So he transitioned out. When he first told me he wanted me to be the CEO, I thought he was joking. I eventually, after several months, realized he was quite serious. And we came up with this plan. It was like a year-long plan where I would, you know, a year from now, I'll take over the role. And we ended up pulling up the timeline because I finally had this epiphany. Like, he's kind of asking for help. Like, he can't wait. I had sort of approached the problem from my perspective of when will I be ready. And first of all, no one's ever ready. You're never ready. So there you go. Just do it. Yeah, right? Just do it. Yeah, just do it. And then secondly, I had to stop being so selfish about what I needed to be set up for success and think about Rand and the company. So we moved up that timeline. I think he and I were both hoping that there would be some, like, crazy epiphany moment where he'd be suddenly, like, relieved and happy about everything. And wouldn't that be great? But that's actually not how depression works. I've learned a lot about depression by having this partnership with him, which is great. We had this, like, unhealthy cycle for a while where he would say, this is awful, this is awful. And I thought I would try to say, well, it's not that bad. Like, this is good and this is good. And I was trying to help cheer him up or make him feel better or help him see things sort of more fully. And I didn't realize at the time, but that actually just makes a depressed person feel worse. Like, I didn't understand enough about depression to know how to respond to depression, right? You're supposed to just listen and just say, yeah, that sucks. And just, like, be with them. Like, they don't need your judgment. They don't need to feel bad about their perspective. They know they just need to be there. They just need to feel heard and loved for where they are in that moment, right? And that took me a really long time because I'm kind of a fixer and I'm really optimistic. So that was hard. And it took us a while to break through that. But we're a lot better now. He's feeling a lot better. He's just shipped a great product he feels really proud of. So it's, we're definitely, like, in our places, but there was no, like, rip the band-aid off moment, suddenly feel he feels great and isn't this the most wonderful thing we've ever done? You know, took time. Yeah. And so over the last three years, three, yeah, three and a half years or so, you know, back about three years, there was definitely some product issues. What did you learn, and you were a different role then, you were COO then, but what did you learn through that thing, that issue, and then what did the company learn? So the first question is, what did you learn, and then post that to the company? Yeah, so for those of you who don't know this story, we, in 2012, we fundraised on this idea of we're going to build an all-in-one sort of inbound marketing platform, right? We had an SEO product. We were going to add on social media and content marketing and local SEO, and it was going to be the sort of great all-in-one platform that's going to do everything for every SEO-oriented person. And we got the money, and we started building on that. It took us, we built it exactly the wrong way. Like, some mistakes you have to make on your own multiple times before you really believe them, before you really know them in your gut to be true. And we did the classic, like, imagine everything you could ever want in a product. And we'll make these beautiful wireframes that will be so detailed, there will just be no confusion for the engineers. And they will be so delighted to have our very detailed, rich, very thorough specs of everything that we possibly want in this product, right? And then we'll give it to the engineers, and they will say, thank you for this very detailed roadmap. We will begin to build. And they will tell us with certainty, right? That it will be done in whatever, you know, 40 weeks, come back. And then they begin to build. And, like, and, of course, everything goes horribly wrong from there, right? And it goes wrong in all the very predictable ways you think it will, right? Where your assumptions about what customers value are wrong. And so we had built this big fantasy castle of what we thought customers wanted and sent the engineers on the road path. And, like, it wasn't even what customers wanted, which we can go back to, because that's part of my personal learning. We were, like, the method by which we were building was wrong. We did not start with, like, let's take this one section and rebuild this one thing and get some feedback on that and then build from there. It was, like, a big, totally new back end, new middleware, new front end, new brands, by the way, we added the rebrand from SEOmos to Moz was supposed to be part of that. I mean, it was kind of a basic disaster. And we didn't, you know, as a leader, there was a lot going on. And so as I would hear sort of from the engineers, like, we're working on it. It's coming along. And you'd say, okay, well, show me what you got. Well, it was working yesterday on staging, but now we should deploy something. It's not quite working. Come back tomorrow. And, like, these sort of series of things that should have been a sign for me that we had overwhelmed the engineering organization. And to their credit, because this is not about engineers being bad, it's the opposite. It's engineers wanting so hard to help you succeed and to help the company succeed. And that they wanted to work on it, to build, to actualize, and they had high confidence because they're very talented. And so I think we all thought that we don't need to follow the regular, all the learnings from Agile. Like, we were somehow immune and that we could be different. And so we didn't even have a process to realistically surface how far behind we were. Sure. So, and we thought we were going to do this big bang. We had this terrible marketing idea where we thought, like, we'll just, we'll shut down our current new acquisition funnel and we'll put everyone to a wait list. You couldn't even sign it for the old product because the new one was going to ship any day. And so we ended up having the wait list for nine months where we weren't getting new customers in and then shipped it, right? And then it turned out we had built the wrong things. We hadn't discovered for how long. It was just so, you see, like, the number, like, so it was both what we designed had some major fallacies around sometimes more is just more and customers don't value it. It actually makes the experience worse. And so we thought we were being so generous, like, we'll keep the price the same, but we'll add all these features. It'll be stickier and retention will go up. No, like there, there is, they don't perceive the value in it. Actually, they just feel like it's more complicated and they're harder to use. So we learned a lesson that sometimes less is more. We learned, don't go like waterfall. You are not immune to waterfall. Like it's, it has the same potential pitfalls at every organization. And so change the way we build to sort of much, you know, smaller ideas that we test into. And then three, we totally royally screwed up acquisition, right? Like you don't do the wait list unless you have a much tighter launch process and deadline. Wait lists may work, but they do not work for nine months, right? I mean, it's, there was many, there were many things. And on a personal level, I learned about speaking up more. I think that was a, as a COO at the time, I had concerns about approach. I voiced them very tentatively, very tentatively, which I shouldn't have. And then said, okay, well, you guys, you all got this. I'm going to focus on these other things. Like I'm going to focus on, you know, all of the hiring you were doing. You were doing some acquisitions, like all this other, all this other stuff. And I wish I hadn't, right? In hindsight, I should have been bolder about some of those, about some of my concerns. Like, wow, this big bang launch idea of suddenly unveiling one day, it's a whole new product and a new brand, ta-da! It's basically inviting risk. So I wish I had spoken up earlier. But we got, we're much better now, friends. Yes. Man, we talk about leadership moments, right? Yeah, it's a whole different world now. Yeah. I mean, you have to push, everyone was working so hard to make this launch happen. And then it launches and you don't get the numbers up and to the right. We got numbers down to the right after the launch. So it wasn't even like a psychological payoff of your hard work was worth it because now look at those numbers go up and to the right. It was like, oh, fuck, hang out, you know, like, hang in with me here, guys. Like, don't give up. Thank your families. And I need you for another, like, four months to redo this. We'll fix this. Stay with me here. Only you can help. Like, you know, it was, it was very, it was very challenging. It was a very challenging time. We're definitely a different, different company today. Yes. And what makes you different today? Oh, gosh, we build and ship much more incrementally. We, we have a lot more visibility into our projects across the orgs. There's a lot more like, hey, I'm not seeing progress here. That means there's a problem, right? Like a lot, don't, don't fall for the, like, it was working yesterday. You don't fall for that anymore, right? We do a lot more dog fooding of our products and our product ideas, way more, way more. We would never do a total, you know, refactor everything. We would do a section. We do a page at a time now. Just in case you don't know what dog fooding is, that's giving smaller pieces, smaller changes at once instead of doing one big major push. Yeah, and use it ourselves first, right? So we do smaller pieces, use it ourselves. We have marketers on our team, so we should be able to ask our marketers, what do you think about this marketing tool, right? Does it suck? Yeah, what could be better? So a lot of different things. I think some other aha moments for me that came from that was, like I said, this perception of value. We're very generous. The G in tag fee are core values, it's generosity. And so I really did just naively cling to this belief that if I give customers more, they are going to love it and stay longer. It's this sort of feature fallacy. video_title: Scott Oki (Oki Developments) at Startup Grind Seattle Eastside
transcript_text: Scott. Welcome. Thanks for being here. Glad for the invitation. So, let's just start kind of from the beginning. Most people know of you, you know, probably a lot of, you know, at least kind of know your story at some level. But give the audience a shortened version of the 10 years, exactly 10 years that you spent at Microsoft, because you spent to the day 10 years, which is, I think, kind of interesting as well. But just kind of give them the rough history of that 10 years of what happened. Because you were, what, under 100, right? The first 100 employees. Actually, I was employee 121. 121. Okay. So, obviously that worked out pretty well for you. So, I'm just, not a surprise. So, give us, just like I said, give us a little snippet about what that was like the first 10 years. So, very quickly. The story with Microsoft actually starts before Microsoft. About two years before I joined Microsoft, I did a start-up down in the San Francisco area. Co-founded a company with three other guys. It was a kind of a turnkey back office solution for doctor's offices. And after two years, we ran the company into the ground. And so, I was out on the streets looking for something to do. And MicroPro, I don't know if anyone remembers MicroPro. They were the hottest software company. They did a word processor. And so, we did. And we basically told Seymour that MicroPro was going to basically go out of business in 18 months. And it was during the research phase that I actually learned about Microsoft. And I'm a Seattle guy. And I'm going, well, what is this company doing up in Seattle? You know, 'cause if you didn't exist down in Silicon Valley or in Boston, you basically weren't in the high-tech business. And so, I became intrigued, studied, did some research, finally ended up writing a letter to Bill Gates asking for a job. And so, you know, he basically told me all about Basic and didn't ask me a question. But, you know, they invited me up again to invite to interview with some other people. And they actually ended up offering me a job, which I was smart enough to accept. It was for a whole lot less money and a whole lot less stock options than what Seymour Rubenstein was offering me to go work at MicroPro. But I knew MicroPro was going to go out of business. So I was smart enough to accept the job with Microsoft. You know, one of the first things that I did when I joined Microsoft was I just kind of was getting the lay of the land. And I noticed that known was paying attention to the international markets. I was intrigued. The more I looked into it, I said, why isn't anyone focusing on the international markets? And so after being at the company for two weeks, I decided to write a business plan, which basically found the international division for Microsoft. I got some time on Bill's calendar, did the presentation, asked for a million dollars for that million dollars. I said, I'll start for wholly owned subsidiaries. And if I have any money left over, you know, we'll do some other things. And I don't know why Bill said yes, but he did. He said, yeah, go ahead and do it. So here's this guy, you know, number one, had I ever sold anything before? The answer is no. Do I speak a language other than English? The answer is no. I mean, I barely speak English. You know, had I ever done business internationally before? The answer is no. And so here Bill is giving me, you know, a million dollars to go start the international division. Well, having gone through a failed startup, I vowed never to fail again. And I'm telling you, I worked my butt off, you know, and after four years starting the international division, the international division accounted for over half the profits of Microsoft and 42% of the revenues. So we obviously were doing something right. We probably did some things wrong too, but we did mostly right things. And that eventually led to, you know, eventually running, you know, all sales, marketing and service for the company. And, you know, I got married. transcript_text: Welcome to the Star with Ryan. So without any further ado, I'd like to welcome the guest tonight, Ryan Buckley from FitCo. Come on up, Ryan. Thank you. You're welcome. interviewee_bio: Eric Breon is the CEO of Vacasa , North America’s largest vacation rental company with 11,000 homes.
video_title: Eric Breon
