Right. So, the, you know, the valuations for companies who are Internet of Things related that have no business model are overblown. I mean, I'm a, I'm a, I, my undergraduate degree is in economics. Yes, you're. Yeah, so, I'm a fundamentalist. That's why, this is why I'm not an angel investor. Right. I'm a, I believe in the fundamentals. Yeah. And in a bunch of things, the fundamentals just aren't there. Yeah. Now, the Internet of Things is real. And there will be people who do amazing things there. Sure. And there will be big money there. But not everything deserves the valuation that people give. So, some of the same thing is true on the enterprise side in the cloud. So, everybody says, well, I've got a cloud foobar, therefore, it must be worth a lot of money. Yeah. The cloud is an amazing place, but it's not nirvana. Right? There's bad businesses in the cloud. And bad things happen in the cloud just like they do on premise. Sure. And, you know, so, I do think, you know, our, one of the challenges in the venture community, which is just sort of part of the game, is that things get on a hot run. And when you have too much capital chasing too few things, the stuff that's hot gets overblown, and money goes to the wrong places. And we're, you know, somewhere in the next two or three years, we're headed for a correction. It's just, it's going to happen. And, you know, part of the challenge in the VC community is, you know, there might be some folks who are big who are going to get caught in the correction. And how's that going to work out? Yeah. It's a, it's a, that's a real challenge. Yeah. Guys, questions, feel free to start raising your hand and ask questions as you, as you feel free. So, one thing about the industries that are, you know, having a lot of attention, right? Sure. So, when they have a lot of attention, what do you think, here's what happens. So, Robbie has a cool company, and then Seton has a cool company, Joe and Bill have a cool company. All of them have a SaaS model, and these are all getting funded. Sure. And then I go, like, oh, shit, okay, well, that's a SaaS model. I could have a SaaS model, too. Exactly. So, I'm going to turn my company into a SaaS model. Right. How do you, how do you, where does that fit in the, you know, the capitalism startup world? How do you feel about that? Is that something that you say, well, you know, it's just part of the game, it's just how it works, or it's like, you know, it's like, come up with your own idea, or how do you feel about that? So, let's, let's distinguish between two different things. Not, everybody doesn't have to come up with a new business model. Everybody doesn't have to come up with a new SaaS. Yeah. Right. So, software as a service is a real thing. And if you have an idea where a software as a service is a powerful benefit to customers, God bless, do a SaaS model. That's what you should do. Sure. Right. The problem is when people want to do a model that doesn't have a benefit for customers, but sounds good to get money that they can raise. That's when the problem happens. And then suddenly you have something that's masquerading as a, just to pick SaaS as an example, it's masquerading as a SaaS business. When it's really. When it's really not, or doesn't need to be, or shouldn't be, or the customer doesn't benefit from that. Yeah. And SaaS becomes the elevator word that gets them a meeting to have a chance at, you know, getting money. And, you know, that's a, you know, that's, that's a classic problem. Right? Buzzwords are buzzwords. You know, it's, it's funny. We would have, Xbox Live, we would have never described that as a cloud service. Never in a million years. It's probably Microsoft's first cloud service. Why would you describe it as cloud? Why wouldn't I? Well, cloud didn't exist. Well, I'm just pointing out branding wise, we would have never thought of it that way. But if you were doing Xbox Live from scratch today, you would say, oh, it's a gaming cloud service. And how many guys have been trying to get funding as gaming cloud service? It's a bunch of them. Right? Not because necessarily that's a better experience. See, what was it, online or on, there's a bunch, there's a bunch of guys who just died because they were going to try to stream games from the cloud because cloud was a big deal. Well, it turns out streaming games from the cloud is really hard. It's not a great customer benefit. And once those companies fail, eventually somebody will make that successful. But that's the point. I mean, you've got to, in the end, all of these things have to come back to, I have a customer. What benefit am I delivering to that customer? And if SaaS or cloud or mobile supports that, then I can say I'm one of those guys. Yeah. And if it doesn't, I've got to say I'm something else. Yeah. And I've got to find out what that is and how to articulate that in a way that captures people's attention. Yeah, stick with it. And stick with the benefit. If you have a compelling consumer benefit, you will find a way to monetize it. And if you don't have a compelling consumer benefit, or a consumer could be an enterprise, but a compelling customer benefit. If you don't have that no amount of buzzwords and re-engineering and re-architecting, we'll ever get you there. Yeah. And I know that sounds completely obvious and completely silly to say and completely goofy, but the number of things I see where you kind of go, really? Really? How's that going to work? Yeah. Yeah. That happens a lot. Sure. Question. It's 2008. What do you think went wrong with Windows Mobile? It's 2000. I'm just repeating the question. It's 2008. What went wrong with Windows Mobile? Oh, 2008's too late. Windows Mobile was broken in 2004 or 2003, if we're honest with ourselves. In 2007, 2008, we made it worse. I can take credit for that. So I'm happy to raise my hand and say I didn't fix the situation fast enough. But... But in 2004, we beat home. Yeah, but what... Like on BlackBerry. Yeah, okay, but here's what happened. We lost sight, and actually, if we're honest with ourselves, this is true for Windows too. We lost sight of the customer benefit and who the customer was. And the customer for Windows Mobile was the OEM or the service provider, AT&T, Verizon, etc. So we would do anything we could in Windows Mobile to satisfy some demand from HTC, Samsung, and at the time in 2003, there were about eight other guys. And from AT&T, and Verizon, and Deutsche Telekom, and some other guy. And what, you know, in 2007, we did something like 50 releases of Windows Mobile operating systems. Because everybody had their own screen size, and they had their own touch technology, and they had their own widget, and their own this, and their own that. And not because there was any particular customer benefit for that, but because that's what they thought of as their differentiation. And in fact, what Apple did, which was very smart and very effective, is they said, we're going to produce a great phone. And we're going to tell the operators, tough luck, you don't get to tell us what's on the phone. And we're going to tell... We're going to do the hardware ourselves, and we're going to integrate software and hardware together. And we're going to make a great customer experience. Windows Mobile phones were never, if we're really honest, and I can say this, I ran the group, were never our great customer experience. They were great at points in 2003, 2004. Great for the time. They were good relative to what else you could do at that time. But they were never great. And with due respect to people who worked on that business, they did really good work. I'm not really trying... I'm really not trying to slam anybody. And like I said, I managed it for three years, so I can take some credit and blame for this. But the experience was never, hey, wow, as a phone. As a PDA, we did some really cool things. But when it went from being a PDA to a phone, it was sort of a PDA masquerading as a phone. And the phone part was never great. We dropped calls. Reception was never really good. The interaction with the screen, the touch stuff we did required a stylus. And even then, it wasn't very good. They were expensive phones. They were kind of bulky. And we didn't figure out the importance of integrated design and customer benefit soon enough. And when I took over the business, we were working on the same path. I let the team work on that path for a year. One of my bigger mistakes. And we didn't switch our model for 12 months. And in that 12 months, Apple shipped an iPhone. Android came to market. And the market moved in the blink of an eye. And we went from 20 million units of Windows Mobile to 3 in the space of 12 months. And, you know, the damage in that happened long before that. The other thing I will say about Windows Mobile in defense of the Windows Mobile team, it was never resourced to be successful. And ironically, one of the things that really hurt it was long-open. And Vista. Because a lot of technical talent went to fix those products. Technical talent that could have been on other things. And that was a problem at the company. I mean, if we're honest with ourselves, as Microsoft employees, when those products went sideways, people had to fix them. That was a cornerstone of the company, so it had to be fixed. So the best technical talent went there. And there wasn't enough technical talent to go around. That's how I see it. That may not make people, everybody's super happy. But that's the way. Again, in my newfound mode of being able to be objective, that's the way I would call it. And you're not getting the paychecks. You don't really have to worry about it. Yeah, exactly. Well, the hard part about this is, as much as I haven't been at Microsoft for five years, I can now, I don't say we anymore, but I still love the company. I think it's a great company. It's an amazing company. They're still doing really great work. So it's always tough to be critical, but you've got to be honest about it. Yeah, and the, against, for the straight. Yeah, right, yeah. Two fingers in the air. Go ahead. Yeah, so you've come on over time at Xbox. Do you see eSports sticking around for a while or coming to the North American market? Sorry, who? eSports, electronic sports, video game competitions. I'm not an expert on that. I don't know enough about what they do. You know, the funny thing is I don't follow the video game space as close as I used to, so I'm not going to give you a good opinion. I do think there is plenty of opportunity in performance type things. Let me just say it that way. There's plenty of opportunity where people, because of what's happened in the cloud and because of the ability to create virtual gatherings, there's plenty of opportunities for things to happen in that space. Specific's not going to be my strong suit, but there's lots of opportunity there. And I do think you're going to see more types of competition build there. That's why Xbox Live was an interesting experiment, because it's sort of the precursor of enabling those kinds of things to happen. All the way in the back, red shirt. That's you. So if you've got to the cloud, can you tell us a little bit about AWS, Azure, what Azure could do in order to really compete with it? Let me repeat that. Yeah, go ahead. So the question is what Azure can do to compete with AWS, right? Yeah, and Google Docs. Oh, and Google Docs, sure, sure. So what I would tell you is I think right now, that competition is an AWS Azure competition mostly. And Google Docs is there in a certain part of the marketplace, but I would tell you that most of the companies I talk to are making decisions between AWS and Azure. And to me, the number one thing is what I said earlier. How do you get a platform that people make money on? And how can you create an environment where people are doing cool applications in the cloud, new things, not just moving stuff that exists in the premise into the cloud. This is not about necessarily exchange on Azure. It's about new things that happen in the cloud and who's going to develop for those. And, you know, frankly, Amazon has a head start there because a lot of the cutting-edge work was done on AWS. There's a bunch of people who were first in the cloud who used AWS, so there's a track record there. And that now needs to, you know, for Microsoft to be successful, they've got to catch up on that front. And I actually think they're making progress there, and I'm not a big diagnostician about what's going on in that space. It's not my specialty, but I do think it's a place. You know, Microsoft's always been a fast follower in some respects. This is a place where they're following actually sooner than usual, if I can say it that way. And usually Microsoft gets a little further behind and then has to work really, really hard to catch up, and sometimes we never catch up like happened in the mobile space over the last four or five years. But in the cloud space, we were there relatively early. And, you know, I think that's going to be a two. Maybe Google will be a third horse in that race, but that's going to be a long, you know, five, ten-year battle for sure. And I think it'll be good for customers and for businesses that there's two guys there who are credible. So you'll laugh at this. When you read the book, one of the things I do in the book is I write a three-page paper for the United States. So I write a purpose which I steal completely word for word from the Constitution. So it's actually not a great purpose statement. The preamble of the Constitution is actually a poorly written purpose statement by my definition, but it's what we work with, right? The purpose statement was written, and it's there. And so I dissect that and talk about that. I write five principles for how I think the country should work and think about things, and then I have five priorities. And so those five, I'll just tell you what the, since you asked, I'll tell you what the five priorities are. There's an economic business model problem that the country has that we have to fix. And the way we think about revenue and expenses is just screwed up at every level. Our tax system's a mess. We don't really understand how we spend money. There's no accountability for how we spend money. There's places where we should be spending more. There's places where we should be spending less, and there isn't a good way to manage that. So there's an equal, what I think of as a business model problem at the core. Second thing is our education system needs dramatic work, and that's really hard because it's a highly distributed system. So fixing the education system isn't even something that's going to predominantly happen at the federal level. Most of it has to happen at the state and local level. Federal government can help, but a lot of it has to happen at the state and local level. That's a big issue in the state of Washington. As I said, it's a big issue in the state of North Carolina, where I also have a lot of family and interests, and those, unfortunately, are two of the states at the bottom of the education stack right now. Third thing is energy and the environment. If we don't figure out how to have a long-term, environmentally sustainable energy, ability to produce energy, not only is it going to cripple developed economies, but actually the people who get hurt the most are the people who are in undeveloped economies, because it takes away their ability to develop. And so there's a huge mandate. That's a long-term problem. It's not an immediate problem, but there's a huge mandate to deal with the environmental mess we're creating and to produce energy. That's part of it. Fourth one is our safety net. Isn't a net. It's got holes in it. It's bad. The government part of it, nobody's willing to talk about. Government leaders aren't willing to talk about Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, or any kind of safety net problem. It's considered off-limits. I think the government needs to spend a lot of money there, maybe even more than it spends today. But nobody wants to talk about what we spend or how we spend it. And when you can't talk about, you know, 45% of the budget, and then you add defense, because you can't talk about that either, you now can't talk about 75% of the budget, there's no way to fix things. And so thinking through our safety net and how public-private partnerships and government activity together could improve the safety net in our country is a huge deal. And then the final thing is our international policy is, you know, follow the ping-pong ball all over the place, and there's no logic or strategy to it, generally. And that's a big problem. And six, seven, and eight are huge problems. You know, that's the challenge of writing those five is I can have a really engaging debate with you, and you could convince me that what I have at six should be five. Because the things that are at six, seven, eight, our election system is a disaster. Actually, the issue of race in America is a huge problem, and is a disaster of a different type than it was in the 60s, but equally messy. So, you know, I can get to ten things really quick. And the reason I did what I did in the book is because you've got to pick five. And we can have a great argument about which five to pick, and what I wish would happen is that the country would have that argument. Here's the test. I'm going to write a blog post about this at some point soon. Here's the test. When you're figuring out who to vote for president, find out which candidate can tell you five things that are their top five priorities and not change them when they go to the next meeting. So we're not voting? Yeah. So we're not voting, exactly. But that's precisely the problem. Yes. That is precisely the problem. And until we face that problem and until citizens stand up and say we're not going to put up with that, until people demand change on that front, things are not going to get better. So anyway, it's a good question. You played right into the book. Thank you for the marketing opportunity. Much appreciated. Let's go down here. Hi, Robbie. I totally agree. Great company. That's fine. So I have a three-prong question. Sure. You only get one problem. First problem. How did you generate the Xbox culture within an enterprise payment? And what was the genesis? Because I know it was not the type of culture that would generate a sort of a, sort of a, I would say, a spin-off or whatever you want to call it. Okay, that's the first one. That second one, do you recommend an aspiring entrepreneur who wants to really be doing very well to join a startup with 3,000 employees? And if that is the case, how do you spot whether that's the right one? And the third one is what is your dominant gene? You only get one question. All right, let me. Wait, wait, so hold on a second. So let's just start with at least one question. Yeah. So the first question, we're not going to answer three. Xbox culture. Yeah. Let me talk about Xbox culture because it's actually, it's actually the most interesting part of those questions. Sadly, one of the things I can be very objective about, about my own performance on the Xbox business, is that I didn't think about culture anywhere close to enough. If I had to pick, if I was starting a company today and I had to pick two or three things, that mattered the most, culture is on the list. Yes. It's on the list with technology. It's on the list with ideas. It's right up front. At some level, you know, I can make the argument it's the most important. And that's probably taking it to a bit of an extreme. Our culture on Xbox for the first four or five years was a function of who we hired and no, nothing more than that. And since we hired people from the systems group, we hired people from the hardware team, we hired people from office, we hired people from the gaming community, it was a mess. And there was no culture. And it was, or it was, as I said, the United Nations. And it wasn't until we said we're going to define the culture and write principles and force people to think about it that way that we got disciplined about the culture. And in fact, some senior people left. Important people opted out. They said, that's not the place I want to work. And that was bad for us in the short term and fabulous for the team in the long term because we were able to start to work as a team. We went from being an incredibly talented group of individuals who kind of got the first version launch based on sheer energy and intellect. And there were some amazing people to a really good team. That's the difference between watching an NBA All-Star game, which is just an awful piece of basketball, but you see a lot of good players, and then watching the San Antonio Spurs play, which has less talent than most teams in the NBA and has a reasonable shot at winning the NBA title. And to me, that's what culture is. And it's incredibly powerful and important. How many people were there? On Xbox? When we started in December of 99, so January 2000, there were 25 people on the project. By the time we shipped Xbox, the first version, they're probably close to 2,000. So that's in 18 months. So just think about that for a moment. You know, how many startups go from 20 to 1,800 in 18 months? I mean, they just don't. But, you know, we could hire lots of people in Microsoft. Sure. Right? And I mean, you didn't need a recruiter. Yeah, exactly. Jay's favorite strategy was to go say, I'm going to go give a presentation on what we're doing to another group. And people would come and watch, and then they'd all apply for jobs. It was great. And you'd hire 10 or 15 people at a time. It was a good strategy. Question? Yeah. Yeah. Robbie, I was hoping you'd... I'm sorry, what does that mean? Yeah. I was hoping to expand on something you said earlier that I thought was really interesting. You talked about how you believe in people. Yeah. One thing you've learned is to have less patience with people. Right. Can you maybe give some tips, you know, things we can hang our hat on, specific things when you know you've got too much patience and you need to have less? Well, here's the thing. So you hire somebody for a job and you say, okay, I've hired this person. I think it's a good hire. I think they can do the job. But they've got this set of weaknesses. And you've got to... The first thing you've got to do when you hire somebody who you know has strengths and weaknesses is you've got to have the hard talk with them about their weaknesses. You have to sit down and say, look, these are the things that are your strengths, your superpowers. I'm excited about them. That's what's going to make you successful. But you've got these things that, from my process of interviewing, that I think are going to be real challenges in what you have to do. And the litmus test is, does the person listen? Do they take it seriously? And do they try to make progress on those things? And that's a hard test for people. It's, you know, think of it this way. Your first conversation with the startup leader of your company is what you're bad at. It's a really hard conversation to have. But you have to do that conversation in the context, look, I hired you because I expect you to be successful. I'm making a big bet. This is important. We have 10 people at the company. You're number 11. That means you're whatever that is, nine percent of the resource at the company. So, oh my gosh, you better be good. And so I've got to look at the places where I see you have weaknesses and be really straightforward with it. And you have to be strong enough as an individual to accept that feedback and say, you're right, I've got to get better. And you will instantly know how people take that feedback. And some people will say, verily, I get it. And they'll go work on it. And you'll see the progress and see the change. Some people will say, verily, yes. And you'll see no change. And you'll see no effort. And that's what I call the pocket veto. That is a bad sign. And then there's some people who just don't get it and who are offended by it. And then you've probably not paid the right hire. So, and that's a, I don't literally mean the first meeting, but that's the conversations that you have to be able to have with people when you bring them on board and you're working with them. You've got to be able to have those sort of truthful conversations. The guy who is the CEO at Sonos is a guy named John McFarlane. And he is the true believer. He is in the dictionary next to transparency. And sometimes in ways that just scare the crap out of me. Because he's just really honest with people. And he's an engineer by training, but a good people person. And, you know, a couple years ago, I'd been working with the company for about two years. They hired a guy in product management. And after three months, John knew that the fit wasn't right. And the guy hadn't moved his family. He literally went and had a conversation with them. He says, you and I both know this isn't going to work. And here's why. And the guy said, you're right. And moved back to the valley. It's the right thing for the guy, right thing for Sonos. Being able to have those kinds of conversations is hard, but you've got to be able to do it. It's a real test of being a people manager and being somebody who cares deeply about people, which I do, and yet caring enough to have the honest conversations. It's hard. One more question, guys. Does Sonos have any plans... Sorry, me? Yeah. Sure, go ahead. Does Sonos have any plans to integrate some sort of like ergonoma or Siri type stuff? It's a good question. You know, if they did, I couldn't tell you because that would be proprietary information. The thing they've done to date, which is in some ways the most powerful, if you're a real music lover, one of the most powerful things they've done is they have cross-service universal search. So it's text-based today, so it's not Cortana. But the idea that I can be a user of Spotify and have my own library on a hard drive and be a user of Deezer and have all those services on Sonos and do a search and have it pull up music from all the different services and find the song is very cool. And so search and discovery, to get at the underlying part of your question, search and discovery, hugely important. When you have all the world's music, but it's not completely at your fingertips and it's hard to find, that's tough. And so one of the things they do focus on is figuring out how you work across all those services to get things done. It turns out most people have more than one service. Almost everybody has Pandora plus one. And then there's people who have Pandora plus two, three, and four. It's actually not uncommon. And then when you throw YouTube in there, which is, you know, second largest music service in the United States, which is weird, but it is. You know, figuring out how you sort through all that data is actually really important. It's a cool space. Any more questions? I'm sorry. No, I'm just joking. No, really. One more question. I'll just give you the hand up right there. Yeah, with a thumb up. Go. Okay. So for startups, what should we be looking for in board members? Here's the whole board. What are you going to be looking for? There's no perfect answer for that. Let me answer it this way. In every company, when you look at the team, I use this analogy when I talk about leadership. And people ask, so what's the most important attribute to be a great leader? And I tell people, well, there isn't one attribute. Everybody, it's sort of like thinking through the Avengers. each of the Avengers has a superpower. And your challenge as a leader is finding your superpower and then surrounding yourself with people who have different superpowers so that collectively you're better than individually. And so I think of boards the same way. So you have to think, okay, what things do we have inside the company that we're really talented and, you know, where we, you know, we don't need help? And what places do we need help? And how do I find board members who are willing and able to fill that superpower need? Now, some board members you're going to get because they funded you and the price of funding was a board member. And in that case, your conversation with them is not, are they a board member, but who from the company is the board member and what role do you ask them to play? And I'd encourage you, even though it's uncomfortable, to have that conversation with them. If they come to your board, if, you know, if you've got a venture capitalist or an angel who's funding you and is on your board, you have to tell them what you expect from them. And the good ones actually want you that. And the good ones want you to do that. They really will, do want you to do that. And so I think of your board as an extra asset and how do I fill it with people who aren't just names but people who have a specific role. Why am I on the Sonos board? Why did John McFarlane ask me to be on the Sonos board? Because a whole bunch of the things that we went through on Xbox, Sonos goes through. They're a software company. They're a hardware company. They're a retail company. They're trying to figure out how to integrate all those things. They're in the entertainment space. So in the sales marketing operations space, when I joined, they had just hired a CMO. They had no head of sales and they had no head of operations. And so my superpower for them was helping them navigate through those waters. And it turns out the problem doesn't go away. Even when you hire the people, there's still more things. And so the time, I spend most of my time with the head of sales and marketing at Sonos. We talk once a week. And that's what you want from a board member. And you want to be able to give them the flexibility to talk to your employees. So if you're the CEO and you have somebody who's good in finance on your board, you want them to have the direct line to who's ever managing finance for you and to not feel like they have to go through you because you want your finance person to get the benefit of their expertise. I think oftentimes people think of boards as governance things or they think of things where they're filling slots or names they want to put on their slide deck. If you're doing that, you're doing it for all the wrong reasons. You want people there who are going to add value to your company and you should demand that from them. It's an interview just like you interview anywhere else. It is. It totally is. And sometimes people think, well, they're interviewing me about whether they want to invest. In fact, you have to decide whether you want them to invest because of what they can add to your company. And to me, that's the difference between a value-added VC and somebody who's just trying to make a buck on your idea. And that's an important distinction. Well, Robby, thank you very much. Cool. That was fun. Enjoyed it. Good to see you. Thank you.

interviewee_bio: Spencer co-founded Zillow in 2005, and served in various roles until his appointment to CEO in 2010.
video_title: Startup Grind Seattle Hosts Spencer Rascoff, CEO (Zillow)
transcript_text: Here we go. So again, for people that have not been to start Brian, this is really just a fire-sized chat, right? So it is really about just two people talking about asking questions about what he's done in the past, etc., etc. So feel free to hold questions until the end, which is around 8 o'clock-ish, and then we'll open the open for Q&A for Spencer to ask questions. Again, like I said, feel free to get up, get some drinks, food wherever you want, if you're not going to bother us, and some faculty can drink some. So do you believe that I'm crazy or am I on the right track? I definitely don't believe that you're crazy. I think that is one dimension of the optimistic view. And the way I like to put it is, think of AI as standing for augmented intelligence. And then think about all the AI system that you're already using. For example, the Google or Bing search engine, right? A lot of AI systems built into that. How many people have used the translation software, Google Translate or some other thing, right? That's AI par excellence. So it's constantly the case that AI is empowering us. When I was talking about autonomous vehicles, it's important to understand these vehicles are going to make us a lot safer, but they're not going to decide where to go. It's not like you get into this car and say, hey, I want to go home. And it's like, nah, let's go to a bar and have a few drinks. That's not how it works, right? So AI is very much a tool, and it's a potentially very empowering tool, potentially empowering of our creativity, both by reducing the drudgery that we have to engage in and by giving us more successful tools. So a project I haven't mentioned yet is Semantic Scholar. It's an AI-based search engine for scientists, medical researchers, and ultimately everyday folks who want to be educated about science. It uses AI to cut through the clutter and help you home in on the information you're looking for. And it's a very popular project. We have millions of users each month. Check it out at semanticscholar.org. And it's meant to do exactly what you're talking about, make the literature search more efficient so that you as a scientist or a researcher can do a much better job on the more creative stuff that machines still cannot do. Two more questions, guys. Do you mind if I take this one? Well, speaking of GDPR, so one thing it does well, I thought, is that it gives users access to all the data that an organization stores about them, so you can download all of it, and also the ability to delete it. Now, if you think of a model that was built based on user data, based on some behavior, that's a derivative of this user data. What rights do you think somebody should have to that derivative? Should they be able to say things like, well, I want you to unlearn all behavior based on me? Maybe impossible, actually. Or should they be compensated in some way? I'm asking because I read an article about this, and I thought it's very murky territory. I do think it's very murky, and it's outside my area of expertise, but you're making a good point that GDPR isn't just about those annoying clicks. At the same time, it's very tricky. The New York Times had an article called The Tyranny of Convenience. And basically, we collectively have shown that we're willing to trade away an enormous amount of information about ourselves for a bit of convenience. I, for example, tried to delete a bunch of information out of Google Maps, but it's cleverly designed so that I also can't get a lot of the features. And finally, I just gave up. I'm like, whatever. Track my movements. I just want to be able to go back to that wonderful Thai place in Portland next time I'm there. Last question, guys. Who's it going to be? Go ahead. Oh, you know what, Warren? You've got those t-shirts. Oh, my gosh. The most important part of the day. So, if you've asked a question. Actually, let's not take our time. If you've asked a question, please come up, and I will give you a wonderful, free, and very soft and fluffy AI2 t-shirt. I only got one size, so it might not fit. But then you can give it to somebody else. You could sell it on eBay. In an ever-polarizing world, from your professional opinion, do you believe AI in the future will open our minds or close our minds? Wow. Again, I think your question and the answer to it really illustrates the key thing about technology. It's one step forward, one step back. It has trade-offs built into it. So, it will definitely open our minds, right? Semantic Scholar is an example of that that we've been working on for years. It's going to make it easier for you to find information. And even if you're not a technologist, you know, when I have a medical condition, that's my first stop. Because it's got all kinds of medical information. It really makes it easier to find. So, it opens my mind. At the same time, we know that, you know, the adaptive algorithms in Facebook and YouTube and others have led people down garden path that are very much mind-clothing. We have all kinds of echo chamber effects that are amplified by technology. So, the answer is very clearly yes and no. Thank you. Thanks for taking the time from the family. You know, thanks for being here, as always. You're wonderful. So, I'll see you in another four years or so. And next month, we're right here in the same spot. Last Tuesday of the month, we have an AR/VR panel discussion. So, a really interesting group. Three CEOs from AR/VR companies here locally. And until next time, be safe. You can hang out here for a few minutes and have a great month and have a great day. Take care. Bye-bye. Thank you.

interviewee_bio: Len Jordan is the Managing Director of Madrona Venture Group.
video_title: Len Jordan
transcript_text: So without further ado, I would like to welcome our guest Lynn Jordan. Come on in, come on Lynn. I got to see Bono in Paris in September and it was not that good. He did not get that much applause. No, no. Very nice of you guys. He wasn't there to see me, by the way. He wasn't there to talk. It was Lynn Jordan. Well, thanks Lynn for coming. Appreciate it. Great to be here. Yeah, take a little bit of time from your family and the tennis match you might be playing, right? Poorly. Poorly. Well, again, for people who have not been here before, this is truly just a conversation between two human beings, obviously talking a lot about startups. If you have a question, try to save it to the end, but if it's a burning question you need to ask it, feel free to interrupt. It's not going to bother us one bit. So, you know, the last 15-20 minutes we'll go for Q&A, but like I said, if you have a burning question, just blurt it out. We'll happily answer it. And, you know, feel free to get up, get your water, drinks, whatever. We don't want anything left when we leave here, right? Drink up and have a good time. So, again, thanks for being here. You know, we've known each other for a few years, but, you know, tell the story of why you're a VC. Like, you've been, what, 18 years now, 19 years? Yeah, I started in, depending on how you count, 2003, 16 years, yeah. So, tell us why you went from, I guess you went from Real Networks to Frazier. And then, so what was the transition to be a VC? And, like, why are you, why do you get up in the morning and say, "Ah, I'd love to be a VC"? Well, my back story is, I was supposed to be an attorney. Are there any attorneys in the room? Okay, no attorney jokes today. Well, Buzz is an attorney, there's lots of attorney jokes. You know, like a lot of people, I sort of stumbled into tech. When I was 21, I'd gotten done with college and was sure I was going to be an attorney. Had applied to law school, was accepted. But a buddy of mine was working at a software company. This was a long, long, long time ago. And I walked into the software company and decided, "You know what, I think I'll put off law school for a year." And I put it off every year for 32 years. And I just loved the software business. It felt like magic. You know, when I got involved in the software world, it was 1987, which I know seems like a long time ago because it is. And software was like magic. It just felt amazing to me that people could invent new products, and deliver them nine months, 12 months, 18 months later. And do things that seemed not possible before that. So I spent 14 years in the software world. I've never had the courage to do what any of you have done, to start a company. But I did restart my second company. Funded it on my own credit cards. Almost crashed it into the ground. Barely raised an angel round. Ended up raising a venture round. Ended up growing the company and selling it. So before Real Networks, I had two companies before that. Both that were acquired. And then joined Real Networks a long time ago. Helped take that public. Ran that, have the company. Was at Frazier. Was involved in some companies like DocuSign, Series A, and Control 4. And some other companies you may know about. And then I've been here for about 10 years. And I guess to answer the question, I just love startups. You know, we focus at Madrona on early stage companies. I love getting involved as early as possible in helping companies think about their product, think about their market, think about how they're going to build their team. And it's fun because when you're in venture, you get to do it with a bunch of different companies, not just one. Yeah, exactly. It's not like you're just working with one little bucket every day. And in your process of being a funder of companies, what is the thing that you enjoy? I mean, because you're evaluating teams probably on a somewhat constant basis. What's the things you enjoy, like Lin really enjoys about his daily work? Yeah, I think, I mean, there's two parts to what we do. There's finding the next big idea and funding it. And then there's trying to help those companies after we fund it to be successful. I would say both are sort of equally fine. The first one is super ambiguous, right? When you're an early stage investor, you're trying to decide, is this going to be a big market? Is it going to be a really breakthrough product or service? And is it a team that we think can build that and deliver it and market it and sell it? And we rely on a lot of really imperfect information, right? Because you're not getting to evaluate financials and business models so much as you're trying to figure out those things I talked about. Market, product, and team. So it's fun but ambiguous. I think the fun part about helping companies after we've invested is, you know, we try and help with three main things. Talent, you know, helping you recruit people. Just advice, company building advice. You know, we've seen a lot. We've made plenty of mistakes. We're going to make more. But our ability to help maybe navigate or help you navigate strategic challenges or team challenges or company building challenges is the second thing. And then, you know, figuring out how to get to the promised land. We've had four IPOs, I think, at Madrona, meaning four companies we've invested in have gone public in the last two or three years. The average length of time from the time we invested until they went public was 13 years. The shortest was 11 and the longest was 17. So this is a long-term business. None of our successes were overnight successes. None of them happened quickly. And all of them had big rocky roads until they got to the promised land. So people don't always know that. They think it just ended well. But trust me, plenty of them don't end well. And even the ones that do end well, you know, had moments of truth along the way that were hard. Yeah. It's not, no matter how smart or how good a team is, there's still speed bumps along the way. Where is the, like, for Lynn, again, for you personally, what is the piece that is really the stuff that you're good at? Like, what's Lynn really good at? Like, what do we, like, you have a skill. There's something really good. I just don't know. I'm still looking for that thing. What is that? Yeah, no, that's a good question, right? I mean, look, we all try and get better every day. I, because I have a lot of operating background, you know, I was at a company that went from 10 million to 90 million in revenue. I was at one that went from zero to 20. I was at one that went from, you know, 15 to 240 million in revenue. I've been on the boards of lots of companies. I've been involved in some boards of companies that didn't do well, where we didn't, you know, the company didn't grow quickly. I've been at, every one of my companies could have done better. Of that I can assure you, right? They all had steps along the way where they could have done things differently. So I really get energy from working with entrepreneurs and trying to help them think about what the next chess move should be, what the move after that might be. And what I've learned about myself is I'm not sure I'm very creative, but I think I'm good at helping people choose ideas. In other words, if someone's at a crossroads and they're thinking about should I do this or that, I think at a product or a market or a team level, I'm pretty good at helping them lay out what the choices might be, what the pros and cons of the choices might be, and then how to make a decision. But I've learned to not assume that I am great at actually coming up with brand new product and market ideas. I think there are people that are awesome at doing that. Probably many of you are. And I think it's good to know your limitations. And I think sometimes, and I have to be critical of my friends that are venture capitalists, but I know some venture capitalists that aren't as creative as they think they are. And being self-aware is good, right? Knowing what you're not good at is good. It's just a good life skill. And knowing what you are good at is good too. Yeah, exactly. Like knowing you're bad at one thing is, you know, that's good to know. Like if you have a bad backhand, you should go there, right? Right. I have a good backhand and a bad forehand, so I go over to this side of the court and I always come to the rear. And so in your process, you know, of evaluating, right, like I come to you with a business and have an interesting team, et cetera, et cetera. What are the things that like, again, you look at, I mean, Madrona looks at things as a group and there's several people. It's not like you're making the only decision whether it's funded or not funded. But for you, what is the kind of things that you care about and what you focus on and what, you know, what are those things? Like what are one or two things you like? These things are just critical. Like there is no, I'm not debating this one. This has to be X. Yeah, it's a great question. I'll point out, I'll use a company as an example. So we seed funded a company here in town called Wrench. Has anybody heard of Wrench? Oh, sure. Yeah. So Wrench is on-prem car repair. And the idea behind Wrench is that taking your car to a repair shop for a lot of people isn't a very fun experience. And I'm not condemning all repair shops. I'm just saying it's a hassle to go there. You have to get transportation. Then they look at your car. Then you have to come back. And then they have to order parts. And then you might have to come back again. And it turns out that a lot of things that you can do to your car when it needs repair can be done when it's parked in front of your office or in front of your school or in your driveway or your house. And so the founder, Ed, who really knew nothing about cars except how to drive one, had an experience where he realized, gee, this just doesn't work very well. What if I could hire mechanics that would come to you and fix your car where it is? Yeah. They can't fix everything. But you'd be surprised. Brake jobs, alternators, lube oil filter, windshield. Probably 90% of the things that you typically have done to your car, aside from getting in an accident or something, they can do in front of your house. So what we liked about the idea was that it really is a big market idea. The car repair business is a $47 billion a year business in the U.S. So we had to believe that if you delivered that service differently and better that people would buy it. But if you believe that, it's plenty big, right? And you'll hear a lot of venture firms say, well, we're looking for a big market idea or a big opportunity or a big TAM. And what we've learned is that some ideas just aren't that big. And it doesn't mean they're not a great business, but they may not be a venture scale business. And what we mean by venture scale, and I think this is worth explaining because people often wonder, well, you know, why isn't my idea big enough to be venture scale? Well, it might be useful to take one minute and explain how we make money, what we're supposed to do to be successful as a venture firm. So last year we raised a $300 million fund. And our goal is to see if we can triple that fund. So turn $300 million into $900 million. And let's say that when we invest in a company, we own 20% of that company. And let's say by the time we exit, either take it public or sell it, we own 10%. I'm using 10% because that's easy math for me to do. Well, if we're going to take $300 million and turn it into $900 million, and we own 10% of companies, it means the collective value of all the companies has to be $9 billion. Right? That's what 10% of $9 billion is. So now we're going to have plenty of companies that wipe out. A classic venture model says that for every three companies we invest in, one will lose everything, one will kind of get our money back, and one will get five times our money back. But the reason venture firms are focused on really big ideas is we need to have some big ideas that really, really work in order to drive those kinds of returns. And the reason we're trying to drive those returns is if it takes us 15 years to return three times our money, if you do the math backwards and you look at things like internal rate of return, that'll generate a rate of return for our investors, the LPs that invest in our funds, of about 20% to 22%. And that's a great return. You might ask, well, why doesn't everybody put all their money in venture? Because it's super risky, right? If you hit the wrong vintage or the wrong fund, you could lose a lot more than 20%. And Lynn, the life of the fund is, make sure you're clear on that. Usually the life of the fund nominally is about 12 years, but most funds in the last 10 years have had to get extensions, so they last longer than that. We're still active in our third fund, which is a fund that we closed in 2004, so that fund is 15 years old and we're still not done. We still have some very high quality companies in that fund. The reason I give you all that background is our investors, endowments, foundations, etc., they are patient. They're willing to wait 15 years for us to generate returns, but they want big returns. They're also willing to tolerate a lot of risk, but you can do the math. If we fund your company and your company sells for $50 million and we put $5 million into it and we own 20% of it, that's not a bad outcome. We put in five, we get back 10, but relative to the goal of returning $900 million, it doesn't help us that much. And I'm not saying that's a bad outcome, I'm just saying it's not what we aspire to. So our hope, to answer your question, is that when we fund a company, the company has the potential for that level of return. It doesn't mean they're going to get there. In fact, in most cases they're not. But if we look at it, I looked at an idea today and I just, in my heart of hearts, I just don't think the idea is ever going to be that big. And it doesn't mean that isn't a good company with a good idea that deserves a different type of financing, but I don't know that it's a good venture backlog. Does that make sense? Yeah, yeah, I think it's important that everyone understands that, what is it, 2% of companies are backed by VCs. Like 2%, not the other way around, it's 2%. And plenty of good ones are not backed by venture capitalists. So not every company is a good match for VC money, because VC money brings different things. You've got to deal with a guy like this. And how fun could that be? And how fun could that be? And there's different expectations for venture-funded versus different types of funding or, of course, bootstrapping. So it's just important to understand that not everybody's a perfect fit for VC. And I think that's the key here. And understanding why, which is what you'd explain, I think is the critical piece. Yeah, so big market, and I took a long time to explain that, but I thought it might be helpful for people to just kind of know how a venture firm works. You know, mathematically. Big breakthrough product, and a team that we think can execute. And the one thing I will say about team is, if you look at the most successful teams we've backed, almost never has the founder been an executive at another successful tech company. More often than not, if it's a tech company, and most of our investments are in tech-oriented companies, it's someone that was a pretty, I hate to use the word low-level, but it usually was a fairly low-level person inside a company with a really big idea. I mean, we backed Sujal Patel long before I was involved in Madrona, although Sujal worked in my group at Real Networks. So Sujal started a company here in town that people might have heard of called Isilon in the storage space. And they really reinvented computer storage. And Sujal was, I don't know, three levels below me at Real Networks, four levels below me. I was running half the company, and he worked for, you know, a guy who worked for a guy who worked for a guy who worked for me. But you know what? People knew Sujal was brilliant. And I was not in any way surprised when I learned that he had left to go start a company. Did he look on paper like an executive that knew how to run a company? No. But he was super smart, very technical, had really good domain expertise, had all the right instincts to be a great CEO in terms of leadership and management and building teams and things like that. Yeah. But was totally, truly unproven at it. But Madrona took the bet on Sujal and it worked out. And that company, you know, was a huge win for us in our second fund. And my point is, Sujal's profile is more typical than, you know, the former executive at a big company starts a company. Frankly, that almost never happens. Those folks have made too much money and are too successful to want to go start a company. Well, they're not hungry, right? Well, I mean, I'm sure there are some, but anyway, I think it's important for people to know that we're not looking for the former executive that's starting a company. If you are a former executive and you want to go start a company, we'd be thrilled to talk to you. But I'm just saying that's not usually the profile of the person. Yeah. Like Aaron from Visible, right? Yeah. He was just a guy at Microsoft. He was just a guy in the ad group. Yeah. Aaron's another good example of a classic profile. Yeah. A super smart technical person with good instincts that recognized a big problem and said, "Hey, I think I can fix that and make that better." Yeah, exactly. And again, in your day job, where are the things that you like to see in a founder? And I'm talking about like early stages. I'm not talking like, "Hey, Lynn, nice to meet you. Can you write me a check?" Not that guy, but I'm talking about like, what are you looking for in the individual? Again, like the Aaron's of the world. You don't know they're going to be good. You meet them, but you're like in your head like, "That guy's got something." Like, what is that thing to you? Like, why? Like, and how do you cultivate that relationship? I mean, you have... Here's what I think what people understand is you have to cultivate relationships, just like they have to cultivate relationships with you. Because you want to talk to the best people. And so you have to evaluate, "Is Mike any good? Do I want to keep a relationship with him?" I mean, you do do that. It's not like, it's not maybe that black and white, but how do you do that in your job? I mean, because it's part of you have to do is evaluate talent. Yeah, it's a great question. We probably meet with eight to 900 companies a year and we fund about 10. And so we meet with a lot of entrepreneurs. And I think the basic attributes that we look for are kind of what you'd expect. I'm thinking of one that I met yesterday where they had a really great understanding of a big market idea, a big opportunity to disrupt a market with a problem that they articulated really well. They had a really clear idea of what their product or solution might be. Their understanding of the market, competition, other solutions, other approaches was incredible. The number of customers they've had really hard conversations with around this idea to really smoke test it and see if that made sense. They've done a lot of that work. They didn't just sort of, you know, jump at the first idea. They really were curious. That's probably the top attribute I would look for in an entrepreneur is curiosity. They were really curious about finding out how many things could go wrong with my idea. And the way they were exploring that was by talking to lots of prospect customers. And the third thing I, so just sort of, you know, being smart, being curious. Those two things aren't always the same thing. I know plenty of people who are smart and not curious. They're sure they've already figured stuff out when in fact they haven't. And that's dangerous. Super dangerous when someone that's really smart because they're able to persuade other people that are less smart that they have already figured things out. And that's the worst combination. And then the third attribute is, I would just say humility and transparency. I backed a company, Control 4, which in the home control and automation space in 2005, or 4. And they went public after a long journey. And I remember in the first meeting I had with the entrepreneur, he had all of these different elements of the business that I really didn't like. They were getting into hardware and they had a weird channel strategy and too many products and poor gross margin. I remember seven things I didn't like about the business. But Will, and he was actually aptly named by his parents because Will was the most willful person I've ever met. But he would, in a lot of instances, instead of sort of changing his answer to appease me when I pressed him hard on things, I remember many times him just saying, well, you know, he'd answer my question, he'd listen to it, then he'd answer it. And then he'd explain, well, this is why we're approaching it this way. Maybe there's a different approach, but I think you're just going to have to get comfortable that that's just a risk of this business. And if you don't like the hardware business, you shouldn't invest in us. And I remember thinking, well, that takes courage, but there are some things about his business model he could alter or change, and there are some things he couldn't. And instead of trying to change my mind, he was pretty comfortable saying, like, that's what we are, that's what we're selling. I'm going to do my best to explain to you why we're doing it that way. If you're not okay with it, you know, maybe we're not for you. And he wasn't cocky about it. He just was being straight with me. And I remember thinking, I can really trust this person. Because if we don't feel like we can trust the entrepreneur, not to just be honest with us, that's like the lowest level of trust, right? If I ask you a question and you don't lie to me, that's not the standard. Right? The standard is-- You are now-- Right. You know, that's a court of law standard where you don't get put in jail for perjuring. But we're looking for a different standard than that. So we're looking for how open will you be before we even ask the question about things that you haven't figured out. There was an entrepreneur that I met with last Thursday. She was awesome. And one of the things I loved about my meeting with her is when she hadn't figured things out yet, she would just say, that's just something we haven't really figured out yet. No explanation. No concocted answer in the meeting. And, you know, sometimes she'd say, well, we think we might go about it this way. But there were some good questions where I felt like, you know, she could have started to contrive something. She just said, yeah, we don't know on that. That's a good question. And she also didn't say, well, we'll figure it out by next meeting. Because some things she just said, I don't think we're going to be able to figure that out for a while. And I felt like, well, I can trust this person. She's decided the top four things she needs to figure out are these. These ones can be put off. I can trust her. But I'm never going to be worrying that, like, there's some answer that's being constructed on the side. Yeah. Yeah. So that transparency is, well, it's critical because you obviously don't know what's happening internally. Contrary to some popular belief, the VCs have no idea what's going on internally. They're not that close to the situation. So, you know, it's your job as a CEO to ask for help because that's, again, what your job is, is to help me be successful. We both have like goals in that aspect. Where is the secret sauce for VC? Like, what's the secret sauce? Why are some VCs more successful than others? What do you think the secret sauce is? And I'm not saying Madronas, I'm just saying in general. I think we have to be curious. I mean, the first thing I would say is I think we have to be really curious as well. My partner, Tom Olberg, who is one of the co-founders of Madrona, is a mentor to me and someone I have a lot of respect for. I feel like Tom is one of the most curious people I've ever met. He sees things before other people see things. And I hope when I'm of Tom's vintage that I can continue to not dismiss ideas out of hand because they seem to break through. I mean, how many people dismissed Facebook? There are a lot of venture capital firms in Silicon Valley who did not fund Facebook because they thought social networks have already been tried. They don't work. And Facebook was different. I'm not necessarily the world's biggest Facebook fan. I'm just saying it turned out to be a pretty good idea in terms of building a massive new way that people can communicate and connect with each other. But a lot of firms dismissed it because they'd sort of written off that category. The same was true of Search. No one here remembers Alta Vista or Lycos or Yahoo or others. But I do. But Google went to raise money and some people thought, well, Search is over. It's been won. Because they're already there. Yahoo's there. Yahoo's kind of done. And those people were wrong. Google is one of the five most valuable companies on the planet now. So one thing I think that separates venture for the really good folks from less good ones. And I press myself on this every day. Is to not dismiss new ideas because you think they've been tried before and they didn't work. Because the world changes all the time. The ecosystem changes. And people's appetite, consumer behaviors, technologies. I mean, a lot of people, you know, I was at a company that pioneered streaming audio and video. You know, that company isn't as successful as it certainly could have been. But we were on the right track. I mean, the notion that eventually you'd be able to stream audio and video and that cable would be disrupted. Now, were we the big winner in the space? No. But we were approximately right that something like Spotify would exist. And that something like Neptune would exist. And that something like Netflix would exist. And so I just learned that, you know, whether it's AR/VR or autonomous vehicles or, you know, robotics for drones or some of the bigger new ideas or things around blockchain. You know, there are some ideas that people immediately dismiss and I think the best VC firms don't let too much institutional knowledge shape that. I think the same is true of people. I think we have to be careful not to stereotype what a great entrepreneur looks like because it changes all the time. Right. I mean, like there is no, there is no model for what someone should look like in terms of their background or experience to start a company. There really isn't. Yeah. No, I would absolutely 100% agree. Where is the, where's the landmines, right? Where are the landmines for people? And I think let's talk about the people who are not going to be funded from VC because it's just not a right fit. It's not scalable. And there's several factors there. But what are some of those landmines that may cloud the decision process for that entrepreneur to think, I am a VC company? Like, you know what I'm trying to say? Yeah. And then, but I stayed close to him and said, hey, you know, if there's room, we'd love to jump in and co-invest in that seed round with, with the other angels that you're, because, because we'd be happy to do that. And then I also made it clear, hey, if the company does well and hits its milestones, you know, we, we'd probably be in a better position to lead the next round, which, which we ended up doing. And then we helped bring in another investor for the round after that. So we do a lot of seed investing. I, I think people should, should feel like we're totally open to looking at seed deals. And if we tell you it's too early, you should say, hey, but Len said it's never too early. And ask us what we mean by that. Yeah, absolutely. It doesn't mean we'll fund every angel and seed round we see. There are times when the truth is exactly what I just said. We see a seed round or an angel round get funded and we go, you know what? We're not quite there yet at Madrona, but, but we still like having met you because we, we carefully track all those companies. And, and we, if we do a good job, we stay back out in front of those entrepreneurs. We, I mean, we have a list of companies that we passed on where we didn't quite get there on the angel or seed round. And we say, and we review it, you know, frequently to make sure we're, we're trying to do a good job of staying back out in touch in front of those entrepreneurs. We don't do as good a job as we could and I'm sure we missed some where we could do better. But we want to stay in touch with those people. So I don't think it's time wasted. Last question. So I was just curious, where do you think the trend for CPG startups are headed? And do you feel like your company would invest in CPG startups? Or are there other we see for the Seattle area that could be interested in the trade? And more specifically, I'm curious about like the healthy food startup industry, which I think is like booming. And there's a lot of demand in that. And it's projected to have a very high growth over the next few years. So when is the opportunity for that, the potential for that? Yeah, the question is around what would our enthusiasm be for investing in a CPG, a consumer packaged goods or in a consumer product company? And specifically in the healthy food area, like which would fall under the CPG as well? Yeah. It's a good question. I think, I think our big questions would be how do you protect it? Yeah. And I didn't mean to be so negative about brands. I mean, I think brands are powerful. I just think that you can't lean on it. I think you need some level of differentiation in the early days. And that could be that your product just, you know, is better on some level, tastes better, works better, is less expensive. You know, something that's sustainable, that's drawn from something protectable. So that would probably be one of our top questions. I mean, I'll bring up one quick example. This isn't CPG, but if you've heard of a company called Indochino, that's a company that we invested in very early on. Indochino basically makes bespoke suits and shirts. So, and their innovation was really more about business model and distribution and manufacturing. So their premise was if you're a young man and you have to buy a suit for work, you could spend a thousand dollars on a really expensive suit. But you could instead spend half to a third that, have someone measure you, get to look at fabric swatches, order the suit, and then have it made overseas and shipped custom made for you at about a third to half the price. And you could order it on the internet. Now they also have stores. There's a store here that people have probably seen on, I think, fourth or fifth. Yeah, sure. Yeah. Now they have 40 of those stores. So, I mean, was that a tech company? Not really. I mean, it's a consumer product. It's a relatively expensive consumer product. But what they were disrupting is the supply chain, the distribution model, the inventory model. They don't have to carry a bunch of inventory, which in retail apparel is a killer, right? So if they can just have enough inventory and enough samples that you could go get a sense for the product, order it, have it custom made for you. That was the innovation that we were betting on. So I think we'd be very open minded about that kind of a product. We just, we kind of want to ask what's special that will let you protect it in terms of the way you build it. And the healthy foods market. I mean, you're right. That's a big, interesting trend. Thank you, Lynn. Thank you. Let's give Lynn a big round of applause for you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

video_title: Jeana Jorgensen
transcript_text: Welcome to the Star of Growing. So without any further ado, I'd like to bring up Gina Jorgensen. Come on up. Thank you. So, yeah, it's a great space. But thanks for being here. And can you tell the audience, because when you and I met, I was super enthusiastic because I was just surprised of the changes you guys have made at Microsoft and what you're trying to do for startups. So can you give them the elevator pitch of how it's changed? Because, you know, three years ago, it was a whole different ballgame. I mean, it was something that I was, it was the who cares. Yeah. So I run what's called the Microsoft for Startups program. It was formerly known as something called BizSpark. Is anybody familiar with BizSpark? Good. At least in Seattle, we should know that. And BizSpark was a great program. It was run more like a marketing program where we were giving out credits. And we're trying to win this credit war. And we quickly realized, well, that's not super effective, right? You could just keep giving out people, you know, millions of dollars in credits. And at the end of the day, they would know that the churn was pretty high. They would just move over to AWS and get credits from them, then go to GCP and then come back to us under a different name and ask for more credits. And just this very circuitous path that we were seeing. And, you know, I took over the business about a year ago. And the thing that was really interesting to me, I'd been on what we call the ISV business, the partner business at Microsoft for a very long time. And been working on what we call Azure, our cloud service, for, since its inception, since before we'd even written a line of code. I was asked to go recruit ISVs to use this cloud service. And so, so many of the people that we worked with were startups. And the thing that they asked us for very simply was, hey, I need a distribution channel. You guys got that. And I need access to customers. And you guys got that. And so we said quickly, this isn't really about, you know, just giving out credits. It's around how do we differentiate our offer in the Microsoft for Startups program. And then how do we be of service to these startups and give them what they need. But that's also in alignment with where Microsoft is going directionally. So we said, all right, let's take a look at the landscape. Where can we win? Where can we have the biggest impact? And how can we be of best service to startups? And we said, that focus really is on B2B. It's around giving them access to our distribution channel. We have 300,000 partners around the world. That resell stuff. And we're going down a path right now where we are giving them incentives to sell third-party apps and services from startups and from our ISV partners. And we have a pretty large access to an enterprise customer base. And so we said, great, let's start paying our sellers to sell third-party apps and services the same way they sell Office or Azure Plumbing today. I want them to sell a startup solution in that same exact way. And I want them to get paid to do it. And so about four years ago, we launched, we call our co-sell program. And it's all about giving people who are in that program access to enterprise customers and paying them to do so. So our sellers retire quota based on selling your solutions. And so we're really focused in the Microsoft for Startups program about not just credits. We do have a set of credits and offers we give, but we also give technical support with that. We also give go-to-market support with that. And so when you enter our program as a startup, you get designated a point of contact that helps you navigate your relationship with Microsoft. And then when you reach that co-sell eligible status, there's a set of criteria to get there. We need the innovation that comes from you guys and the innovation that comes from our entire partner ecosystem. And so it's not just about, you know, I joke with people, we're plumbers. In the Azure business, we're plumbers. We sell plumbing supplies, essentially. And we need really smart people to go put interesting apps and service that are helping companies transform the way they do business. And that's what you guys do. And so we said, what's the best way we can be a plumber, right? What's the best plumbing service we can provide? And how do we give startups access to the things they want? And so we just turned the whole program around and said it's really going to be focused on those B2B startups. Now we do work with consumer-based startups, but to be honest, you know, it's a, we tend to see them not as loyal to a platform, right? And so, and we tend to see like, hey, our real bread and butter and where we can really have an impact on a startup's business and help them grow is in the commercial space, is giving them access to customers, is giving them access to that channel. And so that's the real area of focus for us going forward. So how long did it take the internal workings to understand that, that that was the path you wanted to go down? How long did it take you to make that shift? Was it about three minutes? Really? Three minutes. Well, so I, again, having been working on the ISB business and just, if you look at our startup space and you just look at the analytics behind our business and where we were pleasing our startups the most, it was in the B2B space. Where the people who were most happy were the people who were customers. Yeah, they wanted customers. And then we said, how do we double down and do this even better? And so Microsoft is a very large organization. And then we have a large, very, very large customer base and an even larger channel. And when you think about that, how do you, as a startup, navigate all of that? And so we said, we're going to put somebody on point who knows how to do this, that sits with the startup and helps that startup navigate that relationship and navigate their go-to-market. I love the accountability in the middle of the room. That is impressive. Right there. Oh, I have examples like that all the time. Don't you worry. I just have to heckle when I can. That's it. You're good. We're good. Totally good. Okay. So let's take a couple steps back about kind of your last few years at Microsoft. I mean, you've been kind of all over the place there. As it relates to startups? Yeah. Not actually startups. Just more like how you got your path. And I've got some other questions. Oh, I will be super honest right now. I have never had a skill set for any job that I've had at the company. I've been at Microsoft 18 years and never had I had a skill set. It was always somebody saying, hey, I like the way you sweep the floor over here. Do you want to go sweep the floor over here on this thing? It was like you did something in a division where somebody said, thought you did okay work or decent work, and they wanted you to go do it again. Every job I've had has been like that. It's been a tap on the shoulder to go do something in another division. And for me, I started out only working for companies that were less than 30 people. So Microsoft was the antithesis of the company I ever thought I would work for. And if I'm being perfectly honest, every year I was like, what am I doing at this company? It's so big. And every year it exceeded my expectation on every front for what I was looking for in my employer. And that's why I stayed. And every year I reevaluate, am I still getting something new out of this company? Am I learning something new? And am I being of service to the company myself? Am I doing my best work for them, right? Because I feel pretty loyal. They, my resume was nonprofit in healthcare. I did not know the difference between an Intel chip and a potato chip. And they hired me. And I will tell you, I felt like they took a big bet on me. And so every single job I've had, you know, I said the only thing I need when I started at Microsoft on day one is I need a technical mentor. Plop. Sitting next to me was this poor PM who had to sit next to me for the first two years. And he taught me so much. And I was a product manager for a developer product for Windows CE, which is a componentized operation. What? Like, that's just crazy. So I couldn't have had a more technical PM role coming out of business school. And I will tell you, you know, we look for great utility players. And that's been my whole career is just try to look at a problem and go simplify the issue and try to make sense out of chaos. And so that's kind of what every, every job has been the same. But I get to feel like I work in a different company with every job I've taken. Exactly. Yeah. And so what is, what do you think has changed? I mean, because Microsoft has changed, at least from the outside, looking in, like all of us are, right? Because we're not inside Microsoft, outside of like three people, four people. How has that new CEO changed, all that changed? I mean, because we see some very positive things happen. And I see some super positive things happen from the startup environment, you know, from the garage and M12. And you guys, like, where, like, what's, where's that coming from? Like, what happened to make that change? Well, I will tell you a lot of times, it's just sort of the times you're in, in the industry, right? Yeah. And when I started the company, it still has all the same threads of when I started at the company 18 years ago, right? It still is a company that gives you tons of autonomy, asks you to take risks.
