It was, there was a, you know, this mentality of, hey, just go do it and ask for forgiveness later. But there is still a little, I mean, that's all still true of Microsoft. The thing of what I love now and sort of, you know, with Satya, and again, I worked in his division on the ISB business before he became CEO. And, you know, it's no secret, he's a wonderful human being, period. He's just an amazing CEO, but he's just a wonderful human being. He is in private, who he is in public, and who he is as a CEO, as he is in a room with one person. And so that just culture of listening and curiosity and showing up and being, like, showing up. And what that means is, like, when you're in a meeting like this, are you on your phone? Are you listening? Are you engaged? You know, what are you, how are you showing up in the world, whether that's at work, in your private life? And I love that he lives and brings this notion of he is so present. He is omnipresent, because that man is everywhere. I've never seen him. Every time people are like, oh, I was just meeting with your CEO. And I was like, how is that humanly possible? Every single time he's everywhere, which is amazing. But he has this notion of it doesn't matter who you are. He is intensely listening to every human being who he comes across. And I just have always really respected that about him. And that is what I think the industry is seeing our whole company start to evolve in that way. You know, and I will say one last thing, and this is something for you guys, is how many engineers do we have in the room? A good fair amount. As an engineer, you are trained to find the inefficiencies. You're trained to find the issues in everything, right? Which means when you have a problem, you just want to go solve it, for one. I've known it's right for years. And so I love that cultural mentality of twisting everything that we've learned on its head and really focusing how everybody individually shows up in their personal lives and you show up at work and you're present and you're listening and you're focused. And that is just about efficiency and that's about making people who you're talking to understand that you're listening to them. And I think that for me is what the industry has really picked up on. So, we're looking for people who have a product. Have some money in the bank. Yeah, well, we're looking for people who actually have a product that they can sell into the enterprise or sell into a customer. If you're just in beta and you're looking for somebody to test it, you know, market viability, that's not the area that we focus so much. We have a bunch of community efforts. We have a bunch of community efforts and technical events where you can come at large. But we don't technically and typically spend one-to-one time helping somebody productize something. And so that's where I think we'll spend more and more time on customization of startup offers and really trying to help discoverability. This is going to be a huge issue for all programs and all service providers going forward is discoverability of these great solutions. I will tell you, which is really exciting to see, three years ago, I was working with a bunch of startups. And there was great co-selling. I couldn't get that with startups because every enterprise was like, are they going to be around in a couple months? I don't know. They were so nervous. If somebody's got a great application or service, bring it in to me. The disruptors are very, very exciting to the enterprise right now. Yeah, people, all those guys are willing to show me, show me something new that's, that's, that's disrupting something. Yeah. Yes, well, I, I, like, there's always going to be opportunity in AI, but I will tell you about that. One, one thing to know is, so everybody's an AI company that we talk to, and everybody's an IoT company. I, I am, I'm like, I, I, that doesn't even sound like AI or IoT. You've got, you're using Excel for reporting, that's not AI. And so, so, so, but, you know, I love, I love that people are using our technologies, but, so I would say within these areas of innovation, because there's tons of work in the, in the, in the vertical space, in the industry space right now, we have pivoted all of our sales force to an industry momentum, because there's more and more excitement from verticals, rather than horizontal workloads, like big data, or security, or whatnot. There are very, very distinct requests for industry-based solutions. So, I would say we would pick an industry. Yep. And build something great that has AI, true AI capabilities, using Microsoft Cognitive Services. And, and then our, and some of our core tech. I think that's, if I think about where Microsoft's really wanting to showcase its partners, hey, you've got to be using our stuff. So, kind of, core basic fundamentals, we want you using our stuff. And the second piece is using sort of the more advanced services, our bot services, our cognitive services, our machine learning capabilities, and I would go do it in a vertical. Now, that's not to say if anybody in this room has a horizontal solution that you shouldn't keep doing it, because there's tons of needs, but you have to verticalize your value prop. You cannot go into this pool of, I'm a security provider. Well, you can't boil the ocean. No, no one in our channel, and no one in our sales force even knows how to bucket you, because that's not the conversations they're having. They're having verticalized conversations, and that's more, I would say, more prevalent now than ever. And, and so, it's not to say that you can't have a great horizontal workload-based solution, but you've got to have a vertical pitch. Yeah. That's what I say. Well, it's, plus, as a startup, it's probably not the best advice of trying to go horizontal. It's just, just because it's a lot of stuff. Yeah. And a vertical approach is so much easier. Well, not, it's never easy, but it's more focused. It's a better word. Yeah. Yeah. I always tell people, it's the only time it feels good when you, are actually put in the box. Like, you want, you want sellers in our channel to say, I know exactly what to do with you, rather than, oh, I'm going to put you over here in this amorphous box with the last 300 people I've talked to. And so, the more verticalized and specific, even if you can serve the world, just start nailing an industry by, like, nail a niche. Like, that's in, in, in- And you can boil the ocean, but you have to pitch the niche, right? Yeah. You have to, you have to pitch that. Yeah. Because then people can say, oh, that's a tricycle, not a bicycle. Right. And, and I will tell you also, like, it's not just a startup thing. It's an every partner thing. Oh, you can, you're in 140 countries. Oh, you have 300,000 resellers. I want access to them all. And I was like, oh, boy, we are not going to have a good time. Because it is, it's so hard to serve everybody, right? And we fail repeatedly when you try to be everything to everybody. And I know that's so obvious, but I can't tell you how often people see and hear Microsoft and think, that's my sales, that's my sales arm, that's my marketing arm now. And they don't invest themselves and have skin in the game and get focused. And so my team, I love them to death. They are so trained to know that we pick very, very opportunistic partnerships where we can say, this is the vertical and here's the pitch. And we actually run it by our sellers in advance of putting you in front of a bunch of enterprise customers to say, does the solution hunt? Is there an opportunity here? And if they say, yeah, I get what they're doing. Great. And so, you know, if we see some folks who aren't maybe cream of the crop on that cap table in that next round, they're like, oh, maybe we don't want to participate there. Yeah. Sure. Hi, my name is Priyanka and I have a startup that makes educational apps for children. I'm curious. You said like among your portfolio of companies, do you have companies in the edtech space? And you talked a lot about the support in terms of the learning experiences from the different Microsoft groups. And do you see any collaboration with Microsoft in the view or the Microsoft big-round-line group with edtech companies at the point? So basically just to make sure we're, and so they can hear the question. So you're saying, are they doing anything in edtech? Yes. Yeah. And at what level if you are? We have one edtech company in our portfolio. They are a Norwegian-based company called Kahoot. Really phenomenal company. I've actually just joined as a board observer there about a month ago. So I was out in Oslo for dinner a couple of weeks ago and got to meet the whole cast of characters there. And they're amazing. They're awesome. You know, I, this was one of our earliest deals. So it was when my boss was M12. It was just him. And so he did this deal. And so it's very unique to anything else that we've done. I mean, generally edtech is hard to make money in, right? It's just hard to monetize. And so this has really been a learning for me, learning about this business and how they operate. I mean, they are close to a billion users. Wow. It is just phenomenal. Like if you guys don't know Kahoot, you should go check it out. Basically what they do, they're now starting to sell into the enterprise for corporate training. But to date their focus has been on teachers and students. And so they call it like you can create a Kahoot, which is a game, right? So any sort of learning that you want to do, you can turn it into a game. But yeah, they are almost up to a billion users. It is just phenomenal. And so it's a fascinating space to be in. But, you know, a really hard one to make money in. And that's why we haven't spent a lot of time in that area. And I think you had asked a question just in sort of collaborating with other teams. Microsoft, the Microsoft education team, it's led by a gentleman named Aaron Magado. And he's been very, very involved in Kahoot. It's something that he thinks about from a commercial partnership angle for Microsoft. Again, because they have a billion users. Like that's a billion new people for us to be engaging with on a Microsoft platform's perspective. And just thinking through like how do we think about the next generation and influencing their technology choices. Sure. Paul? It seems to me, thinking about it from a point of view of an entrepreneur, that huge portion of the value that Microsoft would bring would not be cash. Cash is creating a billion source of capital. To what extent do you think of yourself as putting, I mean, literally not a thing of it, but adding to the capital of a company the value you can bring with relationships with LinkedIn or with Skype or a gazillion companies that you operate. Frankly, if I came to you with a venture, I wouldn't want to cash. I want just, you know, one relationship that would turn my business to something that's not. So to what extent do you do that and really put that forward? Yeah. And that's, you know, certainly the case for many startups and companies in general, right? Which is taking funding from Microsoft is not a priority for them. And it just doesn't make sense for the business. And that's totally fine. For those companies, we have different ways to help. So I was saying like the Microsoft startups team, Azure credits, you know, business development, commercial partnerships, et cetera. And I think that those can be very successful ways for companies to engage with Microsoft. It's not always going to be about funding. You know, the thing I will say about M12, though, and startups who did get funding from us, it is a different level of visibility within the organization. Because even if you think about the Microsoft for startups program, I mean, I don't know their numbers, but if I had to guess, I think that they're probably working with close to a couple of thousand startups, if I had to guess. You know, with M12, we're working with 65 companies. We can spend time with those 65 companies, getting them in front of our CEO or our head of education or head of dynamics or whatever, just in a way that is really hard to do when you are supporting 2,000 companies or whatever their number might be, right? So there is that sort of different level of visibility and exposure and support that you can get through taking a check from Microsoft. And again, it's not always right for every company and we would never encourage people to do it if they didn't want to. The other thing I'll say, and this is a little bit of a tangent, but I think important, I haven't mentioned it yet, which is, you know, we invest in a lot of companies and they're on AWS. Through the benefits that we can offer primarily around go-to-market and co-selling with Microsoft, in order to take advantage of those programs and those benefits, you have to be on Azure. So you have to have moved a couple of workloads to Azure, right? Now, it could be everything, your entire technology stack, or it could just be a couple of workloads, but there has to be something there. And I don't push, like not all 65 of our companies are on Azure, and I don't push them to do that. Like it has to make sense for their business. The worst thing that I can do or my team can do is push a company to be on Azure when they are not ready, and that makes their company fail. Like that would be horrific. And so it has to be absolutely the right time, meaning that they have the right resources in place to be able to support that and that they want to. That's bad PR too. Yeah, very bad PR as well. Very bad PR. I mean, it's a natural way for people to want to engage with Microsoft, certainly that go-to-market, but again, it's not right for everybody. Just like equity, you know, taking equity from Microsoft wouldn't be the right thing for everyone either. Go ahead. So, come up through the Azure Accelerator and the funding from Microsoft in the previous life, what's the main difference between 1012 and maybe the things we have? So he went through in the previous life, in the Microsoft venture days, through some funding and through the Accelerator program. Yeah, I don't know about the funding that you got, but I'm going to guess that it was less, significantly less than $10 million and significantly less probably than $2 million. I don't know that for sure, but I'm going to guess. So I think that's one big difference was just the amount of capital or equity that we can provide, the amount of funding that we can provide. I think the other thing too is, you know, I honestly, and I've been at the company for 13 years. I can't tell you any of those early startups that we invested in. I don't know who they are. I don't know what they do. I don't know what sort of support and engagement they have with Microsoft. Sure. But I don't think that'll be true five years down the road for these M12 portfolio companies. I think that they are, again, kind of going back to the last question, which is getting a different level of exposure and a different level of support within Microsoft. This is certainly not a goal of M12, but it happens, right? We are looking for exits and it turned out that Microsoft bought one of those three companies I was saying earlier. Siemens bought the other one, Nutanix bought the other one, but I think those are some big wins in that level of, you know, having Microsoft on the cap table, helping make some of those introductions and support those conversations is a big one. Yeah, absolutely. Absolutely. I see AI and MR as being the next campaign platform and I don't want to make you do too, but I also see it as being the next advertising platform. So I guess I'm sure you're investing in the tech side of AI and MR or you're investing in advertising and marketing on that side of AI. Great question. We have not. On AI, we've done a lot on both the technical side and the applied side, right? And then on the MR side, we've only actually got one company in the MR and they're a Seattle based company, which is great. Pixfana. I love Forrest. If you guys don't know him, you should definitely have him here. If you haven't, just wonderful, wonderful founder, Forrest Key. So really around creating these immersive experiences using, you know, mixed reality. We are not investing in content creation type businesses. So, you know, MR in and of itself is not super interesting to us. What was interesting on Pixfana is one that a unique technology that they have around kind of compression on 360 video. And then also the fact that their go-to-market model is around large corporations enterprises. So trying to sell into the training space there. On the actual advertising side, it's just, it's not our area of expertise. Anyone on the team has not kind of been involved in any of those businesses. And so it's not an area that we feel like we can uniquely impact in a way. So we haven't done anything there. I know Amazon's jumping in big. I know Amazon's jumping in big in a lot of different areas, in a lot of different areas. They are phenomenal. Phenomenal. Phenomenal. Phenomenal. We do consider, as you can imagine, you know, I'll call it public sector just for a very generic term. But kind of government is a huge market for Microsoft, probably one of our biggest customers. I think like Army, Navy, DOE. I think so. Yeah. Huge, huge. So yes, we would consider that. And we have a couple of companies that sell into public sector. The only thing I would say, though, is that, you know, it is harder. And not just, I would say, for M12, but for VC in general. Just because the sales cycle tends to be much longer in public sector. Right? It's not, you know, somebody, a line of business person making a decision. There's a huge RFP process and budgeting. Like all of that just takes a really long time. So there has to be something super unique that gives the investor an extra level of confidence in the company's ability to sell through that and kind of manage through those longer time cycles. Go ahead. What advice would you give to somebody who wants to work in D.C. or even more specifically in M12? Give me your resume. I would be a co-worker. Yeah. Yeah. Are you, how, yeah, how does that, I'm sure those, is that an internal thing? Like, I mean, many people, are many internal people saying, I want to be on the N20? Yes. I would think so. Yes. It's got to be pretty sexy at this point. It is very sexy. I mean, I will, I will say, I really think I have one of the best jobs in Microsoft of 130,000 people. Like, I literally get to write checks in somebody else's bank account but help make people's dreams come true. Like, that is just phenomenal to me. And I don't only do it on the investor side. I also run the portfolio development team. I run a team called BC Partnerships. I run our marketing and operations for the fund. So, I get to wear many, many hats, which to me is just amazing. You know, to get into BC, I would say, you know, start early and build your network. Like, really, really build your network. I think, in the past, it has been primarily based on experience. You kind of had to grow up in that space in order to move ahead. I think with, you know, this big focus on diversity and inclusion and just thinking about people's experiences. And you see this a lot in the Bay Area. Like, actually, I'll give you a great example here in Seattle. Terry Meyerson. Oh, sure. He used to run Windows. He is now a partner at Madrona. At Madrona, yeah. Right? So, really, really interesting. This technical engineer guy who's now, you know, going to be doing investments for Madrona. So, I think that diversity of thought and leadership and experience is starting to be taken into account as a real asset. But you still have to know people in order to get your foot in the door because it is so competitive. There are very few jobs. And so, it really is just about the network. I would say for M12, you know, we've had a mix. A lot of my team here in Seattle has actually come from Microsoft because a big part of the portfolio development piece is having the network at Microsoft and knowing who you can leverage when you've got a question about, you know, dynamics or HD insights or whatever it might be. So, those have primarily been internal hires. But now that we have a good-sized team there, I think the next few hires will definitely be looking outside the box and for a different set of experiences to bring to the table. And so, I think external hires could be a great one for that. And then, of course, our VC partnerships team. That has mostly been external hires, people who are very well connected in the ecosystems that they belong to. And I will say, I'll give a plug for Microsoft for startups. They are a much larger organization. We're about 25 people. I think they're probably closer to 100 or maybe 150, something like that. And they're all over the place too. They're actually hiring. Right now they're hiring for a managing director in Toronto. Somebody who's got great kind of AI knowledge and technical experience. And they're actually hiring for a managing director in San Francisco, which I think would be a super fun role too. That's Annie Parker's team. That is Annie Parker's team. Yeah. She's great. So, similar to the last question, I'm actually an engineer at Microsoft. Oh, awesome. But the first time here in 2012 was last week. And -- Uh-oh, what happened last week? No, I -- Oh, okay. What do we do? I just got interested in startups and found this meetup. And I feel that maybe engineers definitely in San Francisco and other tech companies are -- have a better finger on the pulse of the startup world in their domains. And I feel like my colleagues, myself, you don't really know what's going on in the price base in startups. I'm wondering -- Yeah, I will say our feedback loop has primarily been at the most senior level. So, kind of saw it here -- our CEO and his direct reports and then maybe even the next level down. Just because they are the ones who have been able to make decisions about changes in the way their orgs work, or sort of new partnerships, new investments, that sort of thing. I mean, the hard part is, like, while there's so many great things about working at a company the size of Microsoft, the hard part is, is that it just takes time to trickle down, right? Like, to permeate a new idea, a new thesis, whatever, through an organization of 130,000 people takes a lot of time. So, I am more than happy -- if you have any sort of ideas on how we can better engage, you know, at the IC level and that sort of thing, I'd love to do it. I mean, one thing that I spend a lot of time on is actually going to different teams and, like, speaking at their all hands. Just, you know, again, super fortunate that people are interested in startups and want to hear about it, and want to hear about how Microsoft is participating in that space. So, that's one way that we've been doing it, but, you know, certainly not a super scalable way. I think about a year ago, we had Mirage, my boss, on MS Web. So, MS Web is, like, our internal homepage of the company. And we had him do a video. And it was, like, a three-minute video or something just kind of skimming the surface. And we were called Microsoft Ventures at the time and that sort of thing. So, we probably need to refresh it. But any sort of ideas you have around how we can better connect and get the story out, I'd be happy to take them. Yeah. Yeah. I got one second. I got a question. Yeah. How did you earn your first dollar that wasn't from a relative when you were a kid? Oh, my gosh. So, what did you do? That can't be from a relative. You had to really do something. It can't be somebody giving you a dollar. Like, what job did you have and how old were you? It wasn't a real job, per se. It was kind of a naughty job. So, I used to find textbooks that students, like, had left around and I would take them and then I would rent them out to people who needed them. Oh, my gosh. So, I was very much-- Really? Yeah. Because textbooks were expensive. I actually really remember this. This was in the fifth grade. I was going to-- So, where were you getting the textbooks? Like, are you just laying around this school? I know. It's so bad. No, no, no, no. People left them. I can't believe I'm admitting that. No, people left them. So, I went to this, I went to school in the Philippines at the time, in Manila. And, I went to IS Manila and, you know, people would leave textbooks around. And, like, if they had been there for a while, I took them and then I'd be like, oh, hey, Mike, like, you're missing your math book. Like, I'll rent this to you for, you know, 50 pesos or whatever it was. Awesome. Yeah. So, that's how I made my first-- So, your cost of goods is pretty low. Yeah. Very, very low. Very, very low. I mean, risk was high, but-- The risk was high, but the cost was low. Yeah. But, you know, thankfully, I didn't get caught. And then, I don't know why I stopped doing that. Probably because it's illegal. Maybe, I don't know. Maybe. I don't know, but I did stop doing it. And then, I don't think I earned my next dollar until probably my first job when I moved to the States. So, I moved to the States to go to college here in Seattle. I graduated high school in Thailand, and I had seen all these movies about American football players and cheerleaders. And I thought, that looks so sexy and cool. I'm like, "Dad, I want to go to the University of America." He's like, "Okay. Well, we have relatives in Seattle or Kansas. Take your pick." I'm like, "Seattle it is." So, I went to University of Washington. And I went to school, and I was 16 at the time. And I quickly realized, though, I'm like, "This is nothing like the movies." Nothing like the movies. And it didn't help that I was super geeky as well. And so, like, I worked, and I wanted to pay my own way through school and stuff. But so, I got a job, and I did data entry. And it was the best job ever. And this was, I mean, a long, long time ago. And I was making 12 bucks an hour. And I could work any schedule I wanted because it was just doing data entry for surveys, right? And there was more than enough to go around. So, I would just go in between class or in the evenings or whatever. And I made 12 bucks an hour doing that. It was amazing. And I paid my way through school. It was great. That's great. That's great. That's my first legitimate dollar. I asked most everybody, I asked that question. Like, "How did you earn your first dollar if it wasn't from a relative?" And, yeah, that's the first time I heard that they were just stealing books and giving them money. I love it. That's the way of being an entrepreneur. Last question. So, as one of the few in a city that's providing 2 million plus checks to startups, how can we as a startup crowd support you? Wow, that's a great question. Because we don't want you to leave. Yeah, we don't want you to stop writing checks. Yes, exactly. It's funny. When you write checks, you become everybody's best friend. It's how weird I am. Everybody really likes you. Yeah. Everyone likes you when you write checks. Yeah. Like, no one doesn't like Lisa. At least not in front of you. That's right. That's right. Especially not when you've got this big thing called Microsoft behind you, which is amazing. You know, I actually don't have an answer to that question. I'll be honest. I don't know that there's anything that I would say I need supporting in. You know, I guess maybe the one thing I would ask is that you all continue to give us feedback, you know, and give Microsoft feedback and give M12 feedback because we certainly, you know, take that back to the company. And our leadership team, you know, is genuinely interested in thinking about how do we take technology to the next level and what can we be doing at Microsoft to impact that. And it's very, very easy, even though none of us want to do it. It's very, very easy to build the technology that you want to build as opposed to the technology that people need, that people want. And unless we have that feedback coming in to us, it's just impossible to do. So I would just say, you know, even when you send emails to the customer service team and you don't get responses or whatever, please don't stop doing that. Feel free to ping me. You know, I will forward those mails along. I can't promise that every single one will get action, but I promise you I will send every single one along to a team because I just think that the customer's experience is tantamount to what we're trying to create. And if we don't get that feedback, we don't try to prove that, then we'll just never be better. Is there a message that you think that is being misread or isn't clear enough to the outside world for you? Is there a message that maybe that you would like us to make sure that we know? No, we actually heard it from Lisa. She said to say, you know, like, is there a message that you don't think that's being communicated correctly for 12? Or is it all pretty? Has it been clear? No, I don't think it's been, you know, 100% clear. I do think that there is still, you know, confusion or lack of awareness that M12 is doing VC work, right? Like that we actually are investing in startups. So I guess maybe to answer your question, you know, helping permeate that and reinforce that would be great. That's probably the biggest thing, I guess. I mean, M12, I think, you know, within our circles, people know what we do now and it's certainly growing. But there's still a lot of people who don't know that Microsoft genuinely cares about this phase and is writing checks and wants to help. And so that would be a big one. Mike, can I ask this question? No. Is it beneficial? No, absolutely not. Okay. Can you have an answer? This happens in real life. I told a company that I was advising that, you know, you might be very interested in your technology and they are, you know, very early in that stage. And then I encountered another one like that and they were so scared of it being, I was asking, did you talk to them? Because I know my company is my IMC partner and I know, you know, the core sharing and all the great things that they can get. And I also know we need cloud and all those initiatives through Peggy and Ember. But outside, people outside of, you know, Seattle, they are so scared. They think, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no way. You know, until later, they think that Microsoft is going to steal their idea. Now, recent initiative that, you know, joined the open source, you know, open network and all that, that's very recent, right? But previously, even several years ago, when, you know, Steve Bomber was the CEO, I knew because I was a Microsoft LCA, you know. LCA is our legal team, by the way. So I was the police, one of the police people to kind of, you know, check on the developers who were putting it in focus on the phone. So, you know, that proprietary sort of the mindset that Microsoft has. Do you have a question? How can we, how can we help this type of, you know, if you want to. That's one. And the second, what's the best way to introduce people to the prospect of talent? Yeah, to the point of stealing IP, I mean, I understand that this fear exists and I understand why. I would just say, you know, the first time we do that is probably the last check that we will write. Because it is a very, very small world and that word will get out quickly and nobody would want to take a check from M12 again. So, you know, if that happens, like, I would be coming to you guys looking for a job. I'd be here at the next startup grind looking for a job. In terms of, you know, how to get companies in front of us, I mean, I just want to be super transparent because I don't want to waste anyone's time. Not every startup is a great fit for us. And I don't have time to meet with every person that I get introduced to. Like, I just want to be honest. And even though I want to, I just can't. So if you really think that this company is a good prospect based on the fact that they're enterprise tech, they're kind of series A or B, some very unique IP, that sort of thing. You know, you can go to our website, m12.vc, and there's a form for entrepreneurs to submit. And we review those every Tuesday in that partner meeting I was telling you about. You can also, you know, send them to me. Personal referrals are always a little bit more helpful, right? And so you can certainly do that. I'm just lisa.nelson@m12.vc. But I would say, you know, just make sure you've got that lens on what, one, is a venture-backed company. That's one thing I'll say. I get a lot of introductions to companies that aren't really, like, VC-backed companies. They're small businesses or, like, sole proprietorships. And that's just not a good fit for what we do. Not saying it's not a good business, but I can't really help in that area. Yeah, absolutely. Yeah. Okay. Thank you very much for being here. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
